The project involves the deployment of a Grid-Scale Lithium-Ion Battery Energy Storage System for Hong Kong (HKBESS), with a capacity of approximately 100MW for 1-hour at Castle Peak Power Station, scheduled to go into service in 2028.Grid-scale energy storage refers to technologies connected to the power grid that can store energy and supply it back to the grid at a more advantageous time. Due to the energy-dense nature of lithium-ion batteries and their ease of installation in various locations, HKBESS project will use lithium-ion battery energy storage technology with an overall capacity of 100MW for 1-hour. The system will be housed in outdoor enclosures designed to comply with local regulatory requirements.The project delivery method focuses on "Design for Safety" principles and highlights the risk management approach adopted for the project. It outlines the key mitigation measures that form the design basis, including provisions for future scalability. This approach demonstrates CLP Power’s confidence in its ability to design, install, commission, operate and decommission HKBESS safely.The paper will highlight key challenges, including the application of regulations to ensure the effective and safe operation of large-scale battery storage systems, battery technologies, case studies with lessons learnt from significant global incidents and best possibilities of land use for today and future applications.

title: AI-Driven Optimization of Eco-Friendly Concrete Mix Design Using Industrial By-Products
authors: Ganesh Naidu Gopu
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The construction industry's heavy reliance on ordinary Portland cement is a major contributor to global CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions, accounting for approximately 8% of the total, thus necessitating the urgent adoption of sustainable practices. In response, this study presents an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven optimization framework for designing eco-friendly concrete mixes incorporating industrial by-productsspecifically fly ash and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBS). These supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) not only reduce the environmental footprint of concrete but also enhance its long-term durability and performance. Leveraging existing datasets of concrete mix compositions, two predictive machine learning modelsArtificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Random Forest (RF)were developed to forecast compressive strength, workability (slump), and material cost. Both models achieved high predictive accuracy (R2 > 0.89), with the RF model slightly outperforming ANN across all metrics due to its ensemble learning capabilities. A multi-objective optimization approach was employed to identify mix designs that simultaneously maximize strength, minimize cost, and maintain target workability, resulting in cement content reductions of up to 35% and CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emission reductions of approximately 25%. The findings underscore the value of AI techniques in optimizing concrete mix design, offering a scalable and efficient alternative to traditional trial-and-error methods. Furthermore, the integration of SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis provided interpretability into model predictions, guiding material selection and proportioning for performance-based design. This research demonstrates a viable pathway toward greener construction practices by facilitating the use of high-volume industrial waste in concrete production.

title: Interpreting Negation in GPT-2: Layer-and Head-Level Causal Analysis
authors: Abdullah Al Mofael, Lisa M. Kuhn, Ghassan Alkadi, Kuo-Pao Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Negation remains a persistent challenge for modern language models, often causing reversed meanings or factual errors. In this work, we conduct a causal analysis of how GPT-2 Small internally processes such linguistic transformations. We examine its hidden representations at both the layer and head level. Our analysis is based on a self-curated 12,OOO-pair dataset of matched affirmative and negated sentences, covering multiple linguistic templates and forms of negation. To quantify this behavior, we define a metric, the Negation Effect Score (NES), which measures the model's sensitivity in distinguishing between affirmative statements and their negations. We carried out two key interventions to probe causal structure. In activation patching, internal activations from affirmative sentences were inserted into their negated counterparts to see how meaning shifted. In ablation, specific attention heads were temporarily disabled to observe how logical polarity changed. Together, these steps revealed how negation signals move and evolve through GPT-2's layers. Our findings indicate that this capability is not widespread; instead, it is highly concentrated within a limited number of mid-layer attention heads, primarily within layers 4 to 6. Ablating these specific components directly disrupts the model's negation sensitivity: on our in-domain, ablation increased NES (indicating weaker negation sensitivity), and re-introducing cached affirmative activations (rescue) increased NES further, confirming that these heads carry affirmative signal rather than restoring baseline behavior. On xNot360, ablation slightly decreased NES and rescue restored performance above baseline. This pattern demonstrates that these causal patterns are consistent across various negation forms (‘never,’ ‘does not,’ ‘doesn't,’ ‘cannot,’ and ‘can‘t’) and remain detectable on the external xNot360 benchmark, though with smaller magnitude. These findings offer direct evidence of a localized and interpretable circuit for logical polarity, providing a mechanistic explanation for how this transformer architecture encodes meaning.

title: A Virtual Framework for the Cross-Layer Analysis of Backcom Systems in UmMTC Scenarios
authors: Roberto Alesii, Piergiuseppe Di Marco, Fortunato Santucci
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Backscatter Communication (BackCom) is emerging as a key enabler for the ultra-massive MachineType Communications (umMTC) vision of 6G networks. However, as BackCom networks scale, interference, collision, and unpredictable RF conditions become critical challenges to address. This paper proposes a Virtual framework (V-FW) that aims to integrate the behavior of multiple protocol layers for BackCom systems in umMTC scenarios. The framework captures detailed interactions across all communication links, incorporating device heterogeneity and electromagnetic field dynamics. In summary, the V-FW embeds a cross-layer approach to collision management, combining MAC-level avoidance strategies and capture effects at the physical-layer. A simulated case study demonstrates the utility of the framework in visualizing signal coverage, tag-to-tag communication feasibility, and collision resolution regions under varying signals and configurations. The results show the potential role of our V-FW approach in optimizing BackCom networks by enabling adaptive protocol design, testing proactive collision mitigation and recovery strategies, ultimately aligning with the core goals of 6G connectivity.

title: Machine Learning Approaches for Surveilling Keyboard Activity on Mobile Devices through Acoustic and Inertial Sensors
authors: Nirasha Kulasooriya, Asanka Sayakkara
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Technology and Computing (ICATC)
abstract: This study investigates keystroke detection on mobile devices using multimodal sensor data, combining acoustic signals with inertial sensor readings taken from an accelerometer and a gyroscope while the device operates in fully silent mode. The primary objective was to evaluate the feasibility of uniquely identifying keypresses when a background application has access only to microphone and motion sensor permissions, which are commonly allowed without raising user suspicion. To achieve this, an end-to-end pipeline was developed, including signal preprocessing, feature extraction, and machine learning models for classification of keypresses typed on a virtual keyboard. An integrated dataset was created, including audio recordings in .mp3 format with 30 keypress samples for each alphabet letter and the space key. To address background noise and minor distortions in the raw recordings, several preprocessing techniques such as normalization, trimming, filtering, and segmentation were applied to enhance signal quality. Initially, audio-only models achieved limited performance, with Random Forest yielding 21% accuracy. After refining preprocessing steps, hyperparameter tuning, and model optimization, the accuracy improved significantly to 94% for audio-based classification. Models trained on inertial sensor data reached up to 80% accuracy, demonstrating the potential of motion-based features in keystroke identification. These results highlight the complementary strengths of acoustic and motion data and underscore the security risks of sensor-based side-channel attacks, emphasising threats to user input confidentiality, device integrity, and privacy protection.

title: Forecasting County-Level Life Expectancy Using Explainable AI
authors: Alexander Phelps, Marjan Asadinia
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Public health outcomes vary widely across U.S. counties due to differences in socioeconomic conditions, health behaviors, environmental exposures, and access to healthcare. Monitoring these trends and forecasting future outcomes is essential for identifying disparities and guiding timely public-health interventions. This work presents a nationwide spatiotemporal forecasting framework that uses sixteen years of county-level data to estimate life-expectancy patterns across the United States. The dataset includes more than 460 indicators such as smoking prevalence, obesity rates, income inequality, education levels, and healthcare availability that collectively reflect community health conditions. To model these complex, long-term dynamics, we apply advanced machine-learning approaches, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, Multilayer Perceptrons (MLPs), and XGBoost. These models are trained to capture nonlinear relationships and temporal trends, enabling accurate prediction of county-level life expectancy for the year 2025. Because algorithmic transparency is critical for real-world public health use, we incorporate explainable AI techniques to identify the key drivers influencing model predictions. We additionally conduct fairness assessments to ensure the framework does not disproportionately disadvantage socioeconomically vulnerable populations. Results show that the proposed system delivers accurate, interpretable, and equitable forecasts that can support data-informed decision-making, resource planning, and policies aimed at improving health equity at the national scale.

title: Design and Development of the R.E.A.C.H. (Rehabilitation, Engagement, Assistance, Control, Healing) Device for Individuals with Physical Disabilities
authors: Eric Yang, Hadley Ellison, Jannatun Naher
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: This study developed and evaluated a rehabilitation device using force-sensitive gloves, grip strength sensors, and interactive video games to improve hand mobility assessment and physical therapy. The research addressed the challenges of maintaining patient engagement in physical therapy by creating a low-cost, home-based solution using Leap Motion tracking and force sensors. We designed two games; the first game being PinchtoDraw and the second game being BalloonPop to measure precision pinch dynamics and extended grip strength. Data from 39 participants (23 female, 14 male, 2 nonbinary) provided task-dependent variations: balloon tasks showed greater joint mobility changes (e.g., 10.21-unit decline in Ring Joint 1 flexion, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}=0.015$</tex>), while pinch tasks demonstrated high reproducibility. Gender differences showed different data outputs, with males having a stronger grip (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20.47-22.28$</tex> pressure units) and females having better finger pinch control (4.26 sensor units). The system successfully demonstrated high sensor accuracy (e.g., pressure sensor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}{ = }$</tex> 0.9974 from calibration), effectively measured patient progress, and proved the significant potential for creating engaging and data-driven physical therapy games.

title: Sentiment Analysis of Public Perception on YouTube Influencer Visit to Indonesia
authors: Evaristus Didik Madyatmadja, Patrice Elizabeth Lamitang, Keke Tribudi, Bryan Tanusaputra Karma, Fernando Lee, Clement Logan, Hendro Nindito
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: This research examines the public’s reaction to IShowSpeed's visit to Indonesia, during which the prominent YouTuber highlighted traditional cultural elements such as dangdut music, batik textiles, and angklung instruments. By analyzing YouTube comments using sentiment analysis methods, including Naïve Bayes and Decision Tree algorithms, the study reveals a generally positive perception among viewers, while also addressing concerns about the potential for cultural misrepresentation. The findings underscore the opportunities for social media influencers to promote local culture on a global stage and emphasize the need for thoughtful and accurate cultural presentations to foster deeper understanding and appreciation worldwide. This study contributes to the broader understanding of digital cultural exchange, while also pointing toward implications for content creators and policymakers.

title: EMG-Based Hand Motion Analyser and Real-Time Energy Tracking
authors: Arya S, Arpita Kulkarni, Suchith H J
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: This paper presents the development of an EMG-based hand motion analyzer integrated with real-time energy tracking for biomedical and fitness applications. Surface EMG electrodes are employed to capture muscle activity, and the acquired signals are amplified, filtered, and processed using an ESP32 microcontroller. Key features such as Mean Absolute Value (MAV), contraction intensity, repetition count, fatigue index, and energy expenditure are extracted and transmitted wirelessly to an IoT-enabled Blynk dashboard. Experimental evaluation was conducted under resting and active conditions. The estimated energy expenditure during resting conditions remained below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.2 \text{kcal} / \min$</tex>, while active hand movements resulted in energy levels in the range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2-2.8 \text{kcal} / \min$</tex>, demonstrating clear separation between rest and activity states. Validation using Bland–Altman analysis indicated that the majority of estimation errors lay within acceptable agreement limits, confirming the reliability of the proposed energy tracking approach for wearable applications.

title: Machine Unlearning for Reinforcement Learning Agents: Methods and Challenges
authors: Ayyadurai M, Nandha Kumar P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) agents are designed to learn optimal behavior by interacting with their environment over time. Through repeated experience, these agents refine their policies to maximize cumulative rewards. However, there are situations where an RL agent must forget specific experiences - either to correct unintended behaviors, respond to changing task requirements, or comply with user privacy regulations such as the “right to be forgotten.” While machine unlearning has been explored in supervised learning domains, its application to RL agents remains largely underdeveloped due to unique challenges such as dynamic environments, long-term credit assignment, and the temporal dependency of learned policies. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for machine unlearning in RL agents by introducing an experience-level unlearning mechanism. Our method identifies target experiences within the agent's replay buffer and systematically removes their influence through targeted policy updates. We demonstrate how this approach can effectively erase unwanted behavior while preserving overall policy stability and performance. Using standard RL environments such as CartPole and Atari, we evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method and analyze trade-offs between unlearning speed and performance retention. Our findings highlight the practical challenges of unlearning in RL and offer a promising direction for further research. This work contributes to advancing the understanding of machine unlearning in adaptive, autonomous agents and opens new possibilities for safe, privacy-preserving RL applications in real-world settings.

title: AI-Driven Acoustic Analysis for Partial Discharge Detection
authors: Nicholas Liang Yung Wong, Sivaneasan Bala Krishnan, Kai Xian Lai, Muhammad Fakharuddin Bin Mashure
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: Partial discharge (PD) is a localized electrical discharge that occurs within insulation systems of high-voltage equipment, such as transformers, switchgear, and cables. It is in the best interest of SP Group, which operates Singapore’s transmission and distribution network, to be able to detect and confirm PD occurrences with a high degree of certainty. This paper presents the development of an artificial intelligence (AI) tool to analyze acoustic emission measurements obtained from SP Group's routine condition monitoring of power system equipment, particularly from transformers and cable termination. The developed AI application is able to automatically process and analyze recorded acoustic measurements in the form of 10 kHz audio files from site measurement and provide a probable classification of PD occurrence. Testing results are presented to evaluate the accuracy of the developed AI tool to transform raw audio data into mel-spectrogram and classify it based on a CNN-based machine learning model. The model is able to pick out a distinguishable difference between PD and non-PD signals with 97.3% accuracy, successfully emulating the trained skill of experienced engineers. This capability enhances PD diagnostic and enables planning for pre-fault maintenance, saving resources and reducing the risk of unplanned downtime.

title: Secrecy Rate Analysis of RIS-Aided Massive Mimo with Active Adversaries
authors: Janith Kavindu Dassanayake, Thulashan Sakalasuriya, Gayan Amarasuriya
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: An adversarial reconfigurable intelligent surface (ARIS) poses a severe threat to the physical layer security provisions of massive multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) systems. When active eavesdroppers aided by an ARIS transmit identical pilot sequences to those of the legitimate users, the achievable secrecy rate can be degraded. This paper hence presents a secrecy rate analysis for a legitimate RIS (LRIS)aided massive MIMO systems under ARIS-aided active pilot attacks. We consider practical impairments including imperfect channel state information (CSI) and spatially correlated Rayleigh fading. Artificial noise is transmitted with the users' confidential signals to counter eavesdropping. Closed-form expressions for the achievable user rate and information leakage rate are derived for conjugate precoding designed when ARIS-aided contaminated CSI is available at the base-station. A statistical CSI-based optimization procedure is adopted for optimizing the phase-shifts at the LRIS. Through our analytical and numerical results, it is unveiled that the achievable secrecy sum rate can be boosted by the proposed optimization procedures.

title: Smart Primary Healthcare : Machine Learning-Driven Maternal and Child Nutrition Assessment
authors: Alfi Yusrotis Zakiyyah, Eka Miranda, Meyske Kumbangsila, Mediana Aryuini, Albert Verasius Dian Sano, Yulia Ery Aryuni
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Maternal and child health remain critical concerns in Indonesia, where high mortality and malnutrition are often linked to limited awareness and manual data management at community health centers. To address this issue, a machine learning–based web system was developed to support early risk detection. Two clinical datasets from Puskesmas Sawah Besar were used. The first dataset contains 261 child records with five attributes for predicting nutritional status. The dataset was imbalanced and therefore balanced using the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique, SMOTE. After balancing and model comparison, Random Forest achieved the best performance with 96 percent accuracy, 89 percent precision, 85 percent recall, and an F1-score of 90 percent. The second dataset contains 1,019 maternal records with eight clinical features for maternal-risk classification and was also balanced using SMOTE. Random Forest and XGBoost both achieved 98 percent accuracy, with XGBoost reaching 100 percent recall. These results show the potential of machine learning to strengthen maternal and child health assessment in primary healthcare.

title: Chem Crafter:AI Driven Molecular Generation and Property Filtering for Accelerated Drug Discovery
authors: Geetha M P, Priyadharshini M, Sangamithra V, Sriharini EN
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Developing a new drug is a lengthy and expensive process, often requiring over ten years of research and investment in billions of dollars before reaching the market. The current development in generative artificial intelligence have enabled the automated design of novel molecular structures with desirable drug-like properties. This work presents a system that leverages MolGAN (Molecular Generative Adversarial Network) combined with SELFIES (Self-Referencing Embedded Strings) to generate 100% chemically valid molecules. Generated molecules are further filtered based on Quantitative Estimate of Drug-likeness (QED), lipophilicity (LogP), and toxicity prediction. Visualization and evaluation are performed using RDKit, with an optional Streamlit interface and automated PDF report generation. The proposed system provides an efficient framework for accelerating early-stage drug discovery, offering researchers and students a powerful tool for molecular design.

title: 450 MHZ Spectrum For Power Utilities: Unlocking the Potentials of Wide Area Private Network
authors: Bin Ishak, Khairul Azhar, Bin Zakaria, Mohd Nasim
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: The digitalization of power utilities is driving the need for secure, reliable, and scalable communication networks to support Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI), Supervisory Control & Data Acquisition (SCADA), and grid automation. The 450 MHz spectrum has emerged as a strategic asset for utilities due to its long-range coverage, deep penetration, and mission-critical reliability. This paper explores the role of 450 MHz private networks in modernizing power grids, focusing on global case studies, deployment strategies, and technical advantages [1]. By leveraging 450 MHz LTE or 5G-ready technologies, utilities can achieve seamless communication for distributed energy resources (DERs), real-time monitoring, and enhanced operational resilience. Compared to conventional public networks, 450 MHz private networks offer dedicated bandwidth, improved cybersecurity, and better SLA compliance. However, challenges remain, including spectrum availability, ecosystem readiness, and cost considerations. This paper presents global adoption trends, including Germany, UK, Netherlands, Poland and Ireland's smart grid deployment, offering insights for Malaysian utilities [3][4]. Additionally, it examines the regulatory landscape, business models, and the potential for integrating 450 MHz with future 5G deployments. As Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) in the midst of implementing a 450 MHz private LTE network, this study provides key recommendations on network design, ecosystem collaboration, and long-term scalability. The findings highlight how TNB can align with global best practices to optimize its 450 MHz strategy and ensure a future-proof communication infrastructure for Malaysia’s power sector.

title: Secure Image Encryption Using Henon Map-Based Chaotic Diffusion and Substitution Technique
authors: Parul Saini, Shamal Kashid, Gaurav Sharma
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: The proliferation of resource-constrained devices, especially in the Internet of Things (IoT) environment, has led to a significant growth in lightweight cryptography in recent years. Data security is essential in the current digital era, considering the rapid growth of data distribution through physical channels and the Internet. In this paper, we have propose a robust color image encryption method that combines chaotic dynamics with two-stage substitution and diffusion mechanisms to ensure image security in resource-constrained environments. 2D Henon chaotic map is used to generate the two keys used in a position-dependent diffusion stage and confusion with a substitution stage. The proposed method is evaluated using Differential and Statistical analysis. The experiments confirm the effectiveness and reversibility of the method compared to related approaches. The proposed method is suitable for secure image communication in resourceconstrained environments such as IoT devices.

title: Application of Artificial Intelligence in Hydropower Operation Automation
authors: Ming Li, Bo Zhang, Lin Hu, Zeyu Ning, Leidan Chen, Ming Zhang
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: The centralized control of hydropower operations has reached a high level of automation and is now facing a sharp increase in costs associated with long-tail demand areas. Following the remarkable success of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies in general applications , exploring further leverage AI to enhance the automation of hydropower operation has emerged as a promising area . This paper analyzes the prospects and feasibility of deep integration of AI technologies with hydropower operation, investigating technical pathways to advance automation levels by coupling large language models, specialized intelligent agents, and traditional automation technologies. The analysis focuses on the transformative improvements and workflow changes that AI will bring to critical processes in hydropower operation, such as signal monitoring, sequence control, automated startup, accident handling as well as trend prediction. Finally, the introduction of automation grading standards to regulate and safely accelerate the advancement of hydropower operation automation has been addressed.

title: Modernising Grid Operations: A CIM-Based Model Approach leveraging Graph Databases and Generative AI
authors: Stephen Major
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: As the utility industry adapts to the growing use of distributed energy resources (DERs) and increasing pressures on grid modernisation, there is a growing need for advanced network modelling tools. The Common Information Model (CIM), provides a standard structured data model facilitating grid digitalisation and modernisation. This paper explores integrating the CIM framework with graph databases and generative AI technologies to develop an advanced common Grid Model Manager (GMM). The approach creates a scalable, dynamic, and error-correcting system for managing modern evolving grids. Graph databases offer superior adaptability and performance for complex utility networks, while generative AI enables greater automation of the creation, update, and error detection in network models. Combining CIM with these technologies builds a unified platform with a common power system ontology addressing grid modernisation challenges. This paper discusses practical insights with examples demonstrating tangible benefits.

title: House Price Prediction for Sri Lanka Using Machine Learning : A Comparative Study of Algorithms
authors: D. Dhinesh Melroy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Technology and Computing (ICATC)
abstract: Machine Learning ML has become increasingly prevalent across various domains revolutionizing tasks such as speech recognition product recommendations and predictive modeling Its applications extend to enhancing customer services and ensuring data driven decision making. In this study we propose the application of ML techniques for predicting housing prices in Sri Lanka aiming to reduce uncertainty in the volatile real estate market The proposed model analyzes a comprehensive dataset 15327 records 172 features 2015 to 2024 sourced from ikman lk ML algorithms including Linear Regression Decision Tree Regression Random Forest Regression and advanced ensemble methods are evaluated Random Forest Regression achieved the lowest RMSE of 41905267 indicating superior accuracy Feature importance analysis highlights key determinants of housing prices This research provides actionable insights for buyers sellers investors and policymakers facilitating informed real estate decisions Ethical considerations related to data privacy are also discussed it this correct

title: Cloud Revolution: Tracing the Origins and Rise of Cloud Computing
authors: Deepa Gurung, S M Zia Ur Rashid, Zain ul Abdeen, Suman Rath
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: The history behind the development of cloud computing is more than several decades of technological progress in the fields of virtualization, distributed systems, and high-speed networking, but its current application is much broader than the underlying technologies that made it possible. This paper reexamines the historical evolution of the field, including the initial ideas of resource sharing and utility-based computing approaches and the development of hyperscale data centers and modern globally federated cloud ecosystems. We also analyze the technological and economic forces and point to the way cloud platforms altered the organizational computing habits, decreasing the entrance-level to the data-intensive and computationheavy apps. The study also takes into account the ongoing limitations which have come with the large-scale adoption of clouds which include exposure to security due to the weaknesses in configuration, particular establishment regulations, and structural reliance on the single vendors. Lastly, we address some of the new trends that are transforming the cloud environment, including the convergence of edge and cloud infrastructure, the increased prominence of AI-optimised architectures and the initial adoption of quantum computing services. Collectively, the developments above describe an emerging but quickly changing paradigm with its future direction being determined by a strike of balancing between scalability, openness, and trust.

title: Face Recognition Using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Ayush Mahanta, Rohan Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: The face recognition systems have significant difficulties associated with leakage privacy, presentation attack, cross domain robustness, and efficiency of edge devices. Our suggested face-recognition system would integrate four challenges of optical privacy protection, multimodal liveness, cost effective classification, and secure federated learning. Inherent privacy protection <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A$</tex> diffractive optical front-end is used to scatter raw facial images so that they never exist in digital form. Several times, event-based neuromorphic vision and mmWave micro-Doppler sensing are fused to liveness check to detect micro-motions and physiological signals, which cannot be printable, reproducible and 3D mask spoofable. The recognition is done at the cost of a quantum-inspired tensornetwork classifier that entails high accuracy and low computational cost. Lastly, federated continual learning using homomorphic encryption will allow device-specific model adaptation offering privacy. The results of experiments IJB-C, VGGFace2, CelebA-HQ and several spoof datasets demonstrate a decrease in the Equal Error Rate by a factor of 41, inference energy by more than 3 times, and error in spoof detection by less than 1 %. This paper shows that privacy-by-physics, multimodal liveness, and efficient learning can be combined to provide secure, robust, and deployable edge environment face recognition.

title: Hybrid BiLSTM–BiGRU for Bitcoin Price Prediction with Artificial Bee Colony Optimization
authors: Wilbert Coandadiputra, Amalia Zahra
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Introduced in 2008, Bitcoin has become one of the largest cryptocurrencies assets until now. Its highly volatile price has challenged many researchers to forecasting its price. Recent studies shows that superiority of deep learning models to forecasting Bitcoin price. Hyperparameter also crucial in model performance. In this study, Artificial Bee Colony algorithm was proposed to do the hyperparameter tuning for proposed Bidirectional Long-Short Term Memory–Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit model. ABC is metaheuristic algorithms that inspired by foraging behavior of honeybees. There are 7 hyperparameter to be optimized, consisted of units, dropout rate, learning rate, batch size, look back, regularizer, and regularizer rate. The result shows that proposed model outperformed all the models that were used as benchmarking with Mean Absolute Error at 2185.43 and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score at 0.98. It concludes that proposed model gives good combination between ABC optimization and hybrid architecture for forecasting models.

title: Transformative Insights into Groundwater Prediction in India
authors: Sachin Patel, Dharmendra Sharma, Nilesh Maltare, Safvan Vahora, Hiren Patel, Bhavik Brahmbhatt
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The research provides details about the applications of machine learning (ML) in predicting groundwater levels. The evaluation reviews novel research developments shown in groundwater forecasting which illustrates the field's recent discoveries. This paper focuses on examining different ML algorithms which are used to predict groundwater level. Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) has established itself as a prevalent method among water quality assessment and recharge estimation schemes along with other techniques. Research shows that ANN models appear in between 30% and 35% of the investigated studies. Support Vector Machines (SVM) emerge as a second frequently used method to analyze groundwater contamination alongside prediction which comprises 15-20% of the research studies. Multiple advanced methods including Random Forests and Deep Learning (DL) models prove effective for forecasting groundwater level. The studies performed in India primarily utilize monthly data while producing R2 scores throughout 0.7 to 0.9. The analysis confirms that collecting groundwater measurements and weather data daily will strengthen prediction accuracy. The research seeks improved water resource strategies through an evaluation of ML techniques for groundwater prediction.

title: How Effective is In-Context Learning with Large Language Models for Rare Cell Identification in Single-Cell Expression Data?
authors: Huaiyuan Yao, Zhenxiao Cao, Zhongman Wang, Xiewei Ni, Jinyan Dong, Hongyi Cai, Yuqiang Han, Xiao Luo
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: The recent development of single-cell genomics requires more powerful computational tools to differentiate between different phenotypes, and rare cell identification has been one of the most important problems in this area. Recent data-driven approaches usually adopt feature selection techniques to identify important genes for anomaly detection, which require extensive training data or domain knowledge from experts. In comparison, large language models (LLMs) have achieved certain progress in scientific research with strong generalization ability, which has shown potential in this area. In this paper, we make an attempt to comprehensively evaluate the performance of in-context learning with LLMs in rare cell identification. In particular, we carefully design a chain-of-thought prompt combining token probability analysis and cross-query comparison to generate scores to identify rare cells. From the experimental results on benchmark datasets, we find that LLMs are competitive compared to existing training-based methods, which demonstrates extensive potential for rare cell identification. Our source code and data are available at https://github.com/Hyan-Yao/InContextCells/.

title: Comparison of Different Models for Toxicity Prediction using AI
authors: Rakesh Kumar Galav, Sujata Negi Thakur, Pratik Angaitkar, Apurva Gupta, Ashish Kumbhare, Shivam Kumarrao
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has truly transformed the way we predict toxicity, offering a scalable, accurate, and scientifically sound alternative to traditional experimental methods. Early studies highlighted the effectiveness of rule-based and QSAR models, but recent advancements in machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and large language models (LLMs) have significantly improved predictive accuracy for various toxicological endpoints. Today, these applications span drug discovery, nanotoxicology, environmental safety, and human organ toxicity, integrating molecular descriptors, omics, and proteomics data to enhance both interpretability and accuracy. Innovative architectures like multitask learning, TabNet, TabTransformer, and explainable AI platforms such as TIRESIA and ToxNet are paving the way for more transparent predictions of acute, chronic, and developmental toxicity. Additionally, ensemble and hybrid models that combine QSAR, deep neural networks, and spatio-temporal representations are improving generalizability across different chemical spaces. Despite these advancements, challenges remain, including data heterogeneity, interpretability, regulatory acceptance, and the ability to apply findings in real-world scenarios. Future directions are focusing on mechanistic modeling, multi-omics integration, and the development of AI-based decision-support systems to accelerate safe drug development, chemical risk assessment, and environmental monitoring. Collectively, these innovations position AI as a cornerstone technology in predictive toxicology, bridging the gap between experimental and computational approaches for safer and more sustainable innovations.

title: Finger Joint Landmark Prediction from Surface Electromyography Using an Attention Temporal Convolutional Network
authors: Khodimul Istiqlal, Mohamad Agung Prawira Negara, Khairul Anam, Muchamad Arif Hana Sasono, Robby Ardiansyah, Aviq Nurdiansyah Putra
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Accurate decoding of continuous finger kinematics from surface electromyography (sEMG) remains challenging due to signal non-stationarity and complex neuromuscular coordination. While prior studies have widely applied deep learning methods such as LSTM, CNN, and standard TCN for EMG-based movement prediction, their performance often degrades when modeling long-range temporal dependencies or when handling noise variations across sessions. To address these limitations, this study adapts the Attention Temporal Convolutional Network (ATCNet), originally designed for EEG motor imagery classification, into a regression architecture for predicting 15 finger joint landmarks extracted using MediaPipe. The proposed model leverages attention-driven temporal feature selection and dilated convolutions to enhance robustness against sEMG variability, outperforming conventional recurrent and convolutional models reported in previous literature. Experimental results demonstrate consistent performance across 5-fold cross-validation (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.705; CC = 0.862) and strong generalization on an independent test set (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.759; CC = 0.922). Per-joint analysis further revealed high correlations (0.88–0.98), particularly on distal joints, confirming the model’s capability to estimate fine motor behavior. These findings highlight the improved effectiveness of ATCNet for sEMG to kinematics regression and its potential applicability in adaptive prosthetic control and rehabilitation technologies where precise and continuous finger motion prediction is required.

title: A Miniaturized H-Lock Microstrip Antenna with Dual Ground Rings and L-Type Resonators for High-Frequency Communication Technologies
authors: R. Swaminathan, R Saravanakumar, Malik Bader Alazzam, Shamim Ahmad Khan, Jawad Radhi Rustum Al-Assal, JRozhgar Khorsheed Mahmud
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: Microstrip antennas are attractive for wireless terminals due to their low profile, low cost, and ease of integration. Their practical use in compact multi-band platforms is still limited by the inherent trade-off between footprint and performance, where conventional rectangular patches typically offer narrow impedance bandwidth, reduced radiation efficiency at higher frequencies, and unstable polarization behavior. Bandwidth extension methods such as etched slots, modified ground planes, and parasitic loading can improve matching, yet they often increase geometric sensitivity and complicate tolerance control. Maintaining circular polarization over multiple resonances is particularly challenging because the excited modes and groundreturn currents strongly influence the axial ratio response. This work presents a compact H-Lock shaped microstrip antenna that achieves multi-resonant operation while preserving circular polarization. The radiator integrates dual ground rings, a feedback loop, and L-type resonators to shape current paths and generate three targeted resonances. The antenna is fabricated on an FR4 substrate with an overall size of $45 \times 45 \times 1.6 \mathrm{~mm}^{3}$ and a patch size of $30 \times 30 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$, and it is tuned to operate at 4.1 GHz, 4.7 GHz, and 5.1 GHz. Measurements show a return loss better than -18 dB, VSWR below 1.3, peak gain up to 7.43 dB, and radiation efficiency above $81 \%$ across the three bands. The measured RHCP and LHCP patterns indicate stable polarization with low cross-polarization coupling, supporting the suitability of the proposed structure for compact multiband wireless communication technologies requiring polarization diversity.

title: Securing Federated Learning in Health IoT with Edge-Assisted Homomorphic Encryption
authors: Rahul Kavati, Sriven Srilakshmi Pulkaram, Huzaif Khan, Ali Jalooli
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: The rapid expansion of wearable Internet of Things (IoT) devices in healthcare enables continuous monitoring of physiological signals but introduces critical privacy challenges, especially when training machine learning (ML) models on sensitive, distributed data. Federated learning (FL) addresses this by allowing collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data. However, most existing FL systems either transmit model updates in plaintext exposing them to inference attacks or adopt encryption schemes that are computationally prohibitive for resource-constrained devices. In this paper, we present a modular, privacy-preserving FL framework for health IoT that integrates Cheon-Kim-Kim-Song (CKKS) based Homomorphic Encryption (HE) at trusted edge nodes (e.g., home routers). This design offloads encryption from wearable devices, enabling secure model aggregation and update distribution while maintaining low energy and latency overheads. We evaluate our system on real-world datasets, measuring model utility (accuracy, F1), encryption latency, and end-to-end delay. Results demonstrate that our scheme preserves privacy with minimal performance degradation, supporting practical and secure deployment in realtime health monitoring scenarios.

title: Automated Cotton Leaf Disease Detection: A Step Towards Smart and Sustainable Agriculture
authors: Gajanan Ankatwar, Chitra Dhawale
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Cotton is one of the most important cash crops in the world, as it is one of the major sources of textile and a source of livelihood. Leaf diseases are one of the greatest threat to its yield, the bacteria blight, leaf curl virus, and pest damage can leave it with a steep reduction in yield unless diagnosed as early as possible. Conventional methods of detection such as lab tests and manual field inspection are labour intensive subject to human error and delays. The emergence of precision agriculture makes the use of computer vision and deep learning promising ways of automating the process of plant disease detection. This study examines solutions to automated detecting of cotton leaf diseases using lightweight convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We contrast the traditional CNN models (ResNet50, VGG16, InceptionV3) with the lightweight models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, Shuffle Net) to determine their suitability in real-time applications in mobile and edge devices. The metrics that an evaluation uses are accuracy, specificity, recall, F1-score, latency, FLOPs and model size. Findings indicate that lightweight CNNs can be used on resourceconstrained settings as they can be trained to be competitive in terms of accuracy (greater than 90 percent) and computation load. The paper shows the potential of these models in the IoT and mobile applications, which can give farmers solutions that can be accessed early on to detect diseases.

title: Detecting Intent Drift in Continuous Conversation via Temporal Transition Accumulation
authors: Yue Wang, Dehang Fu, Jie Tan, Junxiao Han, Yao Wan, Lixin Cui, Lu Bai, Philip S. Yu
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: As large language models (LLMs)-driven conversational systems have advanced, users have become accustomed to engaging in standalone, continuous interactions. In such interactions, users may abruptly change their intent across turns. However, most existing intent detection methods focus on accurately extracting intents and slots from individual utterances, without considering the broader conversational dynamics. This makes them ill-equipped to handle long, evolving conversations where arbitrary intent drift can occur. To address this challenge, we define the intent drift detection task in continuous conversations. We then propose a differentiable method, termed DriftHunter, that enables neural networks to understand how user intent shifts as the conversation progresses via dynamically accumulating the temporal transition across turns. Unlike existing methods, our proposed method incrementally captures global and local transition patterns between intents and slots without relying on prior statistical results. Moreover, our model sequentially accumulates transition patterns across conversation turns. This allows it to learn temporal accumulated dynamics, enabling neural network models to better focus on the most trending user intents during continuous interaction. Experimental evaluations on real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both intent drift detection, intent identification, and slot-filling downstream tasks. Our case study analysis reveals that the learned temporal transition patterns explain the predicted intent drifts.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>The source code and dataset are available at https://github.com/FDHTJ/DriftHunter

title: On The Sensitivity of Channel Estimation for Indonesia High-Speed Railway Communications
authors: Falindio Muhammad Riyadi, Khoirul Anwar
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: This paper proposes five pilot patterns for channel estimation purposes and test the patterns throughout different speeds based on Future Railway Mobile Communication Systems (FRMCS) High Speed Railway (HSR) maximum supported speed by 3GPP and the current maximum operating speed of Jakarta-Bandung Indonesia High Speed Railway. We use 5G standard modulation, complex binary phase-shift keying (C-BPSK) and SISO-OFDM communication system. Pilot patterns are successfully tested and perform better than block-pilot based channel estimation. The results of this paper are expected to contribute to the improvement of HSR communication technology and implementation of FRMCS in Indonesia HSR.

title: G-EDGE: A Graph-Based Edge-Level Hybrid AI Framework for Real-Time Cyberattack Detection in IoT Networks
authors: Shideh Yavary Mehr, Abhishek Phadke
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has created highly interconnected networks that promise unprecedented automation, efficiency, and intelligence across domains such as healthcare, transportation, smart homes, and manufacturing. However, this intelligent ecosystem also introduces complex security challenges, as IoT devices are increasingly vulnerable to sophisticated cyberattacks. In this work, we propose G-EDGE; a novel method combining Graph-based modeling, Edge deployment, Hybrid AI, IoT cyberattack detection, and Real-time capability for real-time cyberattack detection in IoT networks. IoT devices are modeled as dynamic graphs, enabling graph analysis to capture evolving network topologies and detect stealthy or coordinated attacks. Edge deployment ensures minimal latency and preserves privacy by processing sensitive IoT data locally rather than transmitting it to centralized servers, facilitating timely threat mitigation. The study leverages the NSL-KDD [1] dataset to train and evaluate models using Decision Tree and Random Forest classifiers, augmented with feature selection, hyperparameter tuning, and ensemble techniques including boosting algorithms, to optimize performance. Evaluation metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score are employed to ensure robust detection, especially under class imbalances. Initial experimentation demonstrates promising accuracy, with Random Forest achieving 90.97% and Decision Tree achieving 86% in predicting attacks. Identified cyber threats include Structured Query Language Injection, Distributed Denial-of-Service, Spoofing Attack, System Misconfiguration Attack, and Vulnerability to Network Attacks. The proposed G-EDGE framework demonstrates that graph-based machine learning deployed at the network edge can provide real-time, accurate, and privacy-preserving IoT security. It offers actionable insights into device vulnerabilities and enables proactive threat mitigation, contributing valuable knowledge toward securing interconnected IoT ecosystems against evolving cyber threats.

title: Drug Interaction Modeling using Graph Neural Networks
authors: Krati Dubey, Alpana Suman, Praneet Saurabh, Rajesh Kumar Boghey, Animesh Kumar Sharma
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Drug-drug interactions (DDIs) present significant hurdles in clinical practice, making it essential to develop strong predictive models that can improve patient safety and streamline drug development. Recently, advancements in graph neural networks (GNNs) have shown impressive effectiveness in capturing the intricate relationships between drugs, targets, and molecular structures. This review brings together the latest research on GNN-based methods for predicting DDIs, focusing on techniques that utilize molecular graphs, knowledge graphs, and heterogeneous networks. Approaches such as dual-graph architectures, attention mechanisms, multi-scale GNNs, and contrastive learning have all contributed to better predictive accuracy and interpretability. By integrating structural, chemical, and pharmacological features, these models can predict interactions between drugs and targets. Additionally, automated GNN frameworks and self-supervised learning techniques make feature extraction and model optimization much easier. Comparative studies indicate that GNN-based methods outperform traditional machine learning and similarity-based approaches when it comes to handling large-scale and multi-relational biomedical data. The review also tackles challenges like data sparsity, scalability, and the need for explainable predictions. Future research will likely focus on merging multi-omics data, enhancing model generalization, and supporting real-time clinical decision-making, positioning GNNs as a groundbreaking tool for precision medicine and drug safety assessment..

title: A Medical Big Data–Driven LASSO–Survival Hybrid Model for In-Hospital Mortality Risk Stratification in Cardiac Patients
authors: Boyu Li, Lida Yang, Qiong Wu
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 4th International Conference on Health Big Data and Intelligent Healthcare
abstract: None

title: A “Cognitive Stress Test” for Assessing Early Alzheimer's Risk: A Study of Mental Arithmetic Metacognition
authors: Shanshan Wang, Zimo Li, Shintaro Funahashi, Zhilin Zhang, Jinglong Wu, Jiajia Yang
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 4th International Conference on Health Big Data and Intelligent Healthcare
abstract: None

title:
A Compact Millimeter-Wave Quad-Core Class-F
                    <sub>23</sub>
                    VCO Using Inter-Core-Shaping Technique
authors: Yuan Chun Li, Yingzhen Que, Ling-Yi He, Xi-Yue Zhang, Quan Xue
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: A compact millimeter-wave (mm-wave) quad-core Class-F<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">23</sub> voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) using an inter-core-shaping is proposed in this letter. Based on the complementary currents between pMOS and nMOS cores, both common mode (CM) and differential mode (DM) tanks are established with low complexity. An eight-port triple-coil Class-F transformer is designed to achieve second and third harmonic impedance peaks in the CM and DM operations, respectively, resulting in the phase noise (PN) reduction. Fabricated in a 65-nm CMOS, the VCO has a tuning range of 16.8% from 25.6 to 30.3 GHz. The measured peak PN at 1-MHz offset is −111 dBc/Hz, with a small core area of 0.096 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. It demonstrated that the proposed topology can solve the conflict between the complicated design of harmonic control and compact size in the mm-wave, thereby enhancing the PN performance and compactness simultaneously.

title: Sensor-Based Assessment of Plant–Microbe Energy Interactions
authors: Alessandro Zompanti, Miriana D'Addesa, Bruno Carneiro, Giorgio Pennazza, Etelvina Figueira, Simone Grasso, Anna Sabatini, Davide Cacciari, Luca Vollero, Gil Gonçalves, Marco Santonico
abstract: The BIONOTE-L voltametric sensor was used in this work to exploit cyclic voltammetry as an operating principle to interact with the plant/soil system. Indeed, in bacteria, the metabolic process oxidizes organic compounds from the soil organic matter with extracellular electron transfer. Plants can also release electrons into the rhizosphere—in what are known as plant microbial fuel cells (PMFCs). The electrons obtained, when appropriately conveyed, generate an electric current. On this basis, the innovation of a sensor system “interrogating” the plant/soil system via cyclic voltammetry is proposed. This work tries to place a new paradigm: Rather than exploiting the energy autonomously produced, the plant-microorganism system and the interaction that occurs between them can be analyzed in energetic terms, which is novel with respect to the standard approach measuring other parameters related to plant growth. An external input is provided, and the response of the system to this solicitation is observed. The energy extracted does not have the purpose of powering other devices or being stored. Instead, it is treated as data on the state of health of the plant-microorganism system. This test has been structured as a step-by-step experimental investigation, with a gradual increase in complexity. The BIONOTE-L was tested to analyze the behavior of four different genera of bacteria in solution: Rhizobium, Bacillus, Enterobacter, and Kosakonia. The behavior of Bacillus was then analyzed in interaction with the soil and with a corn plant (Zea mays L.). The gradual growth of the bacteria in the solution was assessed using a spectrophotometer (optical density: O.D.) and predicting O.D. with BIONOTE-L with root mean square errors in cross validation from 0.004 to 0.2 (depending on the different bacteria). Specific fingerprints were detected for each hour of bacterial growth (therefore specific for the concentration of bacteria in solution) and for each different genus of bacteria. Finally, the electrical response of Bacillus in interaction with the soil and plant was characterized, thus providing a specific electrical signature to be monitored in a real-world scenario.

title: Differentially private consensus of multi-agent systems under binary-valued communications
authors: Xiaodong Lu, Yanlong Zhao, Ji-Feng Zhang
abstract: This paper investigates the differentially private output consensus problem of continuous-time heterogeneous multi-agent systems. An algorithm blending estimation, control and privacy is proposed. To prevent privacy leakage, each agent constructs an auxiliary variable regarding to its own initial output, and then adds a non-decaying noise to this variable. To incorporate the network bandwidth limitation, each agent quantifies the noisy auxiliary variable to 1-bit binary-valued data, and transmits this data to its neighbours at some impulse time instants. Based on the received information, each agent builds the recursive projection algorithm to estimate its neighbours' auxiliary variables, and updates its own auxiliary variable. Then, each agent designs its controller by using its own state and auxiliary variable to achieve the mean-square output consensus. To characterize the degree of privacy protection, the differential privacy index of the proposed mechanism is derived. Compared with the existing works, this paper only needs 1-bit communication bandwidth, and the control signals are not required to constantly feed back to the original system. From the aspect of quantization and control, the communication resources can be conserved. An example is presented to illustrate the effectiveness of the theoretical results.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems

title: Multisensor Fusion Enhanced Evidential Space Transformation Model (ESTM) for Structural Defects Inspection of Porcelain Bushings in Power Systems
authors: Ren C. Luo, Hsu-Chia Kao
abstract: The objective of this article is to present a decision fusion method for structural defect inspection of porcelain bushings in high-voltage power systems. Conventional approaches employ heterogeneous sensors to detect surface and internal defects, often resulting in sensor fusion errors caused by conflicting evidence and the one-vote veto phenomenon. An evidential space transformation model (ESTM) is proposed to address this challenge, enabling the application of Dempster–Shafer’s combination rule with a newly defined evidence model. The proposed method effectively resolves evidence conflict and veto issues commonly encountered in Dempster–Shafer theory while addressing the limitations of incomplete evidence sets. The performance enhancement is attributed to transforming the evidence model into an Euclidean norm space, facilitating the rational distribution of conflicting evidence. We have conducted the practical experiments with porcelain bushing, which is used in the current high-voltage power systems industry. Experimental results demonstrate the success of the proposed ESTM approach that improves the inspection accuracy, mitigates the impact of erroneous individual observations, and exhibits fault-tolerant characteristics. The success of this research results contributes to the prevention of unexpected power outages, which negatively impact societal safety and the economy.

title: Attention-Embedded VAE: Enhancing Downlink CSI Prediction in FDD Massive MIMO Systems
authors: Hongming Chen, Xiantao Cheng
abstract: In the 5 G and beyond frequency division duplex (FDD) systems, massive multiple input multiple output (MIMO) is a key essential technology due to various advantages. Conventionally, its application requires downlink (DL) channel state information (CSI) feedback from user to base station (BS). To eliminate such feedback, we propose an attention-embedded variational autoencoder (A-VAE) network to directly predict DL CSI using uplink (UL) CSI. The proposed A-VAE network is composed of two types of VAEs, corresponding to UL CSI and DL CSI, respectively. The key features of UL (or DL) CSI are captured by the latent space of the UL (or DL) VAE. The obtained two latent spaces are connected through an attention block. Specifically, we first use the UL VAE to extract its latent space, then use the attention block to transform the UL latent space to the DL one, and finally use the DL VAE to reconstruct the DL CSI. Simulation results verify the advantages of the proposed A-VAE over the state of the art counterparts in terms of performance and complexity.

title: Unleash Your Potential
authors: N/A

title: Reduced-Order Online Robust Control for Linear Systems Over Stochastic MIMO Vehicular Channels
authors: Minjie Tang, Chenyuan Feng, Geyong Min, Tony Q. S. Quek
abstract: Ensuring robust control in uncertain dynamic systems over wireless networks is increasingly vital for applications such as autonomous vehicles and industrial automation. Traditional robust control methods often fall short in real-time wireless settings due to computational overhead and oversimplified channel models. This work introduces a novel online control framework tailored for linear systems operating over stochastic MIMO fading channels with model uncertainties. By transforming the original robust control task into a reduced-order ergodic optimal control problem, the proposed method enables efficient policy learning while avoiding the curse of dimensionality in traditional Bellman equation approaches. To establish theoretical guarantees, the paper employs a controllability cone decomposition technique together with Lyapunov analysis to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of the optimal solution to the virtual optimal control problem, as well as sufficient conditions for the robust stability of the closed-loop system. Leveraging stochastic approximation (SA) and Lyapunov-based ordinary differential equation (ODE) analysis, we propose an efficient online algorithm that provably converges to the optimal policy. Simulation results confirm that the proposed approach achieves superior stability, convergence, and computational performance compared to existing baselines. This work provides a scalable and theoretically grounded solution for real-time robust control in dynamic and uncertain wireless environments.

title: An LLM-Based Framework for Beamforming Optimization
authors: Wei Guo, Kai Liang, Gan Zheng, Xiaoli Chu, Kai-Kit Wong, Chan-Byoung Chae, Huixian Gu
abstract: Beamforming optimization is fundamental to maximizing performance in wireless communication systems. However, traditional optimization algorithms are computationally intensive, and conventional deep learning models often struggle with high-dimensional solution spaces. This article introduces a novel large language model (LLM)- based framework for beamforming optimization in multiuser multiple-input single-output (MU-MISO) systems. The proposed approach integrates multi-head attention, low-rank adaptation (LoRA), and structural priors of optimal beamforming to enable efficient and scalable learning. We present three representative use cases. First, the framework is applied to conventional MU-MISO beamforming optimization to maximize the downlink sum rate under transmit power constraints. Second, to address CSI aging, we extend the model to jointly perform channel prediction and beamforming. Finally, we incorporate a fluid antenna system (FAS) and develop a joint port selection and beamforming strategy using a differentiable relaxation technique. In all scenarios, the proposed LLM-based approach consistently outperforms conventional neural network baselines in sum rate performance while reducing computational overhead. These results demonstrate the potential of LLMs as a powerful tool for physical- layer optimization in future wireless networks.

title: Detect and Avoid (DAA) with Broadcast Remote ID (RID)
authors: Aashay S. Shah, Wentao Chen, Jason Ho, Samuel Jawhar, Tiantian Lu, Mengjie Xie, Pengfei Yan, Peter J. Burke
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: We design, implement and demonstrate a detect and avoid (DAA) system for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) using broadcast Remote ID (RID). An on-board RID receiver detects UAVs and automatically, autonomously (with no pilot input during the flight) takes avoidance action if a user-defined perimeter is breached (in the xy position and z altitude). On breach, the UAV will land, return to launch (RTL), move horizontally, or move vertically. A video alarm is displayed on the on-screen display (OSD), as well as on ground control stations (GCS) connected via wireless telemetry. All of the software and designs are open source, freely available to the drone community, and can be implemented with off-the-shelf parts. This paper demonstrates one possible detect and avoid technology option for collision avoidance between UAVs.

title: Polar Code Construction for Gaussian Channels Based on Approximated Error Evolution
authors: Huiying Song, Kazuhiko Fukawa
abstract: To achieve good performance of polar codes, certain information bits with the highest bit error probabilities (BEPs) should be fixed at a binary value and are referred to as frozen bits. The assignment of these frozen bits is called code construction. Although the exact BEPs of the information bits can be obtained by density evolution, it requires prohibitive computational complexity for calculating and storing the densities. To reduce such complexity, this paper proposes a method to approximate these BEPs when Gaussian channels are assumed. Specifically, since every density is associated with a BEP at the corresponding stage of density evolution, the output BEPs can be approximated using the input ones without computing the densities, which results in a drastic reduction of complexity. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, computer simulations of orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) transmission over both binary input additive white Gaussian noise (BIAWGN) and frequency selective fading channels are conducted. The results show that in both cases, the polar codes constructed using the proposed method can achieve near-optimal block error rate (BLER) while significantly reducing execution time and memory usage.

title: GPU-Enabled Adaptive Frequency Domain Equalization for Fully Virtualized Coherent Access Networks
authors: Akio Yamasaki, Takahiro Suzuki, Sang-Yuep Kim, Jun-Ichi Kani, Tatsuya Shimada, Daisuke Hisano
abstract: In recent years, access and metro networks have been transitioning toward architectures based on disaggregation and virtualization, where software-based implementation of physical-layer signal processing on general-purpose computing platforms has become an important technical challenge. In next-generation access systems, digital coherent transmission enables high-order modulation and polarization multiplexing but requires computationally intensive digital signal processing, such as polarization demultiplexing and adaptive equalization, to be executed in real-time. However, conventional time-domain equalization suffers from computational complexity that increases linearly with the number of equalizer taps, making real-time operation challenging under long-tap conditions caused by chromatic dispersion compensation and future wideband transceivers. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multi-thread frequency-domain adaptive equalizer (MT-FDE) that fully exploits the parallel processing capability of a graphics processing unit (GPU). By combining fast Fourier transform (FFT)–based frequency-domain processing with batch processing, the proposed method reduces computational complexity to logarithmic order with respect to the tap length and enables real-time adaptive equalization including polarization demultiplexing. Numerical simulations and offline and real-time experiments demonstrate that the proposed MT-FDE improves the tolerance to increased tap numbers by a factor of 4.8 and achieves real-time operation under long-tap conditions while maintaining transmission performance comparable to that of conventional time-domain equalizers, providing practical design guidelines for fully virtualized coherent access networks.

title: Josephson Arbitrary Waveform Synthesizer for Flux-Ramp Modulation in Microwave SQUID Multiplexers
authors: Matías Hampel, Luciano Ferreyro, Oliver Kieler, Ricardo Iuzzolino, Mariano Real, Stephan Bauer, Nahuel Müller, Jesús Bonilla Neira, Manuel García Redondo, Juan Bonaparte, Enrico Petriella, Lucía Sucunza, Timo Muscheid, Robert Gartmann, Luis Ardila-Perez, Mathias Wegner, Alan Fuster, Manuel Platino, Sebastian Kempf, Marc Weber
abstract: Microwave SQUID multiplexers (μMUX) have become a key technology for the readout of large arrays of low-temperature detectors, particularly in cosmic microwave background experiments. A critical element of this technique is the flux-ramp modulation signal, whose noise, especially at low frequencies, directly limits the readout performance. This work proposes and experimentally demonstrates the use of a Josephson Arbitrary Waveform Synthesizer (JAWS) as a low-noise, high precision source for generating the sawtooth-shaped flux-ramp signal required in μMUX readout systems. The JAWS, based on the AC Josephson effect, provides intrinsically quantized and spectrally pure waveforms with excellent signal-to-noise ratio. The operation of a 9000-junction JAWS array is characterized and integrated with a cryogenic μMUX device and a custom SDR-based readout system. With this setup, the noise floor level around the harmonic typically used for phase demodulation of the SQUID signal to recover the detector signal was −104dBc/Hz, and the signal-to-noise ratio of this harmonic was 73dB. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using JAWS-based waveform synthesis for μMUX flux-ramp generation.

title: StabilEdge, Formal Verification of Edge AI Applications
authors: Luca Lazzaroni, Riccardo Berta, Vafali Soltanmuradov, David Martín Gómez, Francesco Bellotti
abstract: This paper addresses the critical need for AI trustworthiness in smart IoT systems by focusing on the stability of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) in Edge AI. State-of-the-art model checkers guarantee feasibility of a single stability analysis. However, a comprehensive assessment requires multiple runs, considering different noise levels and output targets. This is feasible in Edge AI applications, because of the limited size of the involved DNNs. However, direct user interaction with the model checker via its command-line interface is suboptimal, with a high potential for error and inefficiency. We address this issue by proposing a higher-level tool, namely StabilEdge, that abstracts and automatically manages the technical complexities of the underlying verification engine through a graphical layer. The goal is to help designers to concentrate on application-level logic and constraints, rather than the underlying mechanics of the verification tool. StabilEdge performs parametric stability analysis by modeling realistic input perturbations, such as sensor noise/attacks, to assess if they may cause dangerous mispredictions. StabilEdge operationalizes a novel methodology unifying four complementary Edge-AI stability analyses: local robustness, focused evaluation of specific input features (sensitivity) and output target adherence (targeted robustness and sensitivity). The tool generates three key graphical outputs: stability constraint violations, class transition patterns, and input-output constraint trade-offs. It provides continuous verification feedback and specialized support for time series and vision tasks. Overall, StabilEdge automates the full verification workflow - from perturbation modeling to formal constraint generation and solver-level monitoring -, with no need for expert manual configuration. Evaluations on three edge AI tasks demonstrate that StabilEdge quantifies noise impact on DNN performance at a fine granularity, greatly enhancing the depth of NN performance assessment. This analysis yields also key insights, such as the explicit trade-off between model accuracy and its vulnerability to localized perturbations. We thus argue that the tool fulfills its goal of supporting designers in rigorously specifying DNN stability and making informed decisions on sensor selection and overall system architecture. The current implementation exploits the Marabou model checker and may integrate other engines in the future, especially to address scalability issues due to input/output and model size. To support free research in the field, StabilEdge is released open source at: https://github.com/Elios-Lab/StabilEdge.

title: ICAIIHI 2025 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)

title: IOT-SIU 2025 Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)

title: Integrating Community-Based Environmental Assessment into Engineering Education: A Case Study on Microplastics Distribution and River Discharge in Klang River
authors: Jurina Jaafar, Zulhafizal Othman, Nurul Fariha Lokman, Harianingsih, Rohana Hassan, Wan Fazlida Hanim Abdullah
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Conference on Engineering Education (ICEED)
abstract: This study underscores the integration of real-world environmental issues into engineering education. Practical exposure to analytical methods like FTIR and pollution data analysis enhances students' skills and critical thinking. Incorporating simulation tools and multidisciplinary approaches combining environmental science, chemical engineering, and data analytics fosters a deeper understanding of sustainability. Collaborations with local agencies and community-based projects further prepare students to address environmental challenges, inspiring future engineers to develop responsible, innovative solutions for pollution and industrial waste management. Microplastics, defined as plastic particles less than 5 mm, are a growing environmental concern due to their widespread presence and ecological impact. This study investigates the presence, properties, and distribution of microplastics in the Klang River, particularly near the manufacturing zone in Miel, Seksyen 16, Shah Alam. The area is suspected to contribute wastewater that degrades water quality. Water samples collected from this source were analyzed using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), revealing the presence of Polyamide (PA), Polyethylene (PE), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polypropylene (PP), and Polystyrene (PS). These are commonly linked to food packaging and industrial waste. The detection of food additives like carboxylic acid and isothiocyanate highlights the complexity of the pollution. Water quality parameters measured include Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) values of 8.09, 8.24, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$7.96 \text{mg} / \mathrm{L}$</tex>, Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) values of 31, 33, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$45 \text{mg} / \mathrm{L}$</tex>, and turbidity readings of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.13,1.64$</tex>, and 2.17 NTU.

title: Evaluating Image Compression Potential for Physical World Building
authors: Shane Kuo, Ing-Chau Chang
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Conventional algorithms for depth estimation generally use uncompressed images for training, testing, and validation. Additionally, the increased prominence of synthetic datasets has, in part, driven significant increases in their size, making compression increasingly valuable as a means to reduce storage requirements.
