title: Improving the Identification of Fraudulent Activity with Cascade Attention-Based Ensemble Learning: An Interpretable AI Approach
authors: Mehdi Hosseini Chagahi, Abolfazl Yekaneh, Saeed Mohammadi Dashtaki, Hyun-Han Kwon, Behzad Moshiri, Md. Jalil Piran
publish: 2025 International Conference on AI-Driven STEM Education and Learning Technologies (AISTEMEDU)
abstract: Credit card fraud (CCF) has been recognized as a growing issue in the financial industry as a result of the number of fraud-related activities that have been on the upswing. The existing machine learning (ML) solutions are mainly based on traditional classifiers or simple ensemble solutions and often cannot detect complex, non-linear patterns of fraud. This paper suggests an improvement to CCF detection with the addition of two attention layers to a traditional stacking classifier. Two superior aggregation operators dependent ordered weighted averaging (DOWA) and induced ordered weighted averaging (IOWA), are used in the first attention layer to mix the soft outputs of first-level classifiers; DOWA helps to reduce the impact of the outliers by placing heterogeneous weight of the samples whereas IOWA uses gradual weight allocation through the application of a gradient-based optimization procedure similar to the neural network process. The second attention layer uses mechanism of synergistic combination that is developed to detect the most trustworthy aggregated value that will be transmitted to a meta-learner to make final prediction. The conducted experimental findings on a large-scale dataset of credit-card transactions indicate that the suggested model outperforms traditional ensemble classifiers, achieving a detection rate of 94.45% and area under the curve (AUC) of 0.97, which makes it essential to note that the suggested model is the most effective when working in the context of a multifaceted concept of fraud detection.

title: Machine Learning-Based Power Estimation of Cmos Inverter Using Ltspice Simulation Data
authors: Satendra Dhangar, K.B. Yadav, Rajeev Hulekal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive process for power estimation of a CMOS inverter through LTspice simulation data with machine learning methods. A total simulation runs were performed by varying the supply voltage <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.8 \mathrm{V}-1.2 \mathrm{V})$</tex>, load capacitance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \text{pF}, 50 \text{pF}, 100 \text{pF}$</tex>) and operating frequency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \text{Hz}-10 \text{kHz}$</tex>). From each run, average, dynamic, and static power components were obtained and compiled into a summary dataset. A Random Forest regression model was fitted to the summary dataset to forecast average power based on design and operating parameters. The trained model was also used to estimate the switching activity factor (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex>) with the formula <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha{ = }$</tex> (Pavg,ML - Pstatic)/ CV\^{}2f. The average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> values were then used to analytically recompute the average power as Pavg = Pstatic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+\alpha \text{CV}^{\wedge} 2 \mathrm{f}$</tex>, showing close agreement with the simulation data. This hybrid approach, based on SPICE simulations and ma- chine learning, is capable of accurately capturing the nonlinear dependencies within the CMOS power characteristics, and gives us an accurate, scalable methodology to estimate power that is applicable to larger logic gates, as well as low-power designs, which is important for the development and verification of energy-efficient semiconductors. The novelty of this work is the combination of LTspice-derived waveform data and a Random Forest model that can quickly and accurately estimate dynamic power.

title: Spline-Interpolated Density and Dependency-Aware Outlier Scoring for Lightweight Deployment
authors: Angela Li, David Li
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Detecting outliers in high-dimensional data under resource constraints remains a central challenge in real-world deployments such as wearables, IoT, and edge systems. Existing lightweight methods often assume feature independence, which limits their ability to capture joint anomalies that arise from multivariate dependencies. In this paper, we introduce a semi-parametric outlier detection framework that extends spline-interpolated marginal density estimation with a copula-based joint dependency model. Our approach estimates smooth, non-parametric univariate densities using compact cubic splines and transforms inputs into uniform space via empirical CDFs. We then model inter-feature dependencies using a flexible family of copulas—including elliptical (Gaussian, t) and Archimedean types—and automatically select the optimal copula based on AIC and BIC scores. The resulting joint density yields a probabilistic outlier score that captures both marginal extremity and multivariate atypicality. We further derive a Bayesian posterior scoring variant that incorporates prior assumptions about anomaly prevalence without requiring supervision. Extensive analysis demonstrates that our method maintains computational and memory efficiency competitive with HBOS and ECOD, while significantly improving detection of contextual and joint outliers in real-time settings. This work contributes a statistically grounded, scalable, and deployable outlier detection paradigm that unifies interpretable density modeling and dependency-aware anomaly reasoning.

title: Koopman Spectral Dynamics for Interpretable and Predictive Semantic Drift in Language Models
authors: Satyam Mishra, Vi Phung, Phuc Vu, Shivam Mishra, Sundaram Mishra, Vishwanath Bijalwan, Tran Duc Tan
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Semantic drift: the gradual change in word meaning over time, poses a critical challenge to natural language understanding in diachronic corpora. Existing methods detect drift via alignment or distance metrics but lack the ability to model its dynamics or forecast future semantic change. We present Koopman Spectral Dynamics, the first framework to model semantic drift as a nonlinear dynamical system in word embedding space, linearly lifted via the Koopman operator. This enables interpretable forecasting, spectral decomposition, and drift attribution using Koopman eigenfunctions and modes. Applied to the HistWords: All English dataset (1800s-1990s), our method outperforms strong baselines: PCA, LSTM, and Pro-crustes, with up to 24.5% lower RMSE and 19.2% lower MAE in word forecasting, and +7.8 % accuracy in semantic coherence classification. We analyze drift trajectories using spectral energy curves, degradation plots, and mode attributions, and introduce causal influence maps via Granger and mutual information matrices. Our model generalizes to multi-word phrases (e.g., gay marriage) and supports a real-time interactive forecasting tool. This work introduces Koopman operator theory to NLP and data mining, offering a principled, spectral foundation for modeling and interpreting meaning evolution over time.

title: Efficient Real-Time Sign Language Recognition using Deep CNN and LSTM Models
authors: Syed Farhan, Soumya Ranjan Mishra, Parth Sarthi Sharma, Asharib Ahmed Khan, Shozaib Khan, Shahzan Abbas Raza
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: A predominantly used form of language used by the people from the auditory or speech impaired community is the sign language. It differs a quite a lot from auditory speech therefore, having its own grammatical ques and sign sequence. Close to around 465 million people around the globe are auditory or speech impaired, and with the recent advancement especially to models like Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and, LongShort Term Memory (LSTM) architecture in the field of Deep Learning, Sign Language prediction of American Sign Language has become more easier than ever. This study aims to present a deeper view on MobileNetV2-based CNN architecture against an LSTM architecture baseline by their performance metrics, analyzing their architectures and also their inference speeds. The CNN architecture demonstrated superior efficiency, accuracy, and precision when compared to the LSTM architecture, its potential for practical Gesture recognition systems.

title: Multiple Questions Generation to Prevent Plagiarism in Computer Network Lab Assignments
authors: Wai Kok Chan, Keng Tuck Wong, Khairil Anuar
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Conference on Engineering Education (ICEED)
abstract: Even though lab assessment is important for student learning, it is plagued with excessive plagiarism. Technical solutions for plagiarism prevention can be resource intensive and may not be feasible for examiners or institutions with limited resources. In this paper, a methodology for generating multiple lab assignment questions for computer network lab assignments is described. This method can reduce plagiarism by ensuring unique questions for most of the students. It also encourages discussion among students and promotes self-learning among peers as direct copying is prevented. A combination of manual question drafting and coding is used to generate questions. A list of steps to be taken and avoided is also described. Some related issues on unique question generation such as water marking, question reshuffling and question difficulty adjustment are also discussed. The generated lab assignment questions are suitable for lab test, online lab test and take-home lab assignments, depending on the examiner's discretion. All the materials for question generation is given in our GitHub repository

title: Dictionary-based Byte-Pair Encoding tokenizer for morphologically rich languages
authors: Dávid Držík
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: Tokenization is a critical preprocessing step for large language models, especially for morphologically rich, low-resource languages like Slovak, where standard corpus-based methods struggle with vast numbers of inflected word forms. This paper proposes and evaluates a novel training methodology for a Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenizer that utilizes a comprehensive morphological dictionary instead of a raw text corpus. The primary objective is to measure the potential of this dictionary-based approach to produce more compact tokenizations for known, isolated word forms. The prototype tokenizer, DictSlovakTokenizer, is compared against the corpus-trained tokenizer of SlovakBERT using a rigorous, stratified 10-fold cross-validation to ensure an unbiased evaluation on unseen forms. The results demonstrate a statistically significant reduction in the average number of tokens per word across various word-length and part-of-speech categories. This study serves as a foundational analysis, confirming that structured lexical resources are a highly effective data source for building efficient subword vocabularies. While acknowledging the prototype's limitations, such as its inability to process continuous real-world text, the study highlights its potential as a powerful complementary strategy to traditional corpus-based training, particularly for developing morphologically aware tokenizers in low-resource settings.

title: Leveraging RetinaNet for the Automated Detection of Cardiovascular Disease Biomarkers in Retinal Scans
authors: Rasi Ram S R, Baskar D, Sandeep C S
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) globally still stands out as the primary cause of the most morbidity and mortality cases, and it is essential to commence the treatment quite early. A new system is introduced, employing retinal imaging as the non-invasive diagnostic tool for CVD detection, harnessing deep learning skills to recognize retinal features associated with cardiovascular conditions. The system uses the RetinaNet model that has gained a reputation for being very precise in its classifications, it performs feature extraction and classification from images themselves for the signs of CVD, is there detected automatically. This design integrates a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) structure, which can handle complex patterns of images and is capable of managing data types with high accuracy, and also provides speedy output. The main advantage of the system comes from its ability to be the one that usually deals with the situation of high colors sickness, collecting some pieces of information that are closely related to cardiovascular health, and doing the job in a way that is reliable and just human intervention with a minimum. Thus, the approach offered paves the way for a sustainable solution to the challenge of early CVD detection, which might have a very substantial positive impact on the cost-effectiveness of the healthcare sector and over and above that, the changes in the lives of the patients through the operation that are in good time as the outcome.

title: Enterprise Asset Tracking Using YOLOv7 for Visual Recognition and RFID Signal Verification
authors: Aakansha Soy, Bharani B R, Sutar Manisha Balkrishna, M. Murali, Sharipov Dilshodbek Shodmonjon Ugli
publish: 2025 International Conference on AI-Driven STEM Education and Learning Technologies (AISTEMEDU)
abstract: Efficient resource management and transparency in the operations of large organizations are essential with the use of enterprise asset tracking. Integration of RFID technology and visual recognition can present an effective solution to identity and verification of assets in real-time. Nonetheless, the current systems are usually victims of the drawbacks like low precision in identifying objects in changing conditions of light and failure to authenticate those assets that do not have a good response frequency using RFID. The consequences of these problems are misidentification, lost inventory, and impaired visibility of assets. To solve these issues, this paper suggests a hybrid system named as "YOLO-RFID TrackNet" that combines the YOLOv7 to provide high accuracy visual recognition and RFID signal verification to provide dual layer asset validation. YOLOv7 is a cutting-edge object detector that is used to detect and categorize assets with high precision using surveillance feeds, and RFID module cross validates the detection with the relevant asset IDs in real time. Experimental findings indicate that YOLO-RFID TrackNet is better at detecting assets and improving verification errors by 18% and 25% respectively than traditional systems. These results indicate that a combination of a high-level deep learning model and RFID checking can become a viable approach to scalable, smart, and correct asset tracking of the enterprise.

title: A Critical Review of Novel and Non-Traditional Methods in Multi-Focus Image Fusion
authors: Prabhishek Singh, Shailesh Bhosekar, Deepak Garg, Manoj Diwakar, Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Aditya Joshi
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: For visual surveillance and other applications, there is a one big challenge to identify the information of defocused images. This challenge can be resolved using multifocused image fusion approaches using similar images of same object. Visual sensor networks (VSN) make use of these image fusion methods effectively for effective surveillance and motion tracking. The fusion algorithms are then compared using a multi-focus image dataset and established objective fusion parameters. Both the subjective and objective evaluation criteria are given, along with a detailed discussion of a few common objective assessment indicators. Here we found that deep learning provides best outcomes in compare to traditional methods. Multi-focus optical image fusion has come a long way in the past few years, but it still has a long way to go. Therefore in this paper, recent advancements on intelligent multi-focus image fusion have been reviewed. In this review paper, we review existing studies on non-conventional multi focus image fusion approaches, highlighting the most recent progresses and future directions.

title: Monozukuri Assimilation in Higher Education Institutions Beyond Japan: Comparative Studies from the Perspective of Educators
authors: Siti Rahmah Aid, Pramila Tamunaidu, Ooi Chia Yee, Nordinah Ismail, Uswah Khairuddin, Farah Liana Mohd Redzuan
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Conference on Engineering Education (ICEED)
abstract: The concept of Monozukuri, often translated as “the art of making things,” embodies a philosophy of craftsmanship, continuous improvement, and value creation that has been integral to Japan's post-World War II industrial success. Over time, this philosophy has transcended its manufacturing roots and found relevance in education, particularly through problem-based and project-based learning. This study conducts a comparative analysis of the assimilation of Monozukuri from the perspective of educators in three higher education institutions in Malaysia, and one in Japan where the concept originated. Using a Likert-scale approach focusing on implementation practices, the study captures how educators interpret and incorporate Monozukuri into curriculum design and classroom activities. Findings reveal that while the Japanese institution maintains a deep-rooted, formalized integration of Monozukuri, one institution outside Japan shows high awareness and structured adoption. The other two institutions, although not explicitly familiar with the term Monozukuri, nonetheless embrace its core values such as craftsmanship, problem-solving, teamwork, and innovation through their teaching methodologies. These results highlight the implicit and explicit diffusion of Monozukuri values across international educational contexts, emphasizing the pivotal role of educators in sustaining and adapting this philosophy in diverse learning environments.

title: Detection of Fake News on Social Media Using Machine Learning Approach
authors: Shraddha Shah, Sachin Patel
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The digital revolution has transformed information dissemination, creating unprecedented access to news and content globally. However, this technological advancement has also facilitated the proliferation of misinformation across online platforms. Misleading content can significantly impact economic stability, political discourse, and social harmony, underscoring the urgent need for robust systems capable of distinguishing between factual and fabricated information. This research presents a comprehensive framework for misinformation detection utilizing cutting-edge data science approaches and machine learning algorithms. By employing natural language processing (NLP), advanced machine learning techniques, and statistical analysis methods, we identify distinctive patterns, textual characteristics, and contextual indicators that differentiate between authentic and fabricated news content. Our findings contribute to the development of automated verification systems that can enhance information integrity in digital spaces, fostering a more informed and discerning public discourse.

title: A Shopping Service Robot Framework with Visual-WEM Tracking and Intersection-Aware Following
authors: Hanchen Yao, Jianwei Peng, Houde Dai, Fanbiao Li, Tim C. Lueth
abstract: The shopping service robot (SSR) is designed to offer a superior shopping experience through its continuous target following and companion services. However, the SSR encounters difficulties in complex environments, including visual occlusions and spatial constraints in narrow aisles. To address these difficulties, the study proposes a shopping service robot framework that integrates innovative perception, following control, and path planning modules. Firstly, the perception module employs a multi-sensor fusion method for human target tracking, integrating both red-green-blue-depth (RGB-D) camera and wireless electromagnetic (WEM) data by using an extended Kalman filter (EKF). Secondly, the target-following control module employs an omnidirectional constrained control law, which ensures synchronized orientation alignment between the SSR and the human target. Finally, the path planning module employs topological mapping to encode intersection geometries as path nodes, thereby guiding the SSR pass through narrow shelf aisles. In real supermarkets, we evaluated the target tracking approach under shelf occlusions and the human following task within narrow aisles. Experimental results demonstrate that the visual-WEM tracking approach achieves a pose tracking accuracy of (4.56 mm, 2.98<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>) under shelf occlusions. This study establishes the feasibility of human-robot collaboration in facilitating a hands-free shopping experience, highlighting its potential as a substitute for conventional shopping carts.

title: Standby-Mode Burst Operation with Delay-Compensated Bang-Bang Charge Control for Half-Bridge LLC Converters
authors: Ming-Chang Tsou, Jyun-Ru Lin, Yao-Tsung Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Burst mode control strategies have been widely adopted in LLC resonant converters due to the significant increase in switching loss at light load. This paper proposes a standby-mode burst-operation control based on bang-bang charge control (BBCC) for half-bridge LLC resonant converters to improve light-load efficiency while preserving a fast transient response. The voltage variation across the resonant capacitor, which is proportional to the per-cycle charge transfer and thus the output power, is used as a control indicator to compare with the feedback voltage. To compensate for the extra output power induced by turn-off delays, a digital/logic compensation block is introduced to cancel the delay-dependent term, thereby restoring the proportionality between the output power and the threshold voltage. To verify the control method, a controller IC implementing the proposed logic was evaluated on a 400 V input, 20 V/12 A (240 W) output half-bridge LLC power board. At 10 W (4.17% load), the efficiency improves from 87.26% (normal mode) to 93.94% (standby mode), i.e., +6.68%. The mode-transition boundaries are set at 10 W (entry) and 30 W (exit), and the transition times are 108 μs (normal mode to standby mode) and 107 μs (standby mode to normal mode), respectively.

title: Federated Network Slicing in Multi-domain, Multi-technology, and Multi-provider Networks
authors: H. H. Esmat, B. Lorenzo
abstract: In multi-domain multi-technology network slicing (NS), different service providers within the same and/or different administrative domains are involved in meeting a service level agreement (SLA). In fact, the SLA requirements must be dynamically decomposed into portions that each domain can support based on their network condition. This paper presents a framework to design federated multi-domain NS in which providers collaborate to set policies for SLA decomposition, task offloading, routing, and resource sharing across domains. First, a centralized scheme is proposed as a benchmark where a cross-domain orchestrator decomposes the SLA and performs multi-path routing and resource allocation in negotiation with the domain controllers. Then, we adopt decomposition theory to delegate decomposing the SLA to each domain controller and create multi-domain network slices in a fully distributed manner. Through consistency pricing, we enforce fair collaboration between domains to share resources and perform task-split offloading and multi-path multi-domain routing. We evaluate our approach using typical sixth-generation (6G) real-world examples. Our results show that our approach achieves two to four times higher utility compared to schemes without collaboration and/or SLA decomposition.

title: Waveguide-Based Characterization of Dielectric Sheets Using Transmission Coefficients
authors: Bing Xue, Ugur Cem Hasar
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a waveguide-based method for characterizing the complex permittivity of dielectric sheets without requiring reflection coefficients at the terahertz (THz) frequency range. By placing two identical samples between two open-ended waveguides with different separations, the permittivity can be derived solely from transmission coefficients measured using a vector network analyzer (VNA). The method is validated through full-wave simulations and measurements from 140 to 220 GHz. The effects of material thickness and measurement resolution are investigated. Compared with the conventional method, the proposed approach shows improved accuracy and reduced calibration complexity, making it suitable for characterizing thin dielectric sheets in the THz range.

title: Variational Bayesian Inference-Based Method for Antenna Array Diagnosis
authors: Chunyuan Hu, Yujie Lin, Xiangming Li, Kai Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Sparse diagnosis techniques for antenna arrays provide an efficient approach to fault diagnosis by leveraging the sparse nature of faulty elements. In practical scenarios, an unknown measurement scale factor exists between measured signals and fault-free reference signals. Existing works often implicitly assume perfect data normalization, yet even slight scale mismatches can destroy the sparsity of differential signals and induce an estimation error floor. A sparse diagnosis method based on variational Bayesian inference (VBI) is proposed to jointly model and estimate the scale factor and sparse fault parameters. First, a complex measurement scale factor is introduced to quantitatively characterize the scale mismatch between measured signals and the reference signals. Second, within the VBI framework, we design a coordinate ascent variational inference (CAVI) algorithm that delivers stable convergence by alternately updating the variational distributions of the sparse fault parameters and the scale factor. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately localize faulty elements and estimate their parameters. Additionally, it achieves high-precision estimation of the scale factor, overcoming the limitations of traditional methods that either overlook the scale factor or fail to estimate it with sufficient precision.

title: Unifying Geometric and Learning-Based Visual-Inertial Odometry: A Comprehensive Evaluation and Comparative Study
authors: Fuqiang Gu, Kangping Ji, Jiarui Dou, Hui Zhao, Yuanke Li, Xianlei Long
abstract: Visual–Inertial Odometry (VIO) is a core component of state estimation in robotics and autonomous systems. Over the past decade, diverse VIO approaches have emerged, including classical geometric and optimization-based methods, hybrid learning-augmented systems, and fully learning-driven models. However, objective comparison remains difficult due to heterogeneous evaluation protocols, datasets, and metrics, complicating algorithm selection for practical deployment. This paper presents a unified, decision-oriented benchmark of representative VIO algorithms across multiple methodological paradigms. The methods are evaluated on EuRoC MAV, TUM-VI, KITTI Odometry, and the challenging multi-platform Fusion-PortableV2 dataset under a consistent evaluation protocol. Performance is assessed in terms of trajectory accuracy, drift behavior, scale consistency, and computational efficiency. Beyond accuracy, we analyze front-end and back-end computational costs to reveal trade-offs between robustness, precision, and resource consumption. Based on extensive cross-dataset experiments, we provide practical guidance for algorithm selection, identify common limitations and failure modes, and discuss empirically grounded open challenges and future research directions. This work serves as a practical reference for researchers and engineers in the VIO community.

title: Experimental Validation of Thermoregulatory Modeling for Human Back Exposure at 28 GHz
authors: Shoma Kato, Akimasa Hirata, Hiroshi Masuda, Akiko Matsumoto, Sachiko Kodera
abstract: This study investigates skin temperature rise during dorsal exposure to 28 GHz quasi-millimeter waves using an electromagnetic–thermal computational modeling framework considering thermoregulation. Human exposure experiments were numerically reproduced using anatomically detailed models of Japanese adults augmented with age- and sex-related thermoregulatory functions. An exposure system with dielectric lens antennas provided controlled irradiation to the back, and the measured skin temperatures were compared with the computational predictions. The computational framework reproduced the experimental temperature rises well for both the 64-region of interest (ROI) and 1-ROI. Furthermore, the model reproduced the tendency that female participants exhibited higher temperature rises than males, which was mainly attributed to anatomical variations in muscle and fat composition. Age-related effects were modest, likely because the older adult group was approximately 65 years old. The predicted temperature rise was 2.48 °C, which is within the permissible limit of 2.5 °C (derived from the operational threshold of 5 °C with a reduction factor of 2) when evaluated at an absorbed power density of 100 W/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> for occupational exposure, as specified in international exposure guidelines. These findings demonstrate the applicability of the thermoregulatory model to superficial absorption for exposure to quasi- and millimeter waves and support its use for assessment across different populations, such as those differing in age, sex, and thermoregulatory capacity.

title: UAV-Aided 3D Spectrum Mapping Framework: Architecture, Technique, and Opportunity
authors: Jie Wang, Qiuming Zhu, Ziye Jia, Yuanjin Zheng, Kai-Kuang Ma, Qianhao Gao, Yiran Chen, Zhipeng Lin, Qihui Wu
abstract: Spectrum mapping (SM) visualizes spectrum information across geographical regions through radio environment maps (REMs), enabling critical applications in spectrum monitoring, management, and security. In dynamic space-air-ground integrated networks, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can extend the sensing coverage of three-dimensional (3D) SM beyond traditional ground systems. However, constructing accurate REMs in dynamic and unknown 3D environments remains challenging due to sparse, noisy measurements, and nonstationary propagation conditions. This article proposes a comprehensive UAV-aided 3D SM framework, integrating architecture, key techniques, and practical opportunities. It features: 1) Adaptive air-to-ground channel modeling using a data-driven channel dictionary to capture realistic propagation dynamics; 2) Intelligent UAV path planning combining realtime sensing with an information-driven decision framework for efficient data acquisition; 3) Robust spatial completion via sparse Bayesian learning to ensure accurate recovery of unsensed regions; and 4) Channel model-guided deep learning for temporal prediction of spectrum evolution. A prototype system is implemented and validated in both simulations and field measurements. Applications in dynamic spectrum access, interference management, and UAV navigation are discussed, along with open challenges in data fusion, real-time processing, and adaptive modeling. This work provides a foundation for the 6th-generation (6G)-enabled spectrum management and UAV-assisted wireless networks, bridging theory and practical deployment.

title: Bridging Bug Localization and Issue Fixing: A Hierarchical Localization Framework Leveraging Large Language Models
authors: Jianming Chang, Xin Zhou, Lulu Lulu, David Lo, Bixin Li
abstract:
Automated issue fixing is a critical task in software debugging and has recently garnered significant attention from academia and industry. However, existing fixing techniques predominantly focus on the repair phase, often overlooking the importance of improving the preceding bug localization phase. As a foundational step in issue fixing, bug localization plays a pivotal role in determining the overall effectiveness of the entire process.

To enhance the precision of issue fixing by accurately identifying bug locations in large-scale projects, this paper presents BugCerberus, the first hierarchical bug localization framework powered by three customized large language models. First, BugCerberus analyzes intermediate representations of bug-related programs at file, function, and statement levels and extracts bug-related contextual information from the representations. Second, BugCerberus designs three customized LLMs at each level using bug reports and contexts to learn the patterns of bugs. Finally, BugCerberus hierarchically searches for bug-related code elements through well-tuned models to localize bugs at three levels. With BugCerberus, we further investigate the impact of bug localization on the issue fixing.

We evaluate BugCerberus on the widely-used benchmark SWE-bench-lite. The experimental results demonstrate that BugCerberus outperforms all baselines. Specifically, at the fine-grained statement level, BugCerberus surpasses the state-of-the-art in Top-N (N=1, 3, 5, 10) by 16.5%, 5.4%, 10.2%, and 23.1%, respectively. Moreover, in the issue fixing experiments, BugCerberus improves the fix rate of the existing issue fixing approach Agentless by 17.4% compared to the best baseline, highlighting the significant impact of enhanced bug localization on automated issue fixing.

title: RS-LLIC: A Lightweight Learned Image Compression Model with Knowledge Distillation for Onboard Remote Sensing
authors: Dongyang Liu, Junping Zhang, Xuejian Liang, Yunxiao Qi, Yujie Song, Yunqiao Xi, Jing Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: With the rapid advancement of remote sensing satellites toward higher spatial resolution and revisit frequency, the explosive growth of image data has posed severe challenges to the efficiency of space-to-ground transmission. Traditional onboard compression standards, such as JPEG2000, often fail to maintain satisfactory reconstruction quality under high compression ratios, limiting their applicability in large-scale remote sensing scenarios. Although learned image compression (LIC) methods have achieved remarkable improvements in rate-distortion (RD) performance, their high computational complexity hinders deployment on resource-constrained onboard platforms. To address these challenges, this paper proposes RS-LLIC, a lightweight learned image compression framework with knowledge distillation tailored for onboard remote sensing, following the “onboard encoding and ground decoding” paradigm. Specifically, an efficient encoder architecture is designed to significantly reduce onboard computational costs, while a knowledge distillation-based training strategy is introduced to guide the lightweight encoder in feature learning using a teacher model, thereby improving RD performance without incurring additional inference overhead. Experimental results on multiple remote sensing datasets demonstrate that the proposed RS-LLIC achieves superior compression performance with extremely low encoder complexity, providing an effective solution for high-quality and efficient onboard remote sensing image compression. The code will be released on https://github.com/dy196/RS-LLIC.

title: High-accuracy Sensing for High-Speed Vehicles in OFDM: Complete and Missing Data
authors: Yuxin Tang, Hongxia Miao, Mugen Peng
abstract: In wideband communications, user location sensing technology has emerged as a critical topic for the sixth-generation (6G) communications. However, existing approaches often ignore the positioning of multiple moving users with missing data and lack effective velocity estimation capabilities. To tackle these problems, based on orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM), this paper proposes a novel two-stage position and velocity estimation method for multiple high-speed mobile users in both complete data and missing data cases. To develop a unified framework for global optimal estimation applicable to both complete and missing data cases, we transform the parameter estimation problem into a rank-constrained Hankel-Toeplitz structured matrix recovery problem. By solving this problem, positioning and radial velocity parameters can be obtained. For complete and missing data cases, we derive the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) and damped CRB, respectively. Simulation results confirm high estimation accuracy, achieving centimeter-level distance precision (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< $</tex-math></inline-formula>0.03m) and AoA estimation errors closely approaching the derived CRB, with a marginal gap on the order of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-3}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, even with severe data missing. The study also systematically examines the impacts of antenna array size, OFDM parameters and missing data patterns.

title: Smart Solar Panel Orientation and Performance Analytics using IoT and Cloud Integration
authors: R Suganya, S Kavin, N Krishnammal, K R Kevin
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Generating energy from solar PV systems can be suitably efficient if a technology system has the optimal angle to the sun. Fixed solar PV panels can generate substantial energy, but they are often less than optimal because they do not move to obtain the optimal angle with respect to the sun. In this paper, the authors present an IoT system for solar tracking and monitoring that will optimize energy generation and provide innovative performance analysis. Energy generation relies on having the proper angle with respect to the sun. Light-sensitive sensors and servo motors powered by a microcontroller together with real-time measurements allow the tracking system to position the solar panel to align with the sun's angle. An IoT-based monitoring system collects voltage, current, temperature, and other parameters for the panel while also controlling the panel's solar tracking. IoT also allows parameters to be sent to a cloud that users can access to see energy analytics and efficiency reports and receive performance alerts. Experimental testing has been carried out, which verified improved energy output for the solar panel system in practical use compared to a fixed panel. The IoT feature of the proposed system improves operational reliability and increases user engagement. The proposed system shows a viable path to building smart renewable energy infrastructure and increasing the integration of advanced distributed energy resources through sustainable energy use.

title: Messaging Chat with Steganographic Security using Microdots
authors: Bárbara Emma Sánchez Rinza, Mariano Aldair Aquino Regalado
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: Steganography is different from cryptography. While cryptography encodes data to keep it secret, steganography hides the data itself. In real life, it can be applied to digital media such as images, audio files, and text documents.In this work, the steganography process was carried out using microdots (tiny dots in a text that conceals information from another text). This was made possible by converting the text into an image so that it could be read by the recipient. Communication was done through a chat where the text information was sent, and the dots (microdots) carried hidden information.Microdots are extremely small texts or images, approximately 1 mm in diameter, which are used to hide information from unintended recipients.

title: Finding the Structure of a Convolutional Neural Network to Predict EURO Games by Employing Genetic Algorithm
authors: Arisoa S. Randrianasolo
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: Constructing a deep neural network of any type is difficult if you attempt to create it from scratch. This process requires figuring out the number of layers, the number of units per layer, the type of layers, and the learning parameters. In this paper, we illustrate our attempt to use a genetic algorithm to automate the search for the structure of a convolutional neural network used to predict the outcomes of the 2020 UEFA Men’s Euro. Our approach automatically generated a convolutional neural network that achieved an accuracy of 69.8% and still has considerable room for improvement to be explored.

title: Real-Time Evaluation of Smart Meter Data Communication Under Cybersecurity Attacks
authors: Harsh Kumar, Oscar A. Alvarez, Sanjeev Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) relies extensively on reliable data communication to support efficient energy management and system control. Smart meters enable bi-directional communication between utilities and customers, allowing utilities to monitor grid conditions more closely, improve operational decision-making, and collect energy usage data frequently from remote customer locations. Ensuring both data integrity and communication availability within AMI is therefore critically important. This paper presents a series of experiments designed to evaluate the communication availability of smart meters when subjected to cybersecurity threats. Three widely deployed meters from different manufacturers were examined to assess the effects of common cyberattacks, including Ping Flood, Smurf, and TCP/SYN flood attacks. The experimental results showed that these attacks significantly disrupted smart meter communication, with the degree of resilience varying across devices. In several instances, complete loss of communication between the smart meters and the utility headend was observed. Although some meters were able to restore communication once the attacks ceased, the recovery times differed notably among the devices. The findings of this investigation highlight the risks that cyberattacks pose to AMI communication systems and provide insight for smart meter manufacturers seeking to enhance the security of future designs. Additionally, the results offer utilities practical guidance for implementing improved cybersecurity measures to protect smart meter communication within AMI deployments.

title: Topic Modeling and Speech Categorization for Sinhala Parliamentary Discussions Using Natural Language Processing
authors: PHND Jayawardana, DU Vidanagama
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Technology and Computing (ICATC)
abstract: Natural Language Processing has become a powerful means of analyzing large-scale textual corpora, particularly within political communication. Parliamentary speeches are rich repositories of governance priorities and societal concerns. In Sri Lanka, Sinhala parliamentary speeches remain underexplored due to linguistic complexity and limited computational resources. This study introduces an automated system to categorize Sinhala parliamentary speeches into key policy domains such as Education, Agriculture, and Health. The methodology applies lemmatization to normalize morphological variations, Term Frequency Inverse Document Frequency for feature representation, and multinomial logistic regression for supervised classification. Unlike conventional binary classification approaches, the model predicts the most relevant domain for each speech while also providing the proportion of speech content attributed to each category, enabling fine-grained analysis of parliamentary focus. Empirical evaluations demonstrate strong and balanced classification performance, with consistent precision, recall, and F1-score values across all categories. Trend analysis further reveals sectoral priorities and variations by political party and speaker role. This study contributes to legislative transparency and advances Natural Language Processing research for Sinhala, a low-resource language.

title: NLP-Powered Chatbots for Personalised Learning Experiences
authors: M. Laxman Rao, Kranthika Terala, Sathish Kumar Ravichandran, Gunajit, G.M. Prem Kumar, R. Anusuya
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: This empirical research investigates the role of NLP-based chatbots in personalised learning using secondary data. The research analyzes the performance of chatbots, learning results, and student engagement using machine learning tools and educational datasets. The outcomes indicate an increase of 23.4% in the engagement of students and a 18. 7% increase in learning retention with respect to the conventional approaches. The most important indicators are 89.2 percent of accurate response, 82.6% of satisfaction, and 76.8% of task accomplishment. Although its advantages are evident, such problems as prejudice, restricted access, and excessive dependence are also present. The research reveals the importance of ethical use and teacher-involvement to make sure that chatbots are really beneficial to the process of student learning in a meaningful and responsible manner.

title: Rethinking Invertible Neural Networks: Architecture Tradeoffs and Customization Across Domains
authors: Jingyi Yuan, Muhao Guo, Yang Weng
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Invertible neural networks (INNs) have gained attention for solving inverse problems where learning a bijective mapping is essential. However, despite their popularity, little guidance exists on how to select, adapt, or deploy different INN architectures, such as i-ResNet, NICE, or DipDNN, across diverse application domains. In this paper, we provide a unified theoretical and empirical analysis of two major INN families: iterative-contraction networks (e.g., i-ResNet) and triangular coupling networks (e.g., NICE, DipDNN). We show that these models exhibit fundamental tradeoffs in invertibility, expressivity, and compatibility with domain constraints. For example, contractive models fail to recover mappings with large gains or sign flips, whereas triangular schemes inherit rigid channel splits that can restrict feature richness. Building on these insights, we introduce a general-purpose customization framework. It decouples physics-informed constraints from measurement-driven inversion through a parallel INN design. We benchmark representative INNs on cross-domain tasks: edge monitoring, PDE dynamics, and robotic control. Our findings offer first comprehensive design guideline for choosing and adapting INN architectures based on problem structure, shedding light on when and how invertibility-based bidirectional inferences can be reliably applied in practice.

title: Hierarchically Disentangled Recurrent Network for Factorizing System Dynamics of Multi-scale Systems: An application on Hydrological Systems
authors: Rahul Ghosh, Arvind Renganthan, Zachary McEachran, Kelly Lindsay, Michael Steinbach, John Nieber, Christopher Duffy, Vipin Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: We present a framework for modeling multi-scale processes, and study its performance in the context of stream-flow forecasting in hydrology. Specifically, we propose a novel hierarchical recurrent neural architecture that factorizes the system dynamics at multiple temporal scales and captures their interactions. This framework consists of an inverse and a forward model. The inverse model is used to empirically resolve the system's temporal modes from data (physical model simulations, observed data, or a combination of them from the past), and these states are then used in the forward model to predict streamflow. Experiments on several catchments from the National Weather Service North Central River Forecast Center show that FHNN outperforms standard baselines, including physics-based models and transformer-based approaches. The model demonstrates particular effectiveness in catchments with low runoff ratios and colder climates. We further validate FHNN on the CAMELS (Catchment Attributes and MEteorology for Large-sample Studies), which is a widely used continental-scale hydrology benchmark dataset, confirming consistent performance improvements for 1–7 day streamflow forecasts across diverse hydrological conditions. Additionally, we show that FHNN can maintain accuracy even with limited training data through effective pre-training strategies and training global models.

title: PiGLeT: Probabilistic Message Passing for Semi-Supervised Link Sign Prediction
authors: Ka Hyun Park, Junghun Kim, Jinhong Jung, U Kang
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: How can we accurately predict the signs of unseen links in partially observed signed graphs? Signed graphs are widely used to represent complex relationships in areas such as social and biological networks. Although prior methods enhance representation learning by extending Graph Neural Networks with social theories, they rely on the unrealistic assumption that all link signs are known. In practice, however, link signs are often only partially labeled due to the high cost or difficulty of obtaining ground-truth annotations. For example, in the Bitcoin transaction network, only a subset of interactions can be labeled as trusted or untrusted, while many remain unlabeled. In this work, we propose PIGLET (PROBABILISTIC MESSAGE PASSING FOR SEMI-SUPERVISED LINK SIGN PREDICTION), a novel approach for accurate link sign prediction on signed graphs with partially observed sign labels. The main idea is to probabilistically interpret unlabeled links as both positive and negative based on a soft-labeling strategy, allowing information to be propagated through both types of edges. To mitigate the effect of uncertain predictions, we use confidence-based weights to downplay unreliable edges. PIGLET balances the importance of edges with relation-aware attention scores as well. PIGLET establishes a theoretical connection between the ExpectationMaximization (EM) algorithm and its iterative refinement of node embeddings and soft labels. Extensive experiments show that PIGLET consistently outperforms existing methods on realworld datasets under semi-supervised setting.

title: BioDL-BreastNet: A Bio-Inspired Deep Learning Framework for Breast Cancer Diagnosis and Risk Stratification
authors: Kranti Kumar Dewangan, Jyoti Singh
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Early and accurate diagnosis of breast cancer and effective risk stratification remain major challenges in oncology and medical imaging. This study presents a novel deep-learning framework whose architecture and hyperparameters are optimized via bio-inspired meta-heuristics (e.g., Genetic Algorithms, Particle Swarm Optimization) to enhance diagnostic performance and model generalizability. Using benchmark histopathological and imaging datasets, the proposed optimized architecture achieved an accuracy of 99.60% on the BACH dataset and 99.10% on a combined validation set (including BreakHis), outperforming state-of-the-art deep-learning baselines. In a parallel experiment focused on risk-stratification (benign vs malignant vs high-risk progression), the metaheuristic guided model achieved a sensitivity of 96.57%, specificity of 96.49%, precision of 96.57% and F1-score of 96.50%. The architecture integrates feature-selection by PSO, hyper-parameter search via GA, and depth-width optimization of convolutional blocks, yielding a 25% reduction in parameter count and 15% faster inference time compared to manually-tuned networks. Explainable AI modules (saliency maps, layer-wise relevance propagation) were incorporated to ensure transparency for clinical adoption. We discuss methodological design, optimization workflow, evaluation metrics, and risk-stratification logic alongside clinical implications and potential deployment scenarios. The findings demonstrate that bio-inspired optimization of deep-learning systems can significantly advance breast-cancer diagnostic accuracy, robustness and trustworthiness — paving the way for AI-driven clinical decision-support in oncologic screening and prognosis.

title: On the Relationship Between Populated Regions and Adversarial Robustness in Deep Neural Networks
authors: Seongjin Park, Haedong Jeong, Tair Djanibekov, Giyoung Jeon, Jinseok Seol, Jaesik Choi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: In general, deep neural networks (DNNs) are evaluated by the generalization performance measured on unseen data excluded from the training phase. Along with the development of DNNs, the generalization performance converges to the state-of-the-art performances and it becomes difficult to evaluate DNNs solely based on this metric. The robustness against adversarial attack has been used as an additional metric to evaluate DNNs by measuring their vulnerability. However, few studies have been performed to analyze the adversarial robustness in terms of the geometry in DNNs. In this work, we perform an empirical study to analyze the internal properties of DNNs that affect model robustness under adversarial attacks. In particular, we propose the novel concept of the populated region set (PRS), where training samples are actually populated, to represent the internal properties of DNNs in a practical setting. From systematic experiments with the proposed concept, we provide empirical evidence to validate that a low PRS ratio has a strong relationship with the adversarial robustness of DNNs. We also devise a PRS regularizer leveraging the characteristics of PRS to improve the adversarial robustness without adversarial training.

title: Evaluating Flow in Open Source Software
authors: Disha Suresh, Kevin Gary
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Open source projects often incorporate similar principles and practices as Agile and Lean software development projects. Contrary to traditional organizations, the environment in which these projects function has an impact on process-related elements like the flow of work and value. Process metrics are typically employed during Agile Software Engineering projects as a means of providing meaningful feedback. This paper presents an exploratory study investigating whether these metrics can be beneficial to open source communities This knowledge will assist in educating open source ecosystems about the applications of agile software engineering and its variations in open source projects. We analyzed the GitHub project boards of 5 mature and large-scale open source projects, applying multiple agile/lean process metrics to explore whether interesting patterns emerged. The methodology used to collect these metrics and relevant findings are discussed in this paper. The original contribution of this research is to determine whether agile/lean process metrics are helpful in open source, as well as the opportunities and obstacles that may arise when applying agile and lean principles to open source software development.

title: Interaction-Centric Cybersecurity Risks in LLM-Powered Dialogue Systems
authors: Pratyush Jena, Samira Zad, Rehan Akbar
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have forever transformed human-AI interaction by enabling natural dialogue, reasoning, and autonomous task execution. Yet this interactioncentric paradigm introduces cybersecurity risks that traditional AI security frameworks fail to capture. This paper presents a conceptual framework and synthesis derived from a review (2015-2025) of security vulnerabilities in LLM-powered dialogue systems. We consolidate over forty studies spanning adversarial NLP, prompt injection, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) poisoning, backdoors, and human trust exploitation. Although previous works have provided a list of isolated threats or generic LLM vulnerabilities, very few of them have looked at how interactive dialogue broadens the attack surface. Our study proposes a unified interaction-chain taxonomy distinguishing risks at input, processing, and output stages, and maps representative attacks and defenses accordingly. Drawing insights from classical adversarial literature and recent LLM surveys, we derive a layered defense pipeline integrating prompt sanitization, retrieval provenance tracking, backdoor detection, and output auditing. The resulting framework reframes cybersecurity in dialogue systems as an interactional problem bridging AI safety, NLP security, and human-computer interaction, providing a foundation for standardizing evaluation, developing cross-model benchmarks, and guiding the secure deployment of LLM agents.

title: LLM-Augmented Anomaly Detection with Isolation Forest and SHAP in Snowflake
authors: Vilas M V, Ramani Selvanambi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: In modern data-driven systems, detecting anomalies in server logs is vital for ensuring operational stability and security. This work presents a fully cloud-native anomaly detection and explainability pipeline built entirely within the Snowflake data platform. Starting from publicly available server log datasets, we preprocess and ingest the data using staged bulk insertions for efficient loading. Leveraging Snowflake's native Python execution, we engineer features and train an Isolation Forest model for unsupervised anomaly detection, accurately flagging abnormal server behaviors. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values interpret these anomalies by identifying key contributing features and clarifying whether the anomaly is driven by unusually high or low values. Finally, Snowflake Cortex's LLM capabilities are employed to auto-generate concise, human-readable insights, enabling seamless integration into analytical dashboards. This pipeline demonstrates a scalable, explainable, and fully SQL-integrated approach to anomaly detection—without relying on any external compute infrastructure.

title: Cognitive-Ethical Decision Intelligence for SMEs in VUCA Markets Using Human-in-the-Loop XAI
authors: Tej Narayan Gangwar, Jawad Ashraf, Archie Singh Khuman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: SMEs in the telecommunications sector operate in a volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous (VUCA) environment where opaque artificial intelligence (AI) systems often exacerbate existing cognitive and ethical problems in decision making. This study proposes an integrated, fully understandable AI framework for enhancing cognitive and ethical decision-making in SMEs. The research follows a pragmatic and conceptual methodology, synthesizing multidisciplinary literature and management insights to develop three interconnected models: EIARA (Emotional-Intuitive-Analytical-Reflection-Awareness), which captures the cognitive states of the human brain when interpreting AI outputs; TIER (Trustworthiness-Interpretability-Ethics-Reliability), which defines principles at the level of the system; and DMQS (Decision Making Quality Score). Together, these frameworks create a cognitive-ethical decision-making loop that promotes transparency, accountability, and adaptive resilience in AI-supported organizations. The paper outlines the theoretical and management implications and provides the basis for future empirical validation and the development of Decision Intelligence as a Service (DIaaS) solutions tailored to the VUCA market agility of SMEs.

title: Lily: A Perfume Conversational Recommender System Fine-Tuned GPT-OSS-20B with Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Zahra Amiera Putri Sailendra, Z. K. A. Baizal
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Consumers often struggle to select perfumes that match their preferences. This usually happens because scent descriptions are often subjective and difficult to interpret. To address this, we present Lily, a perfume conversational recommender system. This system combines a fine-tuned Large Language Model (LLM), GPT-OSS-20B, with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Instead of relying on fixed questionnaires, Lily processes natural language descriptions provided by users about their fragrance preferences. The system then generates perfume recommendations with an easy-to-understand explanation. User inputs are encoded into vector embeddings using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model to retrieve semantically relevant candidates, which are then passed to the fine-tuned LLM to generate responses. Three models were tested: RAG; fine-tuned GPT-OSS-20B, and a combined model, Lily. Lily’s model performs best on semantic similarity, achieving a BERTScore F1 of 0.8503 and a cosine similarity of 0.5670, while the fine-tuned model produces the most diverse responses with a Distinct-1 score of 0.8274. These results suggest that combining domain-specific fine-tuning with retrieval can improve contextual alignment with reference recommendations and support more varied responses, providing a promising direction for LLM-based conversational recommender systems (CRS) in subjective domains such as perfume.

title: LightGBM Models for Multiclass Stage Classification of Age-Related Macular Degeneration
authors: K. Deepa, Sindhuri Suseela Mantena, S. Sasikumar, Sujatha T, Monisha R, Syed A Belal Mohamed
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a degenerative retinal condition and a primary cause of permanent visual impairment in the elderly. The precise categorisation of AMD stages is essential for accurate screening, prompt intervention, and tracking of disease progression. This paper introduces a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) model for the multiclass classification of AMD stages using the publicly accessible AMDP dataset from Kaggle. The collection comprises retinal images categorized into various disease stages, from normal to severe AMD. The images were pre-processed and converted into a structured dataset appropriate for model training. LightGBM was chosen for its efficiency, scalability, and robust performance in multiclass classification problems. The model underwent training and evaluation using a stratified train-test split, guaranteeing balanced representation throughout all stages of AMD. Experimental findings indicate that the proposed LightGBM methodology achieved an overall classification accuracy of 99.96%, proficiently differentiating among normal, early, intermediate, and advanced stages of AMD. These findings validate the efficacy of gradient boosting–based techniques as a dependable and interpretable approach for classifying stages of AMD. The research highlights the practicality of using LightGBM in clinical decision-support systems for extensive AMD screening and diagnosis.

title: Hybrid Applications of Broadband Over Power Line Communication (BPLC) and Wireless 5.5G in MV Substation Power Communication Fiber Network
authors: Oscar Chan, Andrew Hoi
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: This paper proposes an innovative solution that leverages Broadband over Power Line Communication (BPLC) integrated with emerging wireless 5.5G technology to overcome the limitations of incomplete fiber ring networks. In scenarios where fiber connectivity is unavailable at MV substations, BPLC serves as an effective bridge for data transmission between power equipment, offering a cost-effective alternative. BPLC not only maximizes communication bandwidth and speed by fully utilizing electromagnetic frequencies but also presents unique advantages when used exclusively in a network ring configuration. For long-distance communication where fiber is not available at MV substations, integrating wireless 5.5G technology with BPLC improves the communication network's reliability and coverage, all while maintaining low costs. This integrated method supports the ongoing utilization of fiber networks where they are accessible, thus delivering a versatile and durable solution for power communication networks in urban environments.

title: Performance Analysis of Next-Generation ISAC Assisted Cooperative ED Sensing with Max Select Logic for Enhanced Detection in CR—VANET
authors: A M Balamurugan, R Muniappan, G D Vignesh, N Mohammed Sulaiman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: This paper presents a framework of Cooperative Spectrum Sensing (CSS) based energy detection (ED) sensing with Max select logic enhanced with Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) to improve detection performance in Cognitive Radio Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (CR-VANETS). Traditional CSS based on ED often suffers from reduced accuracy under low SNR and mobility conditions due to channel fading and increased sensing delays. The proposed framework of CSS ED with ISAC integrates sensing and communication into a unified process, enabling simultaneous data exchange and detection among Secondary Users (SUs). The simulation results demonstrate a significant increase in average probability of detection (λd), substantial reduction in sensing time and improved robustness against mobility induced Doppler shift and fading effects compared to conventional technique without ISAC. This integration enhances spectrum utilization efficiency and decision reliability, making it suitable for 6G VANETs under dynamic environment. Thus, this work can be considered as a scalable and high-performance spectrum sensing solution, with future enhancements planned for adaptive resource allocation and energy efficiency.

title: DAMA: A Dual Alignment Framework for Enhanced LLM-Powered Recommendations
authors: Qingwen Zeng, Lining Chen, Jushang Qiu, Fangchen Liu, Huaming Chen, Ling Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Recommender systems play a pivotal role in personalized content delivery but still face significant challenges in sparse interaction scenarios such as cold start conditions. Recent works have explored the integration of large language models (LLMs) into recommendation tasks. It has shown early promise but also reveals some limitations, such as hallucinations and overconfident outputs due to a lack of targeted modeling process. This paper introduces DAMA, a novel framework for Dual Alignment for Motivation-Aware recommendation. DAMA is the first framework to systematically incorporate LLM alignment into recommender system modeling. It employs a dual alignment mechanism that jointly optimizes semantic representations from two dimensions: user motivation modeling and product attribute modeling. By leveraging natural language feedback, DAMA facilitates a structured and interpretable alignment process. Extensive experimental results on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate that DAMA significantly improves recommendation performance, especially in cold start scenarios. We anticipate that DAMA will pioneer a new alignment-centric and semantically grounded paradigm for recommender systems.

title: Digital Immersion Based Prototype Model Of Radial Engine
authors: Y.V. Siva Reddy, Venkatesh. G, Bramhanadna Reddy Teegala, Pradeep Kumar Yadav Allagadda, Harinatha Reddy Chennam, N. Ravi Sankara Reddy
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: Virtual Reality(VR) is a powerful and in teractive technology that will change our lives in contrast to everyone. Virtual reality, also known as immersive multimedia, is a powerful and interactive technology that will change our lives. This property of VR is used to develop radial engines and experience it by an individual user for better understanding as well as developing prototypes and new engine solutions is made easier that eliminates time consuming complications in practical designs and huge cost involvement. This paper discuss about digital immersion of radial engines through VR technology and the details are presented.

title: Realization of Multipath Routing with Secret Sharing Scheme in MIMO Ad Hoc Network Through AODV Conjugated STBC for Disaster Management
authors: Soma Boral, Swagatam Biswas, Soumik Podder, Arun Kumar Mondal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Computing, Astronomy and Renewable Energy (AICARE)
abstract: Efficient Disaster control and management become realistic only due to the boons of Information Technology. The emergence of Mobile Area Network (MANET) has the potential to monitor the physical and environmental conditions through its sensors or nodes. The information gathered on such conditions are to be protected in an efficient way. In the present work, Space Time Coding (STBC) conjugated AODV protocol is proposed to avoid data loss due to high mobility induced network failure. This approach uses multipath routing to amplify the diversity gain and coding gain after the utilization of three-dimensional diversity of MIMO systems. In order to overcome the challenging channel conditions, we analyze the MIMO-Ad Hoc network activity by combining the AODV (Ad hoc On-Demand Distance Vector) impregnated Secret Sharing Scheme (SSS) with STBC. With varying SNR values, this system’s primary goal is to increase network coverage and throughput while conserving energy. Herein, performance analysis of AODV-STBC-SSS Scheme’s in MIMO based communication framework is thoroughly discussed. The bit error rate (BER), packet delivery ratio (PDR) and routing stability are evaluated and it is recorded that BER becomes -3, PDR turns out to be 0.8 implying all data packets were received with minimum loss as supported by BER. The data transmission portfolio was achieved by integrating MIMO, AODV, and STBC with different modulation techniques such as BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM and 64-QAM.

title: Machine Learning–Based Cryptojacking Detection using System and Network Metrics
authors: Manisha H S, N G Shirisha, Nidhi S S, Kavyashree S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Cryptojacking is a malicious activity where attackers exploit computational resources of a victim's system to mine cryptocurrency without authorization, leading to degraded performance, high energy consumption, and financial loss. Traditional malware detection techniques often fail to identify cryptojacking because malicious processes mimic normal system behavior. This paper presents a machine learning–based approach for detecting cryptojacking using system and network performance metrics. We employ Support Vector Machine (SVM) for supervised classification and Isolation Forest for unsupervised anomaly detection. The Cryptojacking Attack Timeseries Dataset (Kaggle), which contains CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network usage metrics under normal and cryptojacking scenarios, is used for experimentation. Results show that the proposed models achieve high detection accuracy, and feature explainability using SHAP identifies CPU, memory, and network metrics as key indicators of cryptojacking behavior.

title: Performance Analysis of YOLOv8–YOLOv12 for Edge-Based Strawberry Ripeness Detection
authors: Marcelo Tsuguio Okano, William Aparecido C. Lopes, Sergio Miele Ruggero, Luana dos Santos Cordeiro
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: This study presents a comprehensive benchmark of the most recent YOLO object detection architectures, from YOLOv8 to YOLOv12, applied to the binary visual classification of ripe and unripe strawberries, a relevant task in precision agriculture and automated post-harvest processing. Using a controlled, high-resolution dataset of 1,364 labeled images and a standardized training pipeline, we evaluate lightweight variants of each model (nano, tiny, and small), focusing on performance indicators essential for real-time edge deployment, including precision, recall, F1-score, mAP{@}0.5, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m A P} {@} 0.5: 0.95$</tex>, and per-image inference time. Results show that the YOLOv9-tiny model achieves the best overall performance, with perfect recall (1.0000), an F1-score of 0.9550, and the highest <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAP} {@} 0.5: 0.95(0.9613)$</tex>, outperforming both earlier and more recent architectures. YOLOv12 variants demonstrate strong generalization and high sensitivity, but with slightly lower precision, indicating a higher tendency for false positives, a critical aspect in automated sorting systems. The study also reveals that newer versions do not necessarily surpass older ones across all metrics, emphasizing that architectural complexity alone does not guarantee superior performance. The main contribution of this work is a unified and fair comparison of five generations of YOLO models under the same hardware, dataset, and hyperparameter conditions, providing new insights into how architectural innovations, such as PGI, NMS-free design, enhanced attention mechanisms, and lightweight transformer components, affect performance in agricultural classification tasks. These findings offer practical guidance for selecting lightweight models for edge-based fruit quality assessment and highlight research opportunities to integrate multispectral sensors, domain adaptation techniques, and real-world evaluations into future studies.

title: Gastrointestinal Disease Recognition Using Advanced Machine Learning Models
authors: Zorawar Jaiswal, Abhishek Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: The Gastrointestinal tract (GI tract) often known as the digestive system is an organ system that is highly prone to cancers, polyps, and other diseases. Examination of this tract is not so straightforward and relies heavily on endoscopy. The real-time feed captured via endoscopy can last for hours and may have many pathological findings and anatomical landmarks. This research presents a deep learning based solution to classify images of the GI tract to highlight the endoscope reaching a landmark or a pathological region of interest. The experiments compare the prior benchmarks with CNN-based architectures (EfficientNet L2 and EfficientNet B7) and Transformer-based architecture (Hiera H). The experiment achieves an accuracy of 93.7%, which is an improvement upon the existing benchmarks. The research is important as it focuses on achieving high accuracy on a limited 2K size training set without overfitting.

title: Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Models for Intrusion Detection in Cybersecurity
authors: Ahmet Ercan Topcu, Melisa Avci, Nefise Buse Uzun
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: Network-based vulnerabilities have emerged as a significant concern for information security in today's digital environment. To lessen the impact of such risks, early detection is essential. Through network traffic monitoring, this study examines the effectiveness of various supervised machine learning algorithms in detecting cyberattacks. Normalizing data, addressing missing values, and encoding categorical variables were crucial preparatory steps. We built a comparison model using seven machine learning classification algorithms, and the models were assessed using performance criteria such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and other characteristics. To evaluate computing efficiency, training and inference times were also noted. With the highest accuracy of 92.23%, Random Forest (RF) outperformed the other examined models in terms of overall performance. Naive Bayes (NB) and Gradient Boosting (GB) demonstrated strong performance, whereas KNN and LR achieved lower accuracy. The findings demonstrate how RF strikes a balance between low error rates and excellent predictive performance, making it a suitable option for real-time intrusion detection systems. Our work showed that machine learning algorithms can effectively detect intrusions.

title: Preliminary Review: Building AI Agents with LLMs for Autonomous Interpretation of Equipment Sensor Data
authors: Anderson Carvalho, Fiona Boyle, John Daly, Joseph Walsh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: The increasing deployment of sensor-driven automation in industrial equipment has led in the collecting of huge volumes of operational data for monitoring and maintenance purposes. However, obtaining usable insights from this data remains difficult due to its complexity and unstructured nature. This study examines how recent cutting-edge research tackles the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in the construction of AI-powered agents capable of autonomously reading telemetry data and maintenance records to facilitate predictive maintenance in industrial systems. The proposed architecture to be built along these series of 5 articles, shall combine LLMs with structured query techniques and domain-specific knowledge embeddings, allowing the AI agent to detect abnormalities, forecast failures, and produce technician-friendly reports. The AI agent improves decision-making processes by utilizing natural language understanding (NLU), pattern recognition, and real-time data synthesis, allowing for preventative maintenance and better equipment performance. We also investigate technical issues like data pretreatment, knowledge representation, and the alignment of LLM reasoning with domain-specific restrictions.
