With regard to the assessment of substations and substation equipment, it has been reported that greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions over the entire life cycle are calculated as carbon dioxide (+) equivalents (carbon footprint: CFP). This paper describes the results of a case study focusing on the upgrading construction of the 500 kV substation, including gas insulated switchgear (GIS) expansion and transformer installation. The CFP was estimated for the construction stage and the procurement stage, and effective measures to reduce CFP were identified and evaluated for their effectiveness. The case study has shown that there is potential to quantitatively assess CFPs, identify sources of emissions and quantify the effectiveness of reduction measures. In addition, this paper also presents initiatives to reduce CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions during the O&M stage.

title: “Development of Diagnosis Algorithm for Early Detection of Cervical Uterine Cancer”
authors: Borse Manjusha S, Mukesh Yadav, Rajeh Nagar
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: One of the most common gynecological diseases affecting women worldwide is cervical cancer. It is crucial to develop precise and non-invasive symptomatic procedures because early detection and treatment of cervical cancer can essentially progress lasting outcomes. In this study, we present a new method for locating cervical cancer using image processing techniques. Our suggested approach consists of dividing the cervical region using a modified watershed computation after preprocessing the cervical cytology images to improve differentiation and eliminate noise. In order to extract pertinent information, the segmented area is then examined using various highlights, including surface, morphology, and color. To predict the presence or absence of cervical cancer, the extracted highlights are fed into a CNN, VGG19, and MobileNet classifier. Our test results on a publicly available dataset demonstrate the feasibility of our suggested approach, achieving 95.83% accuracy, 97.5% affectability, and 93.75% specificity. Our suggested approach has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and consistency of cervical cancer detection, leading to earlier detection and better silent outcomes

title: Digital Monitoring for Agroecology
authors: Pascal Francois Faye, Bassirou Diene, Daba Dieng, Moussa Déthié Sarr, Mbaye Sene, Jeanne Ana Awa Faye, Mariane Senghor
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: The agricultural environment is facing climate change and severe erosion of arable land. Water management in daily operations is crucial, especially in Senegal. In addition, without disregarding the type of soil and amendment, the water quality and the water quantity define the types of agriculture we can do. In this work, we measured with our network sensors the water quality and the soil parameters in order to identify the irrigation frequency that can cause soil erosion and can avoid an optimal development of crops. Further, we give a set of results for decision-making in an irrigation scheme and soil works. After analysing the one month data from our sensors network, we proposed 2 types of amendments that contribute to soil health and limit the appearance of sedimentation, to play a role in delaying capillary upwelling, which can cause stress on crops. In addition, we highlight a set of relationships between the main parameters that define crop cultivation abilities like pH (hydrogen potential), EC (electrical conductivity), N (nitrogen), P (phosphorus), K (potassium), and Fertility.

title: Suitability and Investment Map of Variable Renewable Energy Power Plants in Thailand
authors: Naraset Sinsang, Newin Phongkasemwiwat
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: This study addresses the challenge of Integra variable renewable energy (VRE) into Thailand's utilities p system by developing a spatial framework for anticipating development of grid-scale solar and wind power plants. A based multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) is employ assess land suitability across Thailand using ten environ and socio-economic criteria. The resulting suitability in then used within an optimization model to project pot investment locations, assuming developers prioritize are higher suitability, while ensuring that each location is all to either wind or solar development to avoid overlap findings reveal the spatial distribution of potential wine solar energy development, indicating higher suitability for energy deployment across most regions, while wind development is more localized. This research provides v insights and spatially explicit maps for strategic generating transmission planning, aiding in the effective integration into Thailand's energy mix.

title: Fuel Cell–Battery Hybrid Power System Integration for BLDC Motor-driven Electric Vehicle
authors: Mohammed A. AlAqil, Siva Agora Sakthivel Murugan, C Karthick, A Nandhakumar, M Janarthanan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: The growing demand for efficient and sustainable transportation systems has intensified research into hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs). The presented work offers the design and analysis of a fuel cell–battery hybrid power system for electric vehicles powered by brushless DC motors. A proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) is proposed as the primary energy source due to its higher power density and extended range capability, while a Lithium-ion battery supplements transient power requirements and stores energy recovered during regenerative braking. The system model is developed in MATLAB and simulated under various operating modes by implementing a fuzzy logic controller to regulate motor speed and manage power flow during various drive cycles. Simulation results demonstrate that the PEMFC maintains stable power output under all conditions, with surplus energy used to charge the battery. During hill-climbing conditions (i.e., high-load), the battery discharges to satisfy instantaneous power demands, resulting in increased torque and stator current to counteract gravitational effects. During deceleration, regenerative braking efficiently recovers kinetic energy and stores it in the battery, enhancing overall system efficiency.

title: SUSGEN-GPT: Automating ESG Reporting and Financial Analysis Using NLP
authors: Ravi Teja Gundimeda
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The increasing integration of Environmental, So-cial, and Governance (ESG) factors into financial decision-making has created a demand for advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) tools that can automate ESG reporting and financial analysis. Despite advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), a significant gap remains in open-source models capable of effectively handling both financial data and ESG report generation. This paper introduces SUSGEN-30K, a high-quality, category-balanced dataset covering seven financial NLP tasks and sustainability report generation, along with TCFD-BENCH, a benchmarking framework designed to improve ESG reporting evaluation. Leveraging a data-centric approach, we develop SUSGEN-GPT, a specialized NLP model suite trained on our curated dataset, achieving state-of-the-art performance in ESG report generation and financial document analysis. By automating key aspects of financial sustainability reporting, risk assessment, and compliance monitoring, SUSGEN-GPT enhances decision-making while reducing manual effort, fostering contin-uous innovation in financial automation and ESG research.

title: AI Applications in Clinical Diagnosis: Machine Learning with Wavelet Based Features for Epileptic Seizure Prediction
authors: Sanagavarapu Sunitha, Umadevi Ramamoorthy
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Epileptic seizures are irregular neurological episodes which have great influence on patient safety, autonomy, and quality of life. With the increased accessibility of electroencephalogram (EEG) data, machine learning (ML) becomes more relevant in seizure prediction, with their capacity to detect preictal patterns in real-time becoming useful in a regulated clinical intervention and personalized care. The review brings together the existing methodologies including the conventional ML classifiers, with wavelet-based feature engineering mechanisms applicable to non-stationary and complex EEG patterns. The classical ML models include Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forests and Logistic Regression which are based on manually constructed temporal, spectral features and show good results in controlled conditions but frequently have issues with patient-to-patient generalization. Although significant advances have been made, there are still problems, such as variability of the datasets, low interpretability, high false-alarm rates, and lack of validation on continuous real-world EEG recordings. In general, a combination of ML, and wavelet-based solutions has a high potential of creating efficient and practical seizure prediction and personalized epilepsy management systems.

title: Exploiting Hardware Nonidealities for Robust Low-Precision Ensembles on ACIM
authors: Donghyuck Lee, Tae-Hyeon Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Device variation is a fundamental challenge for analog compute-in-memory (ACIM) systems. Instead of treating it as an error, we strategically exploit it as a source of computational diversity. We harness this natural process-induced variation by programming two low-precision deep neural network (DNN) replicas onto the same ACIM chip. A soft-voting mechanism then aggregates their outputs for a more robust prediction. To quantify the system-level trade-offs, we extend the NeuroSim V1.4 framework to co-evaluate accuracy, area, latency, and energy. On VGG-8/CIFAR-10, as the conductance variation <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sigma $</tex-math> </inline-formula> increases from 0.05 to 0.25, the accuracy gain of the 4-bit ensemble over its 4-bit single-model (SM) counterpart grows from 0.46 to 3.05 percentage points (pp). Notably, at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sigma =0.05$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, the ensemble even surpasses the zero-variation baseline by 0.303 pp. Compared to an 8-bit SM baseline, the 4-bit ensemble becomes comparable to or surpasses it for <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sigma \ge 0.10$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, achieving up to 1.28 pp at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sigma =0.25$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Hardware evaluation using an SRAM-based ACIM architecture shows that the two-replica ensemble achieves a 9.0% smaller chip area than the 8-bit baseline, at the cost of 30% higher latency and 2.1% higher energy. We further observe consistent accuracy–overhead trends for RRAM- and FeFET-based ACIM under their respective nonideality characteristics.

title: Consistent Model Selection in a Collection of Stochastic Block Models
authors: Lucie Arts
abstract: We introduce the penalized Krichevsky-Trofimov (KT) estimator as a convergent method for estimating the number of nodes clusters when observing multiple networks within both multi-layer and dynamic Stochastic Block Models. We establish the consistency of the KT estimator, showing that it converges to the correct number of clusters in both types of models when the number of nodes in the networks increases. Our estimator does not require a known upper bound on this number to be consistent. Furthermore, we show that these consistency results hold in both dense and sparse regimes, making the penalized KT estimator robust across various network configurations. We illustrate its performance on synthetic datasets.

title: Explainable Molecular Property Prediction: Aligning Chemical Concepts with Predictions via Language Models_supp1-3664098.pdf
authors: Wanyu Lin
abstract: Providing explainable molecular property predictions is critical for many scientific domains, such as drug discovery and material science. Though transformer-based language models have shown great potential in accurate molecular property prediction, they neither provide chemically meaningful explanations nor faithfully reveal the molecular structure-property relationships. In this work, we develop a framework for explainable molecular property prediction based on language models, dubbed as Lamole, which can provide chemical concepts-aligned explanations. We take a string-based molecular representation — Group SELFIES — as input tokens to pre-train and fine-tune our Lamole, as it provides chemically meaningful semantics. By disentangling the information flows of Lamole, we propose considering both self-attention weights and gradients for better quantification of each chemically meaningful substructure's impact on the model's output. To make the explanations more faithful to the structure-property relationship, we then carefully craft a marginal loss to explicitly optimize the explanations to align with the chemists' annotations. We bridge the manifold hypothesis with the elaborated marginal loss to prove that the loss can align the explanations with the tangent space of the data manifold, leading to concept-aligned explanations. Experimental results over eight datasets demonstrate Lamole can achieve comparable prediction accuracy and boost the explanation accuracy by up to $14.3\%$, being the state-of-the-art in explainable molecular property prediction. To further illustrate the actionable utility of the explanations derived from Lamole, we integrated the framework with an evolutionary algorithm. This integration established an interpretable optimization pipeline for molecular editing, demonstrating that Lamole functions beyond simple post-hoc analysis but serves as a practical guide for molecule discovery. The code is available at the provided link: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Lamole-7482

title: Input-Output Variation Driven Gain Observer for Ultra-Local-Model-Based Predictive Control of PMSMs without Dependence on Electrical Parameters
authors: Xing Liu, Hui Yang, Shun Cai, Pedram Asef, Ruiqi Xu, Feng Yu, Heyun Lin, Shuhua Fang, Shuilian Xue, Feng Xie
abstract: Model-free predictive control (MFPC) based on ultra-local model (ULM) is characterized by enhanced robustness to parameter mismatches. However, the selection of control gains, actually, still relies on the parameters, and the gain deviation degrades the control performance. To solve this issue, this paper proposes an improved MFPC with a novel input-output-variation driven gain observer. With a conceptual ULM-based MFPC, the impacts of gain deviation on the control performance and system stability are analyzed in detail. Then, the proposed method is expatiated, and in particular the stability of the proposed gain observer is proven based on the Lyapunov stability theorem. Meanwhile, the design guideline of feedback coefficients of the proposed observer is also presented. Based on the theoretical analyses, a finite-control-set model free predictive current control (FCS-MFPCC) method with the suppression of gain deviation is designed and applied to a permanent magnet synchronous machine (PMSM) drive system. The digital delay compensation and the discriminator of zero voltage vectors are considered for performance improvement and inverter switching frequency reduction, respectively. Finally, some critical experiments are conducted based on a PMSM test rig to verify the proposed gain observer and FCS MFPCC method. In particular, different initial values of control gains are set, indicating that the proposed FCS-MFPCC method does not require any prior knowledge of electrical parameters of the PMSM under test.

title: VoltSiren: Exploiting Power Supply Vulnerabilities to Control IoT Devices
authors: Kai Wang, Shilin Xiao, Pengzhan Chen, Xiaoyu Ji, Chen Yan, Ruochen Zhou, Kaixiang Zhang, Wenyuan Xu
abstract: This paper analyzes the security of Internet of Things (IoT) devices from the perspective of sensing, actuating, and communicating. Particularly, we discover a vulnerability in power supply modules and propose VoltSiren attacks. To launch a VoltSiren attack, attackers may compromise the power source and inject malicious signals through the power supply module, which is indispensable in most devices. Consequently, VoltSiren attacks can cause sensor measurements irrelevant to reality, maneuver actuators in a way disregarding the desired command, or disrupt communications. To understand VoltSiren, we systematically analyze the underlying principle of power supply signals affecting the electronic components, which are building blocks to constitute the sensors, actuators, or communication modules. Based on these findings, we implement and validate VoltSiren on off-the-shelf products: six sensors, three actuators, and two communication modules, which are used in applications ranging from automobile braking systems, industrial process control to robotic arms. The root cause of this vulnerability lies in the common belief that noises from the power line are unintentional, and our work aims to call for attention to enhancing the security of power supply modules and adding countermeasures to mitigate the attacks.

title: Multi-modal Large Language Models for Perception in Autonomous Driving: Architecture, Taxonomy, and Challenges
authors: Ying Jing, Xinyu Zhang, Mo Zhou, Yuchuan Ji, Yanchao Ding, Jialun Yin, Ruizhi Jia, Yijin Xiong, Kun Zhao, Jun Yang, Jun Li, Huaping Liu
abstract: Autonomous vehicles rely on continuous environmental perception to assess obstacle distribution to ensure safe driving. However, current perception technologies face substantial challenges, particularly under adverse weather conditions and when encountering long-tail scenarios. With the advent of transformer-based attention mechanisms, large language models (LLMs), exemplified by GPT, have exhibited emergent intelligence, offering new possibilities for achieving high-performance perception. This technological advancement has led to the development of multi-modal large language models (MLLMs), which incorporate multi-modal encoders. These models enable a single LLM to process multi-source data while performing advanced understanding and reasoning tasks, enhancing complex environmental perception capabilities. Despite significant progress in MLLMs, there remains a notable gap in systematic research on their optimal application to perception tasks. Therefore, this paper presents a comprehensive survey of recent advancements in MLLM-based perception. First, we introduce the mainstream vision–language perception tasks, widely adopted evaluation metrics, and existing language-enhanced autonomous driving datasets. Next, we outline the general architectural design principles of MLLMs. Subsequently, we provide a taxonomy and indepth analysis of current MLLMs, focusing on three dimensions: input modality, alignment technique, and scene representation, elucidating their underlying implementation paradigms. Finally, we summarize the key challenges and emerging research directions in MLLM-driven perception. This survey aims to facilitate further progress in MLLMs by synthesizing these insights.

title: mTIPs: Multimodal Time-Series Imaging and Prediction System for Early Differentiation of Retinal Organoids
authors: Yi Wang, Yilin Qiao, Junao Song, Yuan-zhi Liu, Min Ye, Jianbo Mao, Ronald X. Xu, Mingzhai Sun
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Retinal organoids (ROs) hold immense promise for disease modeling and therapy, but their production is hindered by developmental heterogeneity, necessitating robust characterization and early prediction. However, the limited application of current methods to human stem cell–derived organoids reduces clinical relevance; the omission of 3D multimodal imaging leads to incomplete morphological assessment; and the lack of time-series analysis diminishes applicability. To address these gaps, we developed mTIPs (Multimodal Time Series Imaging and Prediction System), which enables automated, early-stage quality control of human embryonic stem cell (hESC)-derived ROs. Firstly, we present the first publicly available multimodal, time-series imaging dataset of hESC-derived retinal organoids, establishing a foundational resource with significant clinical relevance that was previously lacking. Furthermore, we highlight the advantages of 3D imaging, demonstrating that Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) derived volumetric data offers unique insights into development and significantly enhances early predictive accuracy compared to traditional 2D bright-field microscopy. Notably, we developed a practical, unified model using a single time-encoding network that achieves an AUROC exceeding 0.8 at all time points, including as early as Day 6—significantly outperforming expert manual classification. mTIPs offers a scalable framework to standardize organoid production, enhancing the reliability of ROs for research and future clinical applications.

title: Pouring Sound-Based Cross-Container Water Temperature Recognition Using Single-Container Training
authors: Yi-Li Chen, Yu-Wei Kuo, Van-Thuan Tran, Wei-Ho Tsai
abstract: Acoustic-based temperature recognition (ATR) for flowing water offers a non-contact sensing alternative for various applications. However, practical deployment faces a critical challenge: training data is often available from only a single reference container, yet the system must operate across diverse containers in real-world use. Container-specific acoustic characteristics, such as resonance modes, material properties, and geometric effects, can dominate recorded pouring sounds and prevent direct transfer of models trained on one container to others. We tackle this single-container training scenario using the Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) as the base model, combining data augmentation (DA) with cross-entropy (CE) objective and supervised contrastive learning (SupCon) to learn container-invariant temperature representations. To contextualize our approach, we first evaluate several blind source separation (BSS) methods for extracting temperature-related sources as potential solutions; however, all separation approaches underperform the original, unseparated data, indicating that explicit source decomposition is ineffective for this task. Our proposed DA+CE+SupCon framework does not require explicit source decomposition or ground truth separated signals; instead, it leverages data augmentation and contrastive loss to enforce similarity between same-temperature samples regardless of container identity. Evaluating this approach on a dataset of four containers (two ceramic, two glass) across three temperature ranges (cold 20-25°C, warm 50-55°C, hot 80-95°C), we demonstrate significant improvements in cross-container transferability within the evaluated material set. When training on a single container with full data and testing on others, our approach achieves 4-10% higher cross-container accuracy than models trained with standard cross-entropy loss. In extreme data-scarcity scenarios (5-shot learning), the proposed approach further yields 13-23% gains, demonstrating exceptional robustness even with severely limited training data. These results enable practical ATR deployment with minimal training data collection requirements across the evaluated container types; generalization to other materials remains unexplored and is discussed as future work.

title: A Trigger-Based Time Division MAC Protocol for Mobile Underwater Sensor Networks with Heterogeneous Traffic
authors: Taejin Kwon, Ho-shin Cho
abstract: This paper presents a novel trigger-based medium access control (MAC) protocol for mobile underwater sensor networks (UWSNs) that operate under high propagation delay and heterogeneous traffic. Unlike conventional MAC schemes that rely on carrier sensing or frequent handshaking, our approach leverages pre-calculated propagation delays and sink-initiated trigger messages to coordinate packet transmissions with minimal control overhead. Each sensor node registers with the sink via a lightweight initialization process, enabling the sink to estimate the one-way delay and assign time slots accordingly. Time slots are dynamically adjusted based on the node’s data generation rate, estimated using an exponential weighted moving average (EWMA). By synchronizing trigger transmissions with delay-compensated timing, the protocol ensures that sensor packets arrive precisely within their assigned slots. The simulation results show that our protocol improves the throughput, end-to-end delay, collision rate, and energy efficiency while providing robust performance under mobility and varying traffic loads.

title: A Spring-Loaded Chip Interface for Flexible Stripline Cryogenic Readout
authors: L.H. Marting, N. de Keijzer, N. Drobotun, J.M. de Voogd, T. de Bondt, R. Huiting, R. Groefsema, J.J.A. Baselmans
abstract: Scaling astronomical detectors and quantum devices to larger and larger sizes will require more readout lines. A problem with extra readout lines is the complexity and cost of many coaxial cables in parallel. Furthermore, the thermal loading on the coldest stages via thermal conductivity should be kept at a minimum. Delft Circuits provides a 8-channel flexible cabling solution based on stripline technology with Ag or NbTi conductors and polyimide dielectric (flexline) as a solution to these problems. However, a compact and light-tight flex-to-chip interface is lacking, and this would be key to unlocking its full scaling and lightweight potential. Here, we present the design and measurements of a flexline to PCB interface for cryogenic detectors. The flexline is connected securely via a spring-loaded pressing tool, ensuring reliable contact even after thermal contraction. To reduce stray radiation, the chip and connector assembly sits in a light-tight setup. The connector spans a width of 20 mm along one edge of the chip, illustrating the compact design. We show that the flexline connector has an insertion loss of -2 dB at 7 GHz using a probe measurement at room temperature. We also demonstrate the connector with a test chip with kinetic inductance detectors in a dilution refrigerator cooled to 40mK.

title: A Two-Stage Biologically Inspired Robot Navigation Framework Via Reward-Modulated STDP and Obstacle-State Encoding_supp3-3667492.mp4
authors: Rizwana Kausar
abstract: This article presents a two-stage, biologically inspired neuromorphic framework for autonomous robot navigation in cluttered environments. The proposed architecture combines unsupervised spiking neural network (SNN)-based sensory abstraction with reward-modulated spike-timing-dependent plasticity (R-STDP) for decision-making. In the first stage, raw 360° LiDAR measurements are transformed into a compact, interpretable obstacle state through a lateral-inhibition-driven STDP network, yielding a low-dimensional, behaviorally relevant perception of the environment. In the second stage, navigation actions are learned via reward-modulated STDP operating on this abstracted state, supporting long-horizon goal-directed behavior without backpropagation or deep reinforcement learning. Two navigation paradigms are investigated: conventional goal-oriented navigation using relative bearing information, and a probabilistic field–based formulation that enables source-seeking behavior under goal uncertainty. The proposed approach is evaluated extensively in Gazebo and NVIDIA Isaac Sim using a TurtleBot3 platform across static, dynamic, and near-realistic environments. Experimental results demonstrate reliable navigation performance, competitive success and collision rates compared to state-of-the-art SNN and hybrid SNN–RL methods, and substantially lower estimated energy consumption. These findings highlight the effectiveness of modular, biologically plausible neuromorphic architectures for energy-efficient autonomous navigation in complex environments.

title: FloodApp: Real-Time Integrated Mobile Application for Flood Monitoring, Detection, and Alerts
authors: Muhammad Faiz Azlin, Rosniza Roslan, Raseeda Hamzah, Zainal Fikri Zamzuri, Muhammad Aqil Mohd Sabri
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Machine Intelligence, and Cybersecurity Technologies (AMICT)
abstract: This paper presents FloodApp, a real-time mobile application developed that deliver live information to enhance flood monitoring, detection, and alerts. This platform incorporates five primary features. The homepage provides the central hub, user easy access to all functions, and important updates. The camera module allows users to capture or video streaming for instant flood validation. The graph page visualizes water level data and trends, helping users monitor changes over time. The awareness enhancement is further achieved through the map page, which displays geospatial information of affected locations, and flood-prone areas. The weather page delivers current atmospheric conditions, including rainfall data and forecasts, enabling proactive decision-making. The design of all features to present relevant, and informative data through intuitive, user-friendly interface. FloodApp has been applied using Flutter, and Dart as frontend, and Firebase as backend database, to ensure scalability, real-time updates, and cross-platform accessibility. The results showed that FloodApp passed all feature interface, and functionality evaluations. Therefore, FloodApp provides a comprehensive mobile platform for flood monitoring that integrates multiple features into single application.

title: Study of the Rotation Curve of a Low-Mass Disc Galaxy
authors: Suparna Sau, Arkabrata Gupta, Chitrita Dasgupta, Rajiv Ganguly
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Computing, Astronomy and Renewable Energy (AICARE)
abstract: In this work, we have performed a parametric fit of the rotation curve of a low-mass spiral disc galaxy, NGC2403. We have implemented the Marcov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) procedure to fit the observational data with our model. Then, we have summarized the properties of each of the parameters and their contributions to the rotation curve. Finally, we computed the mass of the galaxy.

title: Mortality Prediction in ARDS Patients Using Long Short-Term Memory Networks
authors: S Anand, K. Chandramohan, S. Hemavathi, R. Jagadeesh Kannan, R. Poornima Lakshmi, Swetha G
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Patients in the intensive care unit (ICU) are at high risk of death from Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS), a devastating condition characterised by sudden inability to breathe. Timely and accurate prediction is crucial for enhancing clinical decision-making and patient care. Conventional scoring systems and statistical models, such SOFA and APACHE, have limited prediction accuracy due to their failure to account for the temporal intricacies of patient data. This paper presents a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for predicting death in ARDS patients, using the publicly accessible ARDS Patient Outcomes dataset from Kaggle. The dataset comprises demographic data, clinical characteristics, laboratory findings, and breathing status, which were transformed into sequential time-series inputs for training the LSTM model. The experimental assessment reveals that the proposed LSTM network achieved an overall accuracy of 99.5%, improving traditional machine learning. The results demonstrate that LSTM models may proficiently utilize temporal dependencies in patient data to improve predicted accuracy. The proposed method serves as an effective decision-support tool to assist physicians in the early identification of high-risk patients, hence enabling prompt treatments, optimal resource allocation, and perhaps decreasing fatality rates in ARDS care.

title: Handwritten Character Recognition of Kannada Language Using Machine Learning
authors: Shakunthala B S, Praveen B M, Ullas H S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Handwriting character recognition (HCR) is the capability of a computer to interpret handwritten inputs, representing a challenging area within pattern recognition research. While HCR is established, its application for Indian languages, particularly Kannada, is still developing. Handwritten character recognition is a significant challenge in pattern recognition and machine learning, particularly for regional languages such as Kannada. This study presents a method for identifying handwritten Kannada characters using three machine learning algorithms: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Support Vector Machines (SVM). A dataset from Kaggle containing handwritten Kannada characters was used, incorporating preprocessing techniques like image standardization and noise reduction to improve accuracy. CNN is utilized for feature extraction and classification, while KNN and SVM are employed for comparative evaluation. The models' performance is measured using precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score metrics, with CNN demonstrating superior recognition accuracy compared to KNN and SVM, emphasizing the benefits of deep learning. Additionally, a web-based app developed with Django allows for real-time character recognition, contributing to the advancement of optical character recognition in Kannada and aiding digitization and automation efforts.

title: Predicting Brain Stroke Risk Using Random Forest and SHAP Values
authors: Nagaveni M Padashetty, Veena S Badiger, Sheetal, Alli A, Anitha DSouza Jacintha, Rakesh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Brain stroke is a major global health concern and one of the major causes of death and long-term disability. Timely and precise prediction of stroke risk is crucial for initiating early treatment and improving patient outcomes. This dissertation explores the development of an intelligent stroke prognostication system using machine learning techniques. The study leverages a comprehensive dataset encompassing patient medical history, lifestyle factors, and health indicators. Data preprocessing steps include one-hot encoding for categorical variables and handling missing data. To tackle class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) is applied. The processed data repository is used to train a Random Forest Classifier, whose performance is evaluated using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and confusion matrix analysis. The results indicate that the Random Forest model, enhanced by SMOTE, achieves high sensitivity in predicting strokes. This approach provides a reliable tool to assist healthcare professionals in making informed decisions and implementing preventive strategies.

title: Vehicle detection using AI and ML based model to moderate the traffic avoid congestion conditions
authors: Abhay Bhatia, Anil Kumar, Anzar Ahmad, Sunil Kumar, Lokesh Kumar
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: Traffic management, which is used for detecting the vehicle is essential towards solving the problems that generally occurs due to urban congestion affecting the road safety as well as efficient transportation system. The Traffic management systems that work nowadays are able to collect useful information which helps in many ways likely towards improvement in traffic flow resulting into the overall performance of the road network by detecting vehicles and studying their movements with accuracy and efficiency. The paper reveals about the main needed and benefits of using the vehicle detection technique for traffic management. To cut down on traffic jams and shorten travel times, traffic must flow smoothly. Vehicle detection lets traffic management systems keep an eye on traffic conditions in real time, which helps them make smart decisions. Traffic signals can be changed on the fly to prioritize the smooth flow of vehicles, optimize signal timings, and ease traffic jams by looking at the density and speed of vehicles on different road segments. Making such optimization to the traffic flow makes it better resting into cut down on delays, which makes easier for people and their lives so that they can get around in cities. Detection and responding to incidents occurred like an accident, any breakdown, or the road hazards are much essential to be taken caried off for keeping the traffic moving smoothly and making it sure making these roads safe and safer to get visited. Vehicle detection systems make a quick identification towards any abnormal traffic patterns or a sudden change in behavior, which indicates the occurrence of any incident occurred [1]. This enables prompt response from traffic management authorities, who can dispatch emergency services or implement appropriate measures to divert traffic and minimize the impact of the incident. Effective incident management based on vehicle detection data helps reduce congestion, prevent accidents, and enhance overall road safety.

title: Image Encryption Based on S-Box and Xorshift PRNG
authors: J. A. Aboytes-González, G. Gallegos-García, M. T. Ramírez-Torres
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: In modern cryptography, the design of schemes that ensure high security and structural efficiency remains an active area of research, particularly in applications involving image processing. Image encryption presents specific challenges due to the large volume of data and the high adjacent pixel correlation inherent in digital images. For this reason, recent cryptographic research considers essential that new proposals effectively conceal visual information while maintaining reasonable processing time. This work proposes a new image encryption scheme based on improved S-Boxes and a pseudo-random number generator constructed using XOR operations. The structure of the scheme includes key generation, data transformation, and S-Box enhancement. All components are designed using lightweight computational operations, aiming to enable efficient implementation.

title: DI-Flame: A Decentralized Incentive Mechanism for Federated Learning Model Validation
authors: Ayan Roy, Kaustuvi Basu, Rik Chakraborti, Riley McDonough
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Federated learning enables multiple data owners to collaboratively train a global model, but verifying the correctness of submitted updates remains a critical challenge. Malicious clients may poison model weights, and colluding validators can undermine aggregation. We propose DI-FLAME, a decentralized validation framework that introduces a stake-and-proof mechanism for model verification. Each claim is submitted with a justification and stake, evaluated via a black-box credibility function. Validators engage in peer review, and contradictors may challenge weak claims by submitting stronger proofs and higher stakes. Rewards and penalties are dynamically distributed based on justification strength and challenge outcomes. DIFLAME provides robust defense against poisoned updates, even under adversarial majorities. While blockchain infrastructure is not required, DI-FLAME is compatible with with decentralized ledgers, enabling transparent recording of validation outcomes when deployed over a blockchain.

title: AI Based Real-Time Elderly Aid using Raspberry Pi
authors: Soumyajeet Panda, Subhadeep Ghosh, Sreedeep Ghosh, Souvik Modak, Anindya Sundar Maity, Harshit Srivastava
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Computing, Astronomy and Renewable Energy (AICARE)
abstract: The “AI-Based Elderly Assistance” project provides a new type of voice-controlled, multilingual alarm system for elderly individuals with cognitive, physical, or visual impairments. The proposed new alarm system uses a Raspberry Pi IoT (Internet of Things) platform. The new alarm system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP), speech-to-text, uses Google Text-toSpeech (gTTS) and uses Telegram API to provide a voice-controlled daily companion to the target audience without any screen. The proposed alarm system provides voicecontrolled scheduled alarm, smart reminders, and SOS emergency tracking alerts with the simple press of a button support independent living, safety, and access. Key features of the alarm system include: multilingual (voice) recognition and processing, alarm processing real time, GPS emergency location tracking, and lightweight module architecture (does not need a visual GUI). Voice is scheduled with the Gemini AI API, and the Telethon library allows the caregiver to respond with SOS emergency SMS, audio, and live coordinates via Geocoder. The system was validated in a simulation setting and demonstrated to be very usable for the target audience, particularly people with disabilities occasioned by natural aging. This has been a team effort to integrate assistive technology, embedded systems, multi-threading and human-computer interaction to bring fore a democratic intelligent device for the elderly.

title: IoT-Based Water Quality Monitoring Suitable for Fish Growth
authors: Swetha Thennavan, Prem Savarinathan, Avila Jayapalan
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: In India, fishermen and farmers produce about half of the fish caught in marine recreational fisheries, contributing 7.58% to global production. The country is a major exporter of fish and fish products, earning significant foreign exchange. Fishing provides livelihoods to thousands of fishermen. Aquaculture (fish farming) continues to be an important source of food, nutrition, jobs, and income, especially for rural communities, who rely on aquaculture to feed their families. This mini project aims to monitor the quality of water suitable for fishing. Hence it becomes mandatory to check the quality of water. To help monitor ecological conditions within ponds, different aspects of water quality such as pH, temperature, carbon dioxide levels, turbidity, and nutrient levels are all measured using appropriate sensors and instrumentation. From the pH sensor, it could be found whether the water is acidic or alkaline in nature. A turbidity sensor is used to determine the debris in water. This in turn has an impact on fish survival in water. A sudden temperature change may also affect the survival of the fish. The information thus collected is displayed in LCD. It is then passed to the mobile of farmers. We have used Thing Speak IoT platform.

title: Performance Comparison of Machine Learning Algorithms to Design Shallow Parser for Maithili Language
authors: Prabhat Kumar Singh, Yashika Mathur, Harish Patidar
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: Maithili is an Indo-Aryan language that poses challenges for natural language processing because it has limited tools and diverse grammar rules and word forms. This research focuses on building a simple shallow parser (Chunker) for the Maithili language and examines the performance of different machine learning algorithms in carrying out this task. The shallow parser is designed to recognize different types of phrases within a sentence, such as noun phrases, verb phrases, and prepositional phrases. We have evaluated different performance metrics, including precision, recall, F-measure, and accuracy, utilizing seven different machine learning algorithms. We have found that Multi layer perceptron learning algorithm is most suitable for designing shallow parser for Maithili language. There exists shallow parser for different Indian languages, but for Maithili, this will be the first work towards design of this tool. Shallow parser can be used for the development of advanced machine learning tools such as Parser, Sentiment analyzer, named entity recognition, and machine translation system for Maithili to other natural languages.

title: Advancing mineral carbonation in the circular economy: A sustainable pathway for power industry decarbonization
authors: Afif Izwan Abd Hamid, Nurul Noramelya Zulkefli, Fatin Nuraliya Muziadi, Noraziah Muda
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: The power industry is a major contributor to global CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions, particularly from coal-fired power plants. As decarbonization becomes a priority, Carbon Capture and Utilization (CCU) offers a promising approach to mitigating emissions. Mineral carbonation, a CCU technology, provides a stable and permanent method of CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> sequestration by reacting emissions with alkaline by-products such as fly ash and coal bottom ash—waste materials produced in power generation. This process not only reduces CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> levels but also converts industrial waste into valuable materials for construction, supporting the circular economy. Beyond construction applications, mineral carbonation has potential in marine ecosystem restoration, where carbonated fly ash and bottom ash can be used to create artificial reefs. These reefs serve as marine habitats, contributing to biodiversity conservation while permanently storing CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>. This dual-benefit approach aligns with Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) initiatives, making power plants active participants in sustainable development. Additionally, we examine regional case studies, particularly Malaysia’s National Energy Transition Roadmap (NETR), which promotes CCU adoption in the power sector. Global comparisons highlight best practices and policy frameworks supporting mineral carbonation for coal-fired power plant decarbonization. Key challenges include CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> transport logistics, reaction efficiency, and large-scale implementation barriers. Advances in supercritical CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> processing and process optimization enhance carbonation potential, making it a viable long-term strategy. Findings suggest that mineral carbonation has the potential to be adopted in power plants as a scalable CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> mitigation solution, leveraging existing industrial waste streams while contributing to net-zero goals by 2050. A collaborative effort between policymakers, researchers, and industry stakeholders is essential to unlocking its full potential as a sustainable solution for power industry decarbonization.

title: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence Based Personalization on Purchase Intention in Mobile Super Apps
authors: Anjela Graciana Sariputra, Valencia Himawan, Erwin Halim
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: This study investigates the influence of AI-driven personalization on user experience, trust, and purchase intention within super app. The rapid advancement of AI enables digital platforms to deliver highly tailored product and service recommendations, shaping a more engaging and efficient user journey. However, the growing reliance on personalization technologies also introduces challenges related to privacy, transparency and data security. Which can undermine user confidence and satisfaction. To address these issues, this study aims to examine AI based personalization affects user behavior and perception across interconnected digital ecosystems. Data were obtained through purposive sampling method involving 396 respondents in Jabodetabek (Jakarta metropolitan area in Indonesia) who have experience and actively use super apps. The data collection was conducted through structured questionnaires via Google Forms distributed from August to September 2025. Using Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with SmartPLS 4, the analysis tested eight variables, namely AI-driven personalization, personalization, customer satisfaction, customer experience, perceived risk, purchase intention, perceived ease of use, and perceived usefulness. In addition, there are 10 hypotheses that have been proven to be significant. This result showed that all hypotheses were statically significant, indicating that AI-driven personalization exert a positive and consistent influence on user satisfaction, perceived usefulness, and purchase intention. These findings highlight the dual role of personalization as both an enabler of meaningful digital experiences and a factor requiring strong ethical and data protection measures to sustain long term user trust.

title: Leveraging Sentiment Analysis to Predict Customer Retention on Strava: A Comparative Study of Random Forest and SVM
authors: Agnes Wijaya, Patricia Stephanie Budiono, Therenita Agustin, Evaristus Didik Madyatmadja
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: It is evident that customer retention is crucial to the sustainability of applications like Strava that relies heavily on user satisfaction and experience. This explains the motivation behind this research which aims to perform a sentiment analysis on Strava user posts from X social media while comparing the accuracy of customer retention prediction using Random Forest (RF) versus Support Vector Machine (SVM), ultimately to increase retention. This study works with a corpus of 13,676 Global user reviews about Strava. The data was first collected using crawling techniques followed by preprocessing, feature extraction, sentiment analysis, and visualization. The focus of this research is on the comparison of the two algorithms from the perspective of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. This research found that the SVM Algorithm surpassed RF with an accuracy of 86% compared to RF's 79%. Following opinion or sentiment classification, 51.2% was neutral, 36.8% was positive, and 12% was negative. This indicates that the Strava application can be optimally used by a subset of users who find some level of assistance and support from it.

title: Read-write LSTM: A Novel Approach Integrating Backpropagation to Data in LSTM
authors: Yassine Baghoussi, Carlos Soares, João Mendes Moreira
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Traditional recurrent neural networks operate as passive observers of data, unable to modify the information they learn from despite errors that may arise from suboptimal input representations. We introduce Read & Write LSTM (read-write LSTM), a new variant within the family of read & write machine learning (RW-ML) architectures that address this fundamental limitation by integrating input modification directly into the backpropagation process. Read-write LSTM establishes a dynamic feedback loop where input representations evolve alongside model weights through gradient transformation mechanisms. Our approach introduces a principled gradient scaling framework with an adaptive correction rate that carefully controls the extent of data modification, preserving data integrity while enhancing representational power. We comprehensively evaluate read-write LSTM against traditional LSTMs and state-of-the-art transformer models on the M4 competition and Numenta Anomaly Benchmark datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in forecasting accuracy. Notably, read-write LSTM consistently out-performs standard LSTM models in over 70% of time series with complex patterns and achieves superior performance on 55% of anomaly-rich datasets. Through extensive experimentation and analysis, we establish both the theoretical foundations and practical benefits of integrating data modification with neural computation, paving the way for a new generation of adaptive learning systems that actively reshape their inputs rather than merely adapting to them.

title: Disentangled Contrastive Representation Learning for Zero-Shot Biomedical Text Classification
authors: Ratri Mukherjee, Shailesh Dahal, Kishlay Jha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Zero-shot biomedical text classification requires accurate assignment of biomedical text (e.g., scientific abstract) to previously unseen labels (or concepts). Existing methods often struggle to generalize to novel concepts such as new diseases, drugs, and genes. To address these unique challenges, we propose a framework that combines feature disentanglement with contrastive learning to address this limitation. It separates each abstract into a content representation relevant for classification and a variance representation. This disentanglement ensures that the content features are invariant to the writing style, improving generalization to unseen labels. A contrastive learning strategy further structures the latent space by encouraging semantic clustering and separation of categories. Moreover, we model intra-class variance as a shared distribution across labels to enable variational data augmentation, enhancing robustness. The framework uses a domain-specific biomedical language model for feature extraction and fixed label anchors for semantic alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the largest available biomedical corpus achieve superior performance on zero-shot multi-label classification tasks by learning discriminative and style-invariant representations.

title: Fairness-First AI in Demand Response: Bridging Equity and Efficiency in Smart Energy Systems
authors: Renu Emile, Saroj Koul, Anushka Tatia, Anna Sahgal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Demand response (DR) is central to innovative grid efforts, yet traditional models often neglect social equity. We present a Fairness-First DR algorithm that embeds energy justice principles, digital inclusion, and algorithmic accountability into load-shifting strategies. Using Jaipur, India, as a case study, we propose a conceptual model that balances utility goals, such as peak reduction, with justice-oriented aims, including affordability, participation, and recognition. Our framework expands conventional energy models by integrating equitable DR access regardless of device ownership or internet access. We synthesise demand data under a cost-minimising baseline and our Fairness-First controller. Results show significantly improved equity, as low-income households capture a greater share of bill savings, and the Jain fairness index rises from ~0.65 to ~0.92, with minimal loss in peak reduction. Informal dialogue with Jaipur stakeholders - policymakers, utility engineers, and residents - reveals strong concern about fair benefit-sharing. Residents frequently worry that DR programs may favour those with smart appliances, excluding poorer households. We outline a realistic implementation pathway through Indian institutions, including the Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), the Energy and Resources Institute (TERI), the Self-Employed Women's Association (SEWA), and state governments. Proposed solutions address key barriers to peak reduction, including digital literacy, data gaps, and trust, by providing training and promoting transparent data sharing. By placing justice at the heart of DR, this study contributes actionable insights for more equitable energy transitions.

title: AI-Based Energy Consumption Forecasting System: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Ronit Upadhyay, Yash Dimri, Richa Sharma, Ratan Rajan Srivastava
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Forecasting energy consumption plays a key role in running modern power systems. This holds especially true in places like India. In India power use keeps climbing due to expanding industries, Urban growth and rising population. Better forecasts make grid management easy and cut down on wasted assets. Traditional stats approaches get a lot of play in energy predictions. Traditional stats struggle to spot nonlinear patterns and dynamic shifts. Even context-specific trends in big data sets prove tough. And Traditional ways do not handle improvements well. To fix those gaps AI and machine learning step in as strong options. AI tools manage huge multidimensional energy data and bring more accuracy and. The picked works use all sorts of AI and ML techniques like Long short-term memory network, Convolutional neural networks, Ensemble methods, Hybrids, Iot. These approaches are great success. Forecasting gets precise. Systems adapt in real time. Robustness holds up across most uses. Household energy tracking and industrial predictions improve. Microgrid tweaks work better. The review points out the holes in techniques. The paper calls for solid data setups to emerge. Tough hybrid AI designs need building. On top of that the findings stress AI boosts from IoT and blockchain. Renewables fit in nicely. Together they build a base for smart energy handling which is effective and sustainable.

title: Green AI and Sustainable Technology
authors: Zainab Al-zanbouri
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Metaverse, and Cybersecurity (ICAMAC)
abstract: Although artificial intelligence (AI) continues to drive innovation across various industries, there is a growing concern about its environmental impact. Green AI aims to maximize energy efficiency without compromising functionality by reducing AI's carbon footprint. Our previous research in green computing provided valuable insights into sustainable AI techniques and formed a strong foundation in this field that is consistent with Green AI's objectives. The main objective of this research is to identify the key factors influencing energy consumption in AI models by analyzing their performance and comparing their energy usage. With this work, we hope to gain a better understanding of how to make AI models more energy-efficient without sacrificing their efficacy. In this research we investigate energy consumption patterns in AI models using a real-world database, which includes various operations and configurations. The methodology we use involves comparing and analyzing AI models' energy consumption and estimating carbon footprints. We conduct two experiments to measure and analyze the energy consumed by different deep learning kernels on edge hardware. The results reveal that energy usage differs widely depending on configurations and kernel complexity. Some operations are highly energy-intensive, while others are much more efficient. These insights can guide energy-aware model design and optimization for greener AI systems. The research findings help shift from resource-intensive AI toward more sustainable alternatives, highlighting the persistent need for environmentally aware AI practices. By implementing green AI concepts, the technology industry can continue to innovate ethically while reducing its environmental impact.

title: Low-Latency EDMA Framework for AI-Based DSP SoCs in Multimedia Real-Time Systems
authors: Monisha R, Niharika H, Sakshi Shivakumar, Vivek Raviraj D, Bindu S, Rekha P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Real-time multimedia systems demand highspeed data transfer with minimal latency to support continuous video, audio, and image processing. Conventional CPU-based memory transfer introduces performance bottlenecks due to processor intervention in every transaction. To overcome this, the proposed work presents a low-latency Enhanced Direct Memory Access (EDMA) framework for AI-based DSP System-on-Chip (SoC) applications. The architecture automates memory transfers through dedicated hardware logic, thereby reducing CPU load and accelerating data movement. Both CPU and EDMA modules were implemented in RTL and synthesized using Cadence Genus in 45 nm technology, with functional verification carried out in Cadence SimVision. Results show that the EDMA design achieves lower cycle counts, efficient area utilization, and reduced power consumption compared to the CPU-only approach. Proposed EDMA achieves faster and more efficient data handling while reducing processor workload with total power consumption of 62.41nW and a chip area of 950.666 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu \mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> compared to the results reported in the literature. The framework demonstrates its potential for integration into highthroughput, real-time multimedia SoCs, ensuring faster data handling with minimal latency.

title: Dual-Stream LiDAR-Radar Fusion: Balancing Accuracy and Efficiency for Real-Time 3D Object Detection
authors: Nithin Sathya, Syam Mohan E, Gobi Ramasamy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: This research presents a fusion framework using LiDAR and radar that is dedicated to achieving efficient 3D object detection for autonomous driving in real-time. LiDAR systems provide spatial geometry with a high-resolution output. However, they can be impaired in adverse weather conditions. Hence, the addition of radar provides information that is more robust against various weather conditions, which is worthy of investigation. In the literature, the extant approaches L4DR and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{M}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>-Fusion, either prioritize accuracy at the expense of efficiency at under 10 Frames Per Second (FPS) or efficiency, ultimately conflicting with operational capacity, which ranges from 1 to 3 FPS. We propose a dual stream architecture that applies pillarbased preprocessing, lightweight attention, and multi-scaled feature encoding in the manner of a lightweight detection head, in order to efficiently process traditional fused sensor data to disentangle competition, leading with computational efficiency and utilizing designs that provide as reliable a detection as possible. On the nuScenes mini dataset, we achieved 108.5-120.6 FPS over ten times faster than the state-of-the-art models for fused functionalities in addition to a competitive performance offering an Average Precision (AP3D) score of 44.2 and ablation studies that confirmed the contributions of uncertainty-aware loss balancing, radar denoising, and the data augmentation approach. These performances indicate that a path exists for efficiency-based fusion framework.

title: Efficient Privacy-Preserving In-Network Data Aggregation for Low-Latency Healthcare IoT
authors: Huzaif Khan, Sriven Srilakshmi Pulkaram, Ali Jalooli
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Real-time monitoring of healthcare demands lowlatency, secure, and energy-efficient transfer of sensitive phys-iological data. Traditional cloud-centric systems are unable to meet these demands due to excessive delays, privacy concerns, and poor adaptability. In this work, a decentralized system that combines Homomorphic Encryption (HE) with in-network computing (INC) and deep reinforcement learning is presented to address these challenges. Our proposed approach provides low-latency and privacy-preserving communication of personal health data, reduces cloud aggregation overhead through secure computations within the network, and dynamically adjusts to varying network conditions, such as energy availability, trust, and data congestion. It also efficiently calibrates to realistic healthcare settings without compromising performance or confidentiality. By integrating encrypted aggregation, context-aware INC nodes, and adaptive routing, the framework provides a robust and efficient solution to secure healthcare IoT. The simulation re-sults demonstrate that our approach outperforms alternatives in energy efficiency, communication overhead, and data reliability.

title: Detection of Cataract Using Deep Learning Models
authors: Zorawar Jaiswal, Abhishek Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Cataract refers to the opacity of the eye lens that deteriorates vision, leading to either partial or even complete visual impairment, and a shallower quality of life. Early detection of Cataract may result in mitigating crucial surgeries and easier tracking of the cataract stage. This paper compares two state-of-the-art models: EfficientNet B7 and MobileNet V2, to classify images as normal or cataract. The study is the first to utilize and set a baseline performance on the novel dataset created by Azri. The images varied greatly in terms of size, aspect ratio, and average intensity, increasing the need for stricter preprocessing. Both models exceed performance standards on general deep learning problems and achieve up to 100% accuracy, opening the door to uncharted avenues in mitigating cataract-related ailments on low-resource end devices.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning and Machine Learning Framework for Automated Classification of Leukemia Using Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM and XG Boost
authors: Deepak Rao Khadatkar, Ayush Sah, Ashish Gupta, Chirag Kashyap
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: This research presents a hybrid intelligent framework that integrates deep learning and classical machine learning techniques for the automated classification of leukemia subtypes using microscopic blood smear images. The proposed pipeline involves systematic image preprocessing, CNN-based feature extraction, and evaluation through multiple classifiers including Support Vector Machine (SVM), XGBoost, Random Forest, and Decision Tree. Experimental results demonstrate an average accuracy of approximately 96%, with SVM achieving the best performance, ensuring robust and precise leukemia detection across heterogeneous datasets. The modular design enhances clinical applicability by minimizing manual diagnostic effort. Future work will focus on integrating explainable AI (XAI) components and enabling real-time diagnostic capabilities to further improve interpretability and deployment in healthcare environments.

title: Quantitative Analysis of CNN models for Identifying OSCC
authors: Puneet Misra, Ayushi Chaurasia
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Oral cavity cancer is characterized by high mortality rates and has barely improved results over last several decades. Hence, it is a significant global health concern. This stagnation is largely attributed to the frequent diagnosis of the disease at advanced stages. Histopathological assessment of tissue biopsies is most standard and reliable way for diagnosis of cancer in oral cavity, but this method is labor-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. Computational pathology, powered by deep learning, offers a promising approach to enhance diagnostic accuracy, efficiency, and objectivity. A suite of Convolutional Neural Network (CNNs) trained using imagenet weights for the binary classification of cancer in oral cavity from digitized histopathological images have been evaluated. Utilizing the principles of transfer learning, ten distinct architectures were fine-tuned and assessed based on their accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity. The results identified two models with exceptional performance: EfficientNetB3, which achieved near-perfect scores of 99.00% across all three metrics, and Xception, which demonstrated a robust accuracy of 97.88% coupled with an outstanding sensitivity of 98.80%. The superior performance of these models, particularly EfficientNetB3, signifies a substantial advancement toward the development of a reliable, automated tool to assist pathologists. Such a system has the potential to speed up the diagnostic process. Hence, reducing turnaround times, and ultimately improving patients results by providing earlier and reliable cancer diagnosis.

title: Fine-Tuned Transfer Learning and Hybrid Deep Models for Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring
authors: Subhash Mondal, Sanapareddi Gari Yuva Nithesh Reddy, Kakumani Subba Haneesh, K Vamsi Krishna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Monitoring the health of a fetus means checking its heart rate and movement during pregnancy and labor. To lower the risk of difficulties for both the mother and the child, early intervention and a high survival rate are very important. Doppler and fetal electrocardiography are two common procedures that can check heart health, find problems, and give important information about conditions including arrhythmias or congenital malformations. However, these classic monitoring methods are sometimes affected by signal noise, baseline drift, and interference, leads harder to make accurate diagnoses. This research presents a deep learning-based framework for the classification of fetal cardiac rhythms via direct FECG recordings to overcome existing challenges. The study is conducted by acquiring data from the PhysioNet FECG database. The signal quality was enhanced by using novel preprocessing pipelines such as bandpass filtering, R-peak identification, and Independent Component Analysis (ICA). Several deep learning architectures are employed and analyzed by picking Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), and Transformer networks. Finally, a fusion of two best-performing hybrid CNN-GRU models is proposed. The fusion model exceeded the LSTM (93.75%), GRU, and Transformer (88.54%) models, with an accuracy of 96.88% and a balanced F1-score of 0.98. The efficiency and reliability of the fusion classification model are significantly improved with the integration of a sophisticated preprocessing pipeline and hybrid approach. The proposed AI-driven approach could become an alternative in future real-time maternal-fetal healthcare systems. The solution improves prenatal monitoring, detects distress, and facilitates the early identification of fetal heart anomalies.

title: A Holistic Framework for Realtime UPI Fraud Detection: Balancing Performance, Latency, and Privacy with FL and XAI
authors: Raj Kumar V, Ragul B, Iswarya M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: The rapid global adoption of real-time payment systems like the Unified Payments Interface (UPI) has created unprecedented convenience but has also exposed significant vulnerabilities to financial fraud. This necessitates advanced detection systems that are not only accurate but also private, transparent, and exceptionally fast. Existing research often addresses these requirements in isolation. A key novelty of our study is the introduction of prediction latency as a first class metric, directly addressing the real-time constraints of financial systems a factor commonly overlooked. This paper introduces a holistic, three pillar framework designed for the practical deployment of UPI fraud detection. We conduct a rigorous comparative study of diverse machine learning architectures including Logistic Regression, Multi-Layer Perceptrons, and XGBoost to empirically identify an optimal model based on a trade-off analysis of performance and speed. The empirically validated optimal model is then implemented in a comprehensive framework featuring Federated Learning (FL) for privacy-preserving training and Explainable AI (XAI) for transparency. Our results reveal that XGBoost provides a superior balance, achieving high fraud recall with significantly lower latency than neural network alternatives, making it a more viable candidate for real-world deployment.

title: SakhiSuraksha: an AI and IoT-Based Intelligent Emergency Response System for Women's Safety
authors: Mallegowda M, Sharanya M S, Yash Suresh Gavas, Venella Rudraraju, Anita Kanavalli
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: This paper presents SakhiSuraksha, an intelligent emergency response system designed to enhance women's safety by integrating Artificial Intelligence (AI), Internet of Things (IoT), and mobile technologies. The system autonomously detects verbal distress signals using a real-time audio analysis pipeline and triggers immediate multi-channel alerts via SMS, WhatsApp, and automated calls. Leveraging a sophisticated stack including Stanford CoreNLP for linguistic parsing, AssemblyAI for speech-to-text conversion, and a LLaMA 213 B model for contextual distress classification, the solution ensures high accuracy and reliability. The system also integrates GPS tracking and wearable IoT devices for discreet activation and operates effectively even in low-connectivity conditions through an SMS fallback mechanism. Experimental evaluation on a custom multilingual dataset demonstrates over 96% accuracy in distress detection for Hindi and English, with an average end-to-end alert response time of 1.3 seconds. The architecture is validated to be scalable, supporting over 500 concurrent users with 99.97% uptime. This paper details the complete system architecture, the technical methodology employed, and a comprehensive quantitative performance evaluation, establishing SakhiSuraksha as a robust, scalable, and privacy-conscious real-time safety solution.

title: Battery Energy Storage System Optimization in the Philippine Grid Operations with Significant level of Variable Renewable Energy
authors: Neil Martin Modina, Ricardo Dagle, Jeselle Zafra, Aaron Tadeos
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: The Philippine power system faces operational challenges from increasing renewable energy penetration, particularly in the Visayas grid where variable renewables now contribute significantly to the generation mix [1]. While Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) offer technical advantages for frequency control, their integration through Automatic Generation Control (AGC) presents limitations due to energy constraints from finite state-of-charge capacity and inherent latency in centralized dispatch systems [5].This study examines control strategy enhancements for BESS participating in frequency regulation services. Analysis focuses on comparing AGC with decentralized Active Power-Frequency Control (APFC) approaches, which enables faster response to local frequency deviations through autonomous operation [7]. Preliminary operational data from international implementations demonstrate BESS capabilities in improving frequency stability, though Philippine-specific performance metrics remain limited in publicly available literature [2].The research provides technical insights for integrating energy storage in archipelagic power systems, supporting the Department of Energy's renewable energy targets [1]. Findings emphasize the need for localized control strategies adapted to the Philippine grid's unique characteristics, including its HVDC-interconnected but asynchronous regional networks.

title: ConsentSync: A Smart Privacy Dashboard for Digital Consent and Data Control
authors: Harshana Shruthi P, Bavithra B, Periya Nayaki A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: In today's world, personal data is collected without informed consent. Existing cookie banners and privacy policies obscure rather than reveal data practices. Current cookie banners and privacy policies obscure rather than reveal data practices. ConsentSync is a Chrome extension committed to restoring user control through a real-time privacy dashboard, which will let users revoke permissions, delete cookies, or block access automatically. The AI-powered consent analyzer in ConsentSync uses natural language processing to summarize privacy policies and detect risky terms like “sell data” and “track across websites.” The system will incorporate a privacy reputation engine that will give scores to websites based on their transparency. Combining automation with customization and interpretable summaries, ConsentSync thus lets practical, user-friendly digital consent management become a reality, meeting standard requirements of the GDPR and ISO/IECTS27560. Accordingly, the tool will enable a user to manage their consent settings in a flexible and interpretable way.

title: IPFormer: Identity Feature with Prompt Learning-Based Transformer for Partial Wet Fingerprint Restoration and Recognition
authors: Kuan-Chang Shih, Ching-Te Chiu, Chun-Hao Yang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: We propose IPFormer, a Transformer model that integrates identity feature extraction and prompt learning to restore blurred or wet partial fingerprints. The Recognition ID Feature Extraction Module captures identity-related features and incorporates them into the model’s decoder, ensuring restored fingerprints retain essential identity information. Additionally, the prompt block encodes degradation information to enhance restoration across various types of degradation. To address data misalignment in real-world fingerprint collection, we employ CycleGAN [1] to generate aligned wet fingerprint data, improving performance under real-world conditions. Experimental results on real-world data show that IPFormer achieves an FRR of 11.89%, which is a significant improvement of 34.99% compared to no enhancement. It also significantly outperforms existing methods, reducing the FRR by 35.73% compared to Residual-M-net [2] and by 25.50% compared to FPD-M-Net [3].
