title: Virtual Flow Metering For Data-limited Wells Using Machine Learning For Multiphase Flow Prediction In Commingled Testing Environments
authors: Nik Nur Wahidah Nik Hashim, Tareq Aziz Hasan Al-Qutami
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Machine Intelligence, and Cybersecurity Technologies (AMICT)
abstract: This study presents a data-driven Virtual Flow Metering (VFM) framework for upstream oil and gas operations with limited historical well-test data, particularly in commingled environments where wells are tested intermittently using shared MPFM infrastructure. Using standard wellhead measurements (FTHP, FLP, PDGP, PDGT, CV), machine learning models including Gradient Boosting Regressor, Random Forest Regressor, and K-Nearest Neighbors were developed and validated through time-based splits, cross-validation, and jackknife resampling. Results from a representative well showed that gas prediction accuracy improved by 56.7% for GBR and 46.2% for KNN after outlier removal, though this refinement reduced accuracy for oil and water, underscoring the trade-off in optimizing multi-target regression models under small-data constraints. Ensemble models provided the most consistent performance, and total liquid prediction emerged as a stable proxy in noisy, low-sample environments. The study demonstrates that lightweight supervised models can provide reliable multiphase flow estimates in data-scarce conditions.

title: Adaptive DDSS for Clinical Workflow Integration and Predictive Analytics
authors: Kulvinder Singh, Pawandeep Singh, Akhil Rai, Sukhneet Kaur, Anoop Kumar
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: The need to bring predictive analytics to clinical workflows was one of the base factors to further the diagnostic accuracy and the concept behind precision medicine in contemporary healthcare. The hereby article depicts vardas: Adaptive Data Driven Decision Support System Overcoming the Curse of Complexity Developed Using DSM Hypothesis Testing specifically designed to predict CVR outcomes across the Heart Disease UCI dataset. Multi-level ensemble pattern with stacking method and adaptive optimization learning methods are applied to enhance the reliability and generalizability of predictions. Two ensemble configurations models: traditional stacking-based methodology and novel model implemented through the mechanisms of optimized weight assignment and feature scaling, are explored assumed implemented and compared computationally in terms of accuracy. Retrospective analysis of the results received and practical application shows o that adaptive tuning greatly enhances the predictive performance of the proposed model within the clinical workflow. This renders a designed DDSS model a safe and comprehensible device in clinical environment, which raises chances of detecting heart disease at early stages.

title: Preparing Large-Scale Smart Meter Data for Machine Learning in Low-Resource Environment
authors: Farhana Abdul Hadi, Nurul Asyikin Bte Mohamed Radzi, Syed Ahmad Fuad Syed Abdul Hamid, Faris Syahmi Bin Samidi, Nayli Adriana Binti Azhar
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) has opened access to high-volume, high-frequency time-series data generated from smart meters. These data provide valuable insights for various applications, including customer behaviour analysis, anomaly detection, and electricity grid planning. However, handling and preparing large-scale smart meter datasets remains a significant challenge due to the needs for substantial storage capacity, high processing power, and programming skills. This study aims to prepare a comprehensive, real-world, large-scale smart meter dataset for machine learning (ML) training and testing by leveraging Microsoft Power BI, SQL, and Python. The processed dataset will support the development of machine learning (ML) models for fault detection, classification, and predictive maintenance in smart meter operations.The raw dataset used in this study is sourced from a high-density urban area in Kuala Lumpur, comprising data from 18,240 randomly selected smart meters out of 228,944 installed units. The dataset spans a two-year period (2022–2024) and includes key parameters such as kilowatt-hour (kWh), kilovar-hour (kVarh), events, and alarms. The dataset is extracted from TNB Datalake in text format, compressed and transferred to the local storage.During the data loading into Microsoft Power BI, the this research experienced performance slowness due to the data has exceed its maximum rows [1]. To address this, Python scripts are deployed to automate the extraction, merging, and pre-processing of raw data files. Python is chosen as it is effective in handling CSV and text-based sources, enabling batch processing to merge files, eliminate duplicates, and filter irrelevant data before loading into a structured format ready for ML model training.Following pre-processing, the data is stored in an SQL database, which provides a centralized, efficient storage solution with optimized query performance. Although SQL databases require scripting knowledge, Microsoft Power BI's Power Query feature enables additional data integrity checks—such as duplicate removal, handling missing values, and merging multiple tables—through an intuitive interface, minimizing the need for advanced SQL skills. Once the data cleaning process is complete, descriptive analytics are performed in Power BI to identify trends and patterns. Further data transformation and restructuring are conducted to ensure the dataset's compatibility with machine learning workflows.In conclusion, the use of Python, SQL, and Microsoft Power BI offers an efficient, scalable, and practical solution for large-scale smart meter data preparation within a PhD research context Basically for this study, Phyton was used for file extraction, merging and data loading, SQL provide reliable data storage and data analysis platform while Microsoft Power BI allows data visualization with interactive dashboards. This workflow not only addresses the technical challenges of big data handling but also ensures that high-quality, well-structured datasets are available to support advanced machine learning applications in smart meter fault detection and predictive maintenance.

title: Deep Learning Approach for IoT Vulnerability Severity Prediction
authors: Samira A. Baho, Jemal H. Abawajy
publish: 2025 International Conference on Advances in Machine Intelligence, and Cybersecurity Technologies (AMICT)
abstract: The proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has introduced a vast and complex attack surface, making efficient management and prioritization of vulnerabilities a critical cybersecurity challenge. Fast and accurate assessment of IoT vulnerability severity level is essential for effective prioritization and management of security risks. However, manual evaluation remains time-consuming, error-prone, and increasingly unsustainable given the rapid growth of IoT-specific vulnerabilities. This paper presents a novel approach that leverages a transfer learning model to automatically predict the severity of IoT vulnerabilities based on their textual descriptions, by leveraging the rich semantic information in vulnerability reports. This solution allows us to address the common issue of data scarcity in specialized domains like IoT security. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model significantly outperforms traditional machine learning techniques, achieving high accuracy of 91.5 and F1-scores of 0.90 in severity classification. This enables security teams to rapidly assess and prioritize vulnerabilities, thereby enhancing proactive defense measures and optimizing resource allocation in complex IoT environments.

title: Design of Green Hydrogen-Based Energy Storage System for Microgrid Pilot Project at EGAT Learning Center, Headquarter
authors: Prem Chutiampaiwong, Pimchanok Fungwithtaya
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: Due to the growing trend of renewable energy (RE) in Thailand, the Electricity Generating Authority of Thailand (EGAT), which is responsible for the country's power generation and transmission systems, has planned to increase the proportion of RE integration. However, among various RE sources, solar energy has the greatest potential in Thailand. The large-scale deployment of solar panels often results in energy generation exceeding demand, a phenomenon known as "Excess Energy."Therefore, EGAT has considered utilizing excess energy by storing the surplus solar power for use when demand arises through energy storage systems (e.g., Battery Energy Storage System (BESS), Hydrogen Energy Storage System (HESS), etc.) HESS is regarded as an emerging technology with the potential to support future energy needs. In line with this, EGAT has implemented a green hydrogen-based energy storage system for a microgrid pilot project at EGAT Learning Center, headquarters, to exercise the use of excess energy.This paper presents a conceptual framework for HESS, comprising components such as an electrolyzer, fuel cell, battery, and other essential equipment. These components function as energy storage units, allowing excess energy to be stored and subsequently supplied to meet electricity demand. The paper also provides a detailed analysis of the system’s processes, chemical reactions, characteristics, efficiency, and other relevant aspects. Furthermore, it proposes an optimized solution to minimize the cost of a green hydrogen-based energy storage system by utilizing existing rooftop solar car parking areas for a microgrid pilot project. The optimization is conducted through modeling using PLEXOS software. The analysis incorporates input data on solar energy production and electricity demand collected raw data over one year prior to the project's initiation, along with economic and technical constraints.The result of this paper is the optimal size of HESS including electrolyzer, fuel cell, battery and other essential equipment. In addition, the results present the economic analysis of the Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS), ensuring minimum energy price in covering the project costs. Whilst Levelized Cost of Hydrogen (LCOH) is highlighted here to serve the total expenses associated with hydrogen production throughout its entire lifecycle including both capital expenditures (CAPEX) and operational expenditures (OPEX).At the end, the paper offers an optimal design for HESS applicable to other microgrid systems to support renewable energy integration, enhance energy efficiency, and improve power system reliability. The paper also considers investment costs, competitiveness, and cost-effectiveness to ensure the system sustainably feasible in long run.

title: Grid Sustainability The First 5-Terminal Protection Scheme for Managing Complex Power System
authors: Nur Ismahani Laili Binti Mohd Zamri, Mohd Asri Bin Arshad, Mohd Luqmanul Hakim Bin Yahya
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: This paper presents the overall protection scheme applied to the 5-Terminal Transmission Overhead Line at Tenaga Nasional Berhad’s (TNB) Grid 275KV High Voltage Substation in Eastern Malaysia. This is the first implementation of such a configuration in Asia, making it a valuable reference for designing and testing procedures for Transmission Overhead Line configurations with more than 3-terminals. This study is motivated by the increasing complexity of multi-terminal power transmission systems and the urgent need for coordinated protection schemes that maintain grid stability and reliability. The 5-terminal configuration implemented by TNB serves as a benchmark to address these challenges. The modeling of this complex protection scheme adheres to all relevant IEEE standards and the guidelines provided by the original equipment manufacturer (OEM). This paper highlights the key challenges and barriers encountered introducing the 5-Terminal Overhead Line configuration. Furthermore, the project’s implementation assesses the opportunities and benefits associated with deploying an Overhead Line configuration with more than 3-terminals.

title: Incorporating Unplanned Outage Risk and Countermeasures as an Optional Subject in Applied Science Education
authors: Adam Junid, Azalan Sulaiman, Mohd Rosnizam Zailan, Muhammad Rizal Faizal Ghani, Muhammad Nazim Hakimi Ghani
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Conference on Engineering Education (ICEED)
abstract: Unplanned outages-resulting from electrical nuisance trips, equipment failures, and design or installation flaws-lead to unwanted system downtime. While practical risk analysis, troubleshooting and countermeasures are critical for minimizing such outages, these skills are often absent from conventional academic and vocational curricula. Instead, they are typically acquired through post-incident reviews, correction of contractor errors, online technical forums and coaching design consultants on operational requirements. Ideally, strict adherence to standards and regulations by designers, installers, and construction personnel would reduce outage risks, but enforcement gaps persist-especially where compliance criteria is not specified or mandated for non-residential or non-office structures, such as unmanned toll plazas, gantries, substations, and pipeline crossings. For example, in Malaysia, MS IEC 62305 Lightning Protection compliance is precluded by the Malaysian Energy Commission for unmanned installations, and similar regulatory gaps exist globally. This paper proposes incorporating the study of unplanned outage risks and countermeasures into Applied Science education as an optional subject. Drawing on the authors' career experiences, the paper considers a structured educational approach supported by diagrams, photographs, and references to relevant standards. To enhance accessibility and affordability, publicly available artificial intelligence (AI) tools are used and suggested for calculations and simulations, eliminating the need for costly proprietary software. Beyond its value for engineering education, this work provides practical material for construction contract preparation and QA/QC checklists, supporting industry efforts to minimize unplanned outage risks in the absence of comprehensive regulatory mandates.

title: Scalable Machine Learning Architecture for Securing IoT Networks with Digital Governance in the AI Era of Cyber-Resilient Smart Cities
authors: Ramisa Samin Shammah, Ishraf Rahman, Zenith Matin Guria, Sultana Kowser, Omar Faruq, Niaz Morshed
publish: 2025 International Conference on AI-Driven STEM Education and Learning Technologies (AISTEMEDU)
abstract: With the high rate of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, a new era of smart cities has been ushered in where the cities have been made more connected, efficient and innovative. However, this development poses great problems in ensuring security and stability of IoT networks. The proposed paper suggests a scalable machine-learning architecture that will enhance the security of IoT networks in smarter cities, and in particular, cyber-resilience in the modern AI age. The proposed architecture merges machine-learned models with digital governance approaches to provide real-time anomaly detection capabilities, intrusion prevention, and privacy-preserving solutions; and outlines the ways AI models can be used in IoT security and discusses the essential concerns, e.g., data privacy, compliance with regulations, and resource utilization. The suggested solution is tested with the help of simulations that prove its efficiency in resisting the process of cyber-attacks and in providing the means of scalability and flexibility in dynamic smart-city settings, which, in its turn, preconditions the establishment of the conceptual background of the formation of cyber-resilient smart cities in which the IoT networks will be able to operate safely and effectively even under the influence of the surfacing cybersecurity threats.

title: SecureFlow: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Model for Predictive Cloud Security Threat Mitigation Using Network Traffic Analysis
authors: Taslimmirza Altafhusen Mansur
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: As cloud computing continues to reshape modern infrastructure, the threat landscape has evolved in both complexity and scale. Traditional intrusion detection systems, often reliant on static rules or signature matching, lack the adaptability required to detect novel or time-evolving attacks. We present SecureFlow in this paper, a hybrid deep learning model where we integrate Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks in order to provide real-time detection and predictive threat mitigation through flow-based analysis on network traffic. SecureFlow is designed to capture spatial features inherent in packet structures and temporal dependencies that emerge across sequences of network flows. Evaluated on the CICIDS2017 and UNSW-NB15 datasets, SecureFlow achieves an F1-score of 98.2% and demonstrates the ability to forecast attacks up to 30 seconds before their execution. The architecture exhibits strong generalization, low inference latency, and robustness under adversarial noise, making it ideal for deployment in cloud, edge and real-time environments. This work presents a significant step toward proactive and intelligent cloud threat defense.

title: Software-Defined Federated AI Model for Privacy-Preserving Anomaly Detection in Vehicular Edge Environments
authors: T. Ratha Jeyalakshmi, Pavithra G Shetty, Santhosh G S, Hemanth Kumar S
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The Real-time anomaly detection methods ensuring data privacy and computational efficiency are needed owing to the rapid increase in networked and autonomous vehicles. Raw sensor streams offloading to distant cloud infrastructure is usually required in standard centralised learning frameworks, causing latency problems and potential data leakage. This paper introduces a software-defined, federated learning framework aimed at vehicular edge networks to overcome these obstacles. Localised model training is facilitated in every vehicle through our light-weight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) structure for localised model training, which is formulated for temporal sensor data such as gyroscope and accelerometer signals. Instead of sending raw inputs, nodes send securely encrypted model updates, which are then aggregated by a central coordinator using a privacy- aware averaging mechanism. By adding calibrated noise to gradient updates, differential privacy hides user-specific behavior, further protecting individual data features. Experimentation based on a simulated car dataset demonstrates a detection accuracy of over 96% and considerable 40% communication overhead savings. The capability of the model to perform well in resource-constrained environments further supports the validity of the model for real-time deployment in intelligent vehicle systems. The promise of the framework as a decentralised, privacy-aware anomaly detection method for next-generation transportation systems is solidified by these results.

title: Transfer Learning in Cross-Domain Fault Diagnosis: an Application to Gearbox Faults
authors: Navya Sonal Agrawal, Thanish Bolla, Teena Sharma, Nishchal K. Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Ensuring the reliability of safety-critical systems such as gearbox assemblies requires robust fault diagnosis methods capable of operating under varying conditions. However, practical implementation is often hindered by data scarcity and domain shift between different operating environments. This study investigates the application of transfer learning for cross-domain Intelligent Fault Diagnosis (IFD), with a focus on gearbox fault detection as a representative case. This study systematically examines two deep learning architectures: a one-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (1D CNN) and a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network-within a unified transfer learning framework. The models are pre-trained on a source domain and subsequently fine-tuned on limited labeled samples from the target domain to evaluate their cross-domain adaptability. Experiments are conducted on the open-source Southeast University (SEU) gearbox fault dataset demonstrate that transfer learning enables high diagnostic accuracy even with limited target data. Results reveal that the 1D CNN consistently outperforms the BiLSTM in low-data scenarios, indicating that spatially localized convolutional features exhibit stronger cross-domain generalization than temporal representations. Additionally the effect of various weight initialization schemes is analyzed. The findings confirm that transfer learning offers a practical and data-efficient strategy for enhancing the robustness and adaptability of fault diagnosis models in industrial applications.

title: Early Detection and Attribution of Structural Changes in Dynamic Networks
authors: Izhar Ali, Shen-Shyang Ho
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Dynamic networks evolve through structural changes that significantly impact their functionality. Early detection of these changes remains a fundamental challenge due to the inherent tradeoffs between detection delay and false alarm control. Current methods accumulate evidence of these changes only after they occur, creating unavoidable detection delays that limit practical utility in time-sensitive applications. We address this limitation through horizon martingales that leverage statistical forecasting to accumulate evidence from predicted future network states, enabling detection before changes fully manifest. We establish two fundamental results: (1) horizon martingales preserve the martingale property under proper forecasting calibration, and (2) they maintain rigorous false alarm control with probability bounds of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1/\lambda$</tex> for detection threshold <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda$</tex>. Comprehensive evaluation across diverse synthetic network types and real-world social interaction data demonstrates consistent improvements in detection speed with 13–25% reductions in detection delay while maintaining strict statistical guarantees.

title: SmartRecycleX: An AI-Powered Gamified Platform for Citizen-Driven Circular Waste Management
authors: Prajakta Ghosh, Prama Mondal, Soumyarup Dutta, Riddhiman Rakshit, Malay Gangopadhyay, Subhabrata Banerjee
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Computing, Astronomy and Renewable Energy (AICARE)
abstract: To enhance waste transparency in a bid to do so. clean up activities and promote popular community. involvement, a system run by AI has been skewed such that. Other collaborative methods of waste management in cities can be facilitated. Before and after submissions are checked by tailored computer vision methods through which spatial alignment is considered and waste types such as plastic, paper, metal (anode), and electronics are identified. The integrity of data has been increased and fraudulent submissions reduced by the use of blockchain-based logging and enhanced SHA256 encryption. The system’s reliability has been demonstrated through qualitative parameters, including high recognition accuracy, reduced reliance on manual checks, fast processing times, and reliable performance under simultaneous user activity. Engineered for the fostering of sustainable urban development, circular economy and environmentally-friendly behavior changes - within which is situated through utilization of gamification featuring eco-point rewards systems and real time dashboards.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Diagnostic Utility for Predicting Ertapenem Resistance in Klebsiella Pneumoniae Using MALDI-TOF MS Spectra
authors: Suhani Bansal, Chaya Ravindra
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Klebsiella pneumoniae is a gram-negative bacterium that causes over 600,000 deaths annually worldwide. Carbapenems, a powerful class of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta$</tex>-lactam antibiotics, are used to treat Klebsiella pneumoniae, as a lastresort when other antibiotics fail. However, when bacteria become resistant to carbapenems, treatment options become very limited. Ertapenem, a commonly used member of the carbapenems family, is widely applied in clinical settings, making early detection of ertapenem resistance crucial for preventing the spread of resistant strains and improving patient outcomes. Traditional methods such as Polymerase Chain Reaction, Whole-Genome Sequencing, and Gene Expression Analysis remain limited because they require long processing times, rely on complex and high-cost equipment, and may not reliably detect new/uncommon resistance mechanisms. Newer approaches such as MALDI-TOF MS (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry) offer rapid bacterial identification, but determining whether isolates are resistant or susceptible to ertapenem still requires additional time-consuming manual processes. In this study, we integrated several machine learning (ML) algorithms: Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and Random Forest, with MALDI-TOF MS spectra to predict ertapenem resistance with high accuracy. The results were promising: Logistic Regression, MLP, and Random Forest achieved testing accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86 \%, 84 \%$</tex>, and 78 %. This approach has strong potential to improve the diagnostic capability of MALDI-TOF MS by enabling rapid and accurate detection of Klebsiella pneumoniae resistance and supporting timely, targeted decision-making. Unlike previous research, which has been more generic in scope, this study is specifically focused on ertapenem (an easy-to-administer, cost-effective carbapenem) and employs lightweight ML models that can run on standard laboratory computers, making the method more suitable for real-world clinical applications.

title: Multimodal Dual-Level Recognition of Devanagari Handwritten Documents with CNN-BiLSTM Transformer and StrokeGraph-A-BLSTM
authors: Saravana M K, Chaitra B V, Akash Singh J, Devendra C, Saiyam Kumar Biswas, Yuvraj Kaushik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Handwritten document recognition for Indic scripts such as Devanagari remains a challenging problem due to complex ligatures, shirorekha, noisy pages, and writer-specific variability. This work proposes a multimodal dual-level recognition framework that jointly addresses sentence-level and page-level handwritten Hindi recognition. At the sentence level, a CNN-BiLSTM-Transformer architecture captures both local visual features and long-range contextual dependencies. At the page level, a StrokeGraph-A-BLSTM model represents pen trajectories as graphs and ensures writer-invariant training. A multi-modal fusion mechanism integrates visual and stroke features to improve recognition robustness. Experiments are conducted on the ICDAR 2024-HWD dataset and a Hindi handwritten sentence dataset. Our model achieves significant improvements over baselines, with reduced Character Error Rate (CER) of 2.89 % and Word Error Rate (WER) of 6.12 % at sentence level, and enhanced Page-level Character Recognition Rate (PCRR) of 79.45 % and Page-level Word Recognition Rate (PWRR) of 67.83 % at page level. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining graph-based stroke representations with deep visual encoders for Devanagari handwritten document recognition.

title: 5G NR Network Planning at 700 MHz Frequency for V2I Service (Case Study: Ahmad Yani Road - Tunjungan Road, Surabaya City)
authors: Muhammad Zulfiqri Firdiansyah, Tri Agus Djoko Kuntjoro, Arrizky Ayu Faradila Purnama, Kevin Aditya Pratama
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Development of intelligent transportation in Surabaya requires the support of a 5G NR network with wide coverage for Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) services. This research plans and analyzes a 5G NR network using the 700 MHz frequency with 40 MHz bandwidth in the Ahmad Yani Road to Tunjungan Road corridor, Surabaya. Unlike higher frequency bands that suffer from high path loss, the 700 MHz band is selected to ensure superior Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) coverage in this dense urban environment. The network design was simulated using Atoll software with the Urban Macro (UMa) propagation model. Based on the simulation results, the coverage analysis shows an average SS-RSRP value of -66.15 dBm, indicating strong signal strength. The average SS-SINR value is 11.28 dB, which is sufficient to support robust QPSK to 16-QAM modulation schemes. These results indicate that the 700 MHz frequency provides reliable connectivity for essential V2I safety messaging along the study route.

title: Harnessing LSTM for Climate-Sensitive Precision Agriculture: Predicting Rice Blast Disease Expansion
authors: Maharani Citra Adi Ratna, Mochamad Nizar Palefi Ma’Ady, Berlian Rahmy Lidiawaty
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Rice blast disease (Pyricularia oryzae) is a major threat to rice production, particularly under increasing climate variability. Lamongan Regency, a top rice-producing region in East Java, frequently experiences high humidity, excessive rainfall, and temperature fluctuations—factors that intensify disease outbreaks. This study proposes a predictive model using Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks to forecast disease outbreaks based on monthly climate and infection data from 2019 to 2024. Input features include rainfall, temperature, humidity, and infection reports from five subregional clusters. Data were normalized and smoothed prior to modeling to enhance time-series stability. Model performance was evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), with the central and eastern clusters achieving the highest accuracy (MAPE <17%). Additionally, the model successfully predicted outbreak risks for January–April 2025, aligning with known seasonal trends. These results demonstrate the feasibility of LSTM for climate-based disease prediction and its potential to support early warning systems and timely interventions, contributing to sustainable rice farming in climate-sensitive areas like Lamongan.

title: Machine Learning-Based Heart Disease Detection Using KNN and Random Forest Classifiers
authors: Sapna Singh Khsatri, Tejaswani Gupta, Sakshi Dubey
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) remain the major cause of death in all parts of the world; hence, there is a need to ensure timely and effective diagnosis is done. The current study suggests a machine-learning platform to predict cardiac pathology by training two supervised learning algorithms, i.e., K -nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Random Forest classifiers. A systematic heart-disease data set, which included the health parameters of the patients age, serum cholesterol levels, the systolic blood pressure at rest, and the maximum heart rate, were used to identify the trends that are associated with cardiovascular risk. To ensure an objective assessment of the model, the dataset was normalized and then divided into two subsets namely training and test. The standard predictive measures of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 -score were used to measure model performance. In 304 cases, the results of the experiment demonstrated that the KNN classifier performed better than the counterpart, with the accuracy of 91.8, whereas the Random Forest model achieved the accuracy of 86.9. These findings indicate that despite the readability and strength of the Random Forest to pinpoint noteworthy predictive variables, KNN is more effective when utilized on well-distributed clinical data. The perspectives of a comparative analysis highlight the promising quality of machine-learning methods as dependable supplements to clinical diagnostics, the reduction of practitioner error, and data-driven medical decision-making. Conclusively, the development of such predictive models into healthcare systems has the potential to significantly increase timely prevention of cardiac diseases and the development of proactive treatment of patients.

title: Seismometers Fault Identification based on Time-series Ambient Noise Analysis using CatBoost Classification
authors: Ibnu Muhammad Amir, Titik Lestari, Adhi Harmoko Saputro
publish: 2025 2nd Beyond Technology Summit on Informatics International Conference (BTS-I2C)
abstract: Ensuring seismograph reliability is crucial for generating high-quality earthquake parameters. However, early detection of instrument degradation remains a challenge in large seismic networks. This study introduces an automated fault diagnostic that leverages statistical time series features and machine learning classification to identify seismometer malfunctions using background noise recordings from the 2020–2025 Indonesian Tsunami Early Warning System (Ina-TEWS) network. Seismic recordings were preprocessed, segmented into hourly windows, and converted into representative statistical metrics, including availability, Root Mean Square (RMS), median, mode, number of amplitudes, slope, and unique values. These features are labeled according to the seismometer's health status, including normal status and various types of instrument malfunctions. The resulting dataset is then used to train and evaluate a classification algorithm. We propose Categorical Boosting (CatBoost) as the primary classifier, with its performance benchmarked against Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Decision Tree (DT), Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), and Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM). The boosting model consistently produces near-perfect confusion matrices with strong generalization validated through learning curve analysis. CatBoost achieves perfect accuracy (1.00) for normal-abnormal classification and 0.999 accuracy for multi-fault detection. These results confirm that simple statistical features can effectively convert seismometer fault characteristic patterns and that the CatBoost machine learning model is a reliable and scalable solution for automated seismometer fault identification. This framework provides a practical path towards predictive maintenance and data quality improvement for real-time earthquake monitoring.

title: Comparative Evaluation of YOLOv12 and SAHI for Medication Identification in Hospital Pharmacies
authors: Bharadwaj Malepati, Suman Nandamury, Usha Manjunath, David Rajan, Akash Gajanan Prabhune
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: Medication identification errors in hospital pharmacies pose a critical threat to patient safety and operational efficiency. Conventional verification systems, such as barcodes and RFID tags, though widely implemented, often face limitations due to label wear, scanning dependency, and infrastructure cost. This study investigates a computer vision-based approach using deep learning for automated identification of medications. We evaluated two object detection configurations: (1) a baseline YOLOv12 (You Only Look Once) model and (2) YOLOv12 integrated with Sliced Aided Hyper Inference (SAHI) for improved small-object recognition. A custom dataset comprising 837 annotated pharmacy images across different packaging types-ampoules, blister packs, strip foils, mono-carton boxes, syrup bottles, powder jars, etc., was developed and labelled by pharmacists using Label Studio. Beyond overall improvement, targeted evaluation on four OCR-critical label regions (Batch No., MRP, Manufacturing Date, and Expiry) showed up to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17 \times$</tex> increase in AP@50, confirming SAHI's strong advantage for finetext localisation crucial to medication verification. Complementary YOLOv8 classification models trained for brand, packaging, and view type achieved Top-1 accuracies of 84.0%, 93.3%, and 72.4%, respectively, with corresponding Top-5 accuracies exceeding 96%, confirming strong generalisation across packaging variations. These results demonstrate the potential of combining attention-centric detection and sliced inference for real-time, camera-based pharmacy automation reducing manual verification effort and enhancing medication safety.

title: CPFE-Focus: Contextual Prior and Facial Stream-Assisted Focus Enhancer and 3D Gaussian Transition
authors: Aman Kumar, Chirag Bhatia, Vivek Kumar, Sangharsh Thakur
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Metaverse, and Cybersecurity (ICAMAC)
abstract: In modern video capture systems, effective focus management is critical for enhancing user experience and visual clarity. This research introduces CPFE-Focus, a novel approach to dynamic face prioritization in video mode based on individual session time—the cumulative duration a face remains visible within the video frame. Unlike traditional autofocus systems that primarily rely on depth information or facial recognition to determine the focal subjects without temporal context, our method incorporates real-time facial tracking and cumulative session analysis to identify and prioritize faces that appear consistently or for longer durations. This temporal prioritization addresses key limitations in existing autofocus systems, particularly in multi-subject or continuously shifting environments. Utilizing computer vision algorithms and temporal data aggregation, the system dynamically adjusts camera focus to favour high-priority subjects. Experimental results demonstrate improved focus stability and subject relevance in multi-person scenes, especially in contexts such as video conferencing, surveillance, and event recording. This research highlights the potential of temporal presence as a critical factor in intelligent video focusing systems, paving the way for more adaptive and context-aware camera technologies. Thus, improving the camera's ability to identify and retain attention on high-priority subjects.

title: Efficient Hardware Implementation of Eigen Solver
authors: Uday Korat, Amirhossein Alimohammad
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Eigen analysis is a fundamental module in many real time signal processing algorithms. This article presents the hardware architecture design and implemen-tation of a vector based high-throughput parameterizable eigen solver. The proposed design calculates an eigen values and vectors based on the QR algorithm. The QR algorithm works really well for small dense matrices and it works as additive method in projection for large matrix eigen analysis. This paper presents parallel vector based computational units for eigen solver using basic QR algorithm. The QR decomposition is implemented based on the Givens-CORDIC algorithm and the matrix multiplication of matrices <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{Q}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}$</tex> is based on parallel vector products. The Eigen Solver is synthesized on Xilinx Virtex-7 for a real-valued matrices of size <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4\times 4,8\times 8$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16\times 16$</tex>.

title: Extending Delta Debugging Minimization for Spectrum-Based Fault Localization
authors: Charaka Geethal Kapugama
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: This paper introduces DDMIN-LOC, a technique that combines Delta Debugging Minimization (DDMIN) with Spectrum-Based Fault Localization (SBFL). It can be applied to programs taking string inputs, even when only a single failure-inducing input is available. DDMIN is an algorithm that systematically explores the minimal failure-inducing input that exposes a bug, given an initial failing input. However, it does not provide information about the faulty statements responsible for the failure. DDMIN-LOC addresses this limitation by collecting the passing and failing inputs generated during the DDMIN process and computing suspiciousness scores for program statements and predicates using SBFL algorithms. These scores are then combined to rank statements according to their likelihood of being faulty. DDMIN-LOC requires only one failing input of the buggy program, although it can be applied only to programs that take string inputs. DDMIN-LOC was evaluated on 136 programs selected from the QuixBugs and Codeflaws benchmarks using the SBFL algorithms Tarantula, Ochiai, GenProg, Jaccard and DStar. Experimental results show that DDMIN-LOC performs best with Jaccard: in most subjects, fewer than 20% executable lines need to be examined to locate the faulty statements. Moreover, in most subjects, faulty statements are ranked within the top 3 positions in all the generated test suites derived from different failing inputs.

title: Optimal Design Method of 2-terminal Surge Protection Parameter for Control Power Supply System in Energy Storage System
authors: Hyun-Sang You, Yun-Ho Kim, Min-Haeng Lee, Se-Jin Kim, Hyeong-An Jang, Dae-Seok Rho
publish: 2025 International Conference on Electrical and Computer Engineering Researches (ICECER)
abstract: This paper deals with an optimal operation and design method of a 2-terminal surge protection parameter to protect control power supply system in ESS against the LEMP(lightning electromagnetic pulse). And also, this paper implements the protection coordination device with 2-terminal surge protection parameter considering configuration of control power supply system in ESS. Where, the 2-terminal surge protection parameter for control power supply system in ESS is composed of UPS, PDU and LEMP protection device which combines surge protection parameter and DS connected in parallel with MCB and NCT(noise cut transformer) in series. Based on the test results for combination and impulse, it is found that the proposed design method can keep residual voltages within the surge withstanding voltage and then it is useful to perform surge protection of the control power supply system in ESS.

title: DATURA: A Deep Learning-based Adaptive Traffic-aware Unified Resource Autoscaling Framework for VNFs in 5G/B5G Networks
authors: Kanchan K. Tiwari, Krishna M. Sivalingam
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: This paper introduces DATURA, a Deep Learning(DL)–based Adaptive Traffic-aware Unified Resource Autoscaling framework for virtual network functions (VNFs) in 5G and beyond networks. A predictive GRU–MLP based scaling architecture is proposed and integrated with a deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based traffic generator. The GRU–MLP scaling controller forecasts per-VNF and per-slice latencies from streaming telemetry. Temporal dependencies are captured through the GRU layer, while two multitask MLP heads estimate local (per-VNF) and global (slice-level) latency trends. Once deployed, DATURA operates fully online, executing inference-driven scaling decisions in real time. The DRL traffic generator employs Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO) agents trained to learn transition probability matrices (TPM) that capture realistic transitions between low, medium, high, and very-high-load regimes. The underlying models are periodically retrained with updated telemetry, enabling the framework to adapt to long-term traffic evolution without service disruption. The predicted latencies are then translated by a regression layer into proactive scaling decisions and resource allocations that maintain SLA compliance while avoiding unnecessary over-provisioning. The framework is implemented in a SimPy-based 5G Core model that supports UE registration and PDU session establishment message exchanges. Detailed experiments show that DATURA consistently outperforms other existing schemes based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and GRU. The proposed framework achieves up to 73% latency reduction, 55% throughput gain, and 47% higher provisioning efficiency.

title: Power-efficient Triple-cladding Four-core Fiber Amplifier with Advanced Hybrid Pumping Technique
authors: Taiji Sakamoto, Masaki Wada, Ryota Imada, Takashi Matui, Kazuhide Nakajima
abstract: We propose an advanced hybrid pumping (AHP) technique for multi-core fiber (MCF) amplifiers to improve power efficiency. Unlike conventional hybrid pumping that requires multiple single-mode pump lasers, AHP enables simultaneous core and cladding pumping using a single multimode pump laser. Numerical simulations and experiments confirm that AHP enhances both power conversion efficiency (PCE) and noise figure (NF) by increasing pump density in the cores. Using a triple-cladding four-core erbium-doped fiber, we achieved a PCE of 17.6% and an output signal power exceeding 400 mW per core. These results demonstrate the potential of AHP for energy-efficient MCF optical transmission systems.

title: Deployment Prior Injection for Run-time Re-biasable Object Detection
authors: Mo Zhou, Yiding Yang, Haoxiang Li, Vishal M. Patel, Gang Hua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: With a strong alignment between the training and test distributions, object relation as a context prior facilitates object detection. Yet, it turns into a harmful but inevitable training set bias upon test distributions that shift differently across space and time. Nevertheless, the existing detectors cannot incorporate deployment context prior during the test phase without parameter update. Such kind of capability requires the model to explicitly learn disentangled representations with respect to context prior. To achieve this, we introduce an additional graph input to the detector, where the graph represents the deployment context prior, and its edge values represent object relations. Then, the detector behavior is trained to bound to the graph with a modified training objective. As a result, during the test phase, any suitable deployment context prior can be injected into the detector via graph edits, hence “re-biasing” the detector towards the given prior at run-time without parameter update. Even if the deployment prior is unknown, the detector can self-rebias using deployment prior approximated using its own predictions. Comprehensive experimental results on the COCO dataset, as well as cross-dataset testing on the Objects365 dataset, demonstrate the effectiveness of the run-time re-biasable detector.

title: Representing Egocentric Directions With Torso-Applied Vibrotactile Stimuli_supp1-3667214.pdf
authors: Seungmoon Choi
abstract: This paper investigates egocentric directional perception of azimuth and elevation in response to torso-based vibrotactile stimuli for both stimulus identification and direction association under a common experimental framework. We conducted four perceptual experiments that examined the two tasks for azimuth and elevation, using real vibrations and illusory stimuli generated by the funneling illusion. The results demonstrated that adding illusory stimuli effectively conveyed directional information with fewer tactors than using only real stimuli. Azimuth perception revealed a lateral bias, whereas elevation perception exhibited a downward bias on the dorsal torso, particularly in the upper back. However, both azimuth and elevation cues were generally consistent across vertical and horizontal torso locations. Additionally, we estimated regression models for both egocentric angles and showed that perceived directions could be estimated from actual stimulus positions. Correlation analysis revealed a weak relationship between azimuth and elevation perceptual errors, suggesting that these dimensions are processed with near-independence. Across all findings, azimuth cues proved to be more effective than elevation cues in conveying directional information. This study provides a comprehensive understanding of egocentric directions and offers practical insights for the design of torso-based vibrotactile displays.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A

title: A Neural Codec for Bone-Conducted Speech with Joint Denoising and Bandwidth Extension
authors: Xiaolong Hu, Zhe Chen, Fuliang Yin
abstract: In Internet of Things (IoT) communication scenarios such as wearable and industrial systems operating in extremely noisy environments, Bone-Conducted Microphone (BCM) speech inevitably suffers from noise interference. To address this issue, a neural codec for bone-conducted speech with joint denoising and bandwidth extension is proposed in this paper. Speciffcally, an encoder–decoder architecture composed of the MP-SENet and the BigVGAN vocoder is built as the backbone. On this basis, a Forward Time-Frequency Transformer (FTM) is introduced to effectively capture both local and global time-frequency dependencies in the speech signal. To improve the capacity of speech signal modeling, a Backward Time-Frequency Transformer (BTM) is presented to model the information flow in reverse, complementing the forward modeling and enabling more comprehensive extraction of time-frequency features. Subsequently, KalmanNet is designed to enhance the ability to capture temporal dependencies and dynamic information, compensating for the transformer’s limited effectiveness in modeling continuous dynamics. Finally, a Dual-Path Magnitude-Phase (DPMP) module is proposed to feature an interactive dual-stream architecture where magnitude and phase streams communicate with each other to supplement high-frequency components of the speech signal. The proposed method enables simultaneous speech encoding and enhancement under different SNRs using a single network. Furthermore, it supports coding at different bitrates without requiring modifications to the network architecture or retraining. Simulation experiments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed approach, highlighting its potential for robust and efficient speech transmission in IoT-enabled communication.

title: Strategic Shaping of Human Prosociality: A Latent-State POMDP Framework
authors: Zahra Zahedi, Xinyue Hu, Shashank Mehrotra, Mark Steyvers, Kumar Akash
abstract: We propose a decision-theoretic framework in which a robot strategically can shape inferred human's prosocial state during repeated interactions. Modeling the human's prosociality as a latent state that evolves over time, the robot learns to infer and influence this state through its own actions, including helping and signaling. We formalize this as a latent-state POMDP with limited observations and learn the transition and observation dynamics using expectation maximization. The resulting belief-based policy balances task and social objectives, selecting actions that maximize long-term cooperative outcomes. We evaluate the model using data from user studies and show that the learned policy outperforms baseline strategies in both team performance and increasing observed human cooperative behavior.

title: A Multi-Scale Context Fusion Network With Boundary Refinement for Road Marking Segmentation
authors: Chen Chen, Yandang Jia, Lili Yu, Tao Liu
abstract: Accurate segmentation of road markings, particularly symbol-type patterns, is vital for robust perception in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and autonomous driving. These markings provide essential visual cues for vehicle localization, navigation, and traffic regulation. However, the complex geometries and diverse visual appearances of symbol markings pose persistent challenges to current deep learning models, which often emphasize lane-line extraction while neglecting fine-grained boundary precision. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a multi-scale context fusion network with edge refinement designed for precise road marking segmentation. The network incorporates a Multi-Scale Semantic Enhancement Module (MSEM) to reinforce semantic coherence across scales and a PointRend inspired refinement head (PR-Head) to adaptively optimize boundary representations in uncertain regions. A focal loss function (FLF) is further introduced to alleviate the imbalance between foreground and background, leading to more stable and accurate predictions. Extensive experiments on the CeyMo dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a mIoU of 72.6%, surpassing the baseline by 3.4% while maintaining real-time inference at 118.9 FPS. These results confirm that the network effectively enhances multi-scale feature representation and boundary precision with minimal computational cost, providing a practical and efficient solution for fine-grained road perception in complex traffic environments.

title: Adaptive Semantic Integrity and Privacy Shielding in Agentic AI Networks for Trustworthy 6G Cognitive Communication System
authors: M. Senbagavalli, Saswati Debnath, M. Vaneeta, Thamodharan Arumugam, B. M. Beena, Sunil Prajapat
abstract: The 6G cognitive communication systems are evolving very fast and present novel autonomy, semantic awareness, and agent-driven decision intelligence. Since agentic AI systems dynamically communicate through sharing contextual knowledge to make optimal use of the communication resources, it allows the capabilities of self-learning, the integrity, privacy, and trust become a pressing issue. Traditional cryptographic and authentication systems are incapable of securing the changing semantic data structures and dispersed collaborative AI activities. The new 6G cognitive system is susceptible to semantic manipulations, adversarial reasoning, identity fraud, and privacy intrusion as a result of distributed reasoning, cross-agent cooperation, and real-time training. Such threats undermine reliability, transparency, and trust of the users. This paper proposes AI-SHIELD (Adaptive Intelligence Shield for Hierarchical Explainable Learning and Distributed Security), a new multilayer security model, that incorporates self-adaptive semantic validation, embedding of differentially private, cross-agent trust consensus, and explainable decision auditing. The AI-SHIELD is an active knowledge exchange assessor based on a hybrid semantic fingerprinting model and implements agent negotiation based on federated reinforcement learning at the cost of privacy. The results of simulation show decreased inference attacks by 41%, enhanced semantic consistency by 37%, and better metrics of explainability without compromising the communication latency. AI-SHIELD strengthens trust, resilience, and transparency of agent-based 6G cognitive networks to facilitate autonomous communications at large scale securely.

title: Risk-Aware Privacy Preservation for LLM Inference_supp1-3667458.pdf
authors: Zhiping Cai
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) inference services like ChatGPT are popular for enabling diverse tasks via prompts, yet they exacerbate privacy risks due to the potential exposure of sensitive data in user inputs. Existing local differential privacy (LDP)-based text sanitization mechanisms offer lightweight protection suitable for cloud-based LLM inference. Nevertheless, uniform privacy budget allocation and generalized sanitization mechanisms neglect the critical protection needs of sensitive user data, such as Personally Identifiable Information (PII). Empirical evidence of this work reveals that even with a strict privacy budget (ϵ=0.1), the sensitive information leakage rate can reach an alarmingly high 71.74%. To address these challenges, this paper proposes Rap-LI, a risk-aware privacy preservation framework for LLM inference, designed to be plug-and-play. Rap-LI performs risk identification and personalized labeling on user prompts, then develops a risk-aware LDP mechanism for text sanitization, formally proven to satisfy both token-level and sentence-level LDP guarantees. Extensive experimental results demonstrate Rap-LI’s superior privacy-utility balance. It improves privacy protection against sensitive information leakage by an average of 51.68% compared to methods with comparable utility. Our code is available at https://github.com/Cristliu/RapLI.

title: AI-Driven Telemedicine Platforms: Strategic Adoption Models for Enhancing Healthcare Accessibility in Emerging Economies
authors: Yogesh Mehta, Durgesh Nandan
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: The very fast adoption of the artificial intelligence into the sphere of the telemedicine opens immense possibilities to enhance the access of the healthcare in the new economies. This study examines the factors of AI-based telemedicine adoption based on the Technology-Organization-Environment framework and evaluates the contribution of change management preparedness. Statistical modeling was used to examine the impact of different factors on adoption with results of a cross-sectional survey of 300 stakeholders, among which are healthcare providers, patients, and policy/technology managers in India. Organizational and environmental factors prove to be important predictors of adoption whereas technological factors are not. Change management preparedness has a favourable impact on adoption yet it does not mediate the technology-organization and environment relationships. Out of these results, it is apparent that the dedication of the leadership, conducive policies, and effective infrastructure counts more than the technological savvy. The study being reviewed can advance the research regarding digital health adoption by integrating change management knowledge and offers empirical advice to different extents of improving organizational and policy capabilities to scale AI-enabled telemedicine.

title: Deep Learning for Breast Cancer Segmentation: A Comprehensive Analysis
authors: Ruxana Jabeen, Abdul Wahid
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence for Innovations in Healthcare Industries (ICAIIHI)
abstract: Breast cancer is one of the primarily detected cancers that affects women throughout the worldwide, & finding it early is still very important for lowering the death rate. Medical image segmentation is very important for diagnosing breast cancer since it helps find and outline tumours accurately. Deep learning has made segmentation much better than it used to be with previous methods. This work provides a thorough examination of deep learning methodologies for breast cancer segmentation through many imaging modalities, as well as mammography, ultrasound, and MRI. We examine significant topologies, for example, U-Net and its variants, attention-based models, and transformer-driven networks, while also emphasising benchmark datasets and assessment criteria. The results show that deep learning always improves the accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of segmentation. But there are still problems with generalisation, not enough annotated data, and understanding the results. Future research avenues encompass lightweight architectures for clinical implementation, multimodal integration, and explainable AI to enhance confidence and adoption within real-world healthcare systems.

title: Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Intelligent Customer Service in Electricity Utility Sector : A Case Study of MEA's DTD Doc Insight System
authors: Panisit Fuangfung
publish: 2025 25th Conference of the Electric Power Supply Industry (CEPSI)
abstract: The "DTD Doc Insight" system, developed by the Metropolitan Electricity Authority's Digital Technology Development Department, shows how artificial intelligence (AI) can improve operations and service quality in electricity companies. This system helps employees quickly find information in customer service manuals and frequently asked questions. It uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology to give accurate answers from internal documents. Our findings show that good data preparation and system setup are crucial for accuracy. If documents are not prepared correctly, the system's answers are less accurate. After optimization, the system achieved 79% accuracy, with a precision of 0.83 (correctness of answers) and a recall of 0.77 (coverage of questions). The F1-score was 0.80. Also, response time dropped from five minutes to just 15 seconds. This work highlights the potential of AI to solve challenges in utility organizations.

title: Hybrid EEG-Deep Learning Framework for Objective Alcohol Use Disorder Diagnosis
authors: R. Kishore Kanna, Alok Singh Chauhan, Palak Gupta, Narendra Kumar Sharma, Daya Shankar Srivastava, Bhoopendra Dwivedi
publish: 2025 5th International Conference on Internet of Things: Smart Innovation and Usages (IoT-SIU)
abstract: Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) should be approached as a global health concern, stemming from the underlying need for rapid and accurate diagnosis for customized care and evidence-based treatment. Standard practices rely on clinicians’ incomplete subjective reporting of patients’ health issues. As a cost effective and non-invasive technique, started to put forward the important voltage changes that occur in the brain throughout the duration of Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) while the patients do the activities. Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) alone can challenge clinicians to make the diagnosis. Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) is a dark lit room with plenty noise. Classification of the EEG brain signals aims to uncover the contents. There is a considerable challenge for computers to design a method to classify the signals for patients with Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) in a room that can be loud and dark. Classification of neural signals in a dark and noise free room can be a problem for a computer and the design of a computer should be for the worst-case scenario. This Empirical Neuro-Algorithms Workshop with Classification Theoreticians provides this for detection of complex signals in dense elect Tela structures. The computer and the design of the computer should close to that of a brain for the computer to working properly. here is a challenge for it working properly and this workshop is the Empirical Neuro-Algorithms Workshop with Classification Theoreticians. The computer and the design of the computer should close to that of a brain for the computer to working properly. There should be a challenge for it working properly and this workshop is the Empirical Neuro-Algorithms Workshop with Classification Theoreticians. This study has the potential to enhance AUD diagnosis by providing physicians with a more objective, scalable, and consistent tool, hence enhancing patient treatment in various clinical environments.

title: Methods, Obstacles, and Potential Use Cases of Using Natural Language Processing to Detect Cybercrimes
authors: Govind Yadav, Namita Vyas Joshi, Siddharth Shankar Mishra, Abha Shree Pandey, Sonia Tyagi
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: Phishing is still an ever-present crime that uses social engineering to obtain private data. To counter this., we propose an advanced phishing detection system that employs Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques., using transformer-based models such as BERT. The method includes data noise elimination., contextual embeddings feature extraction., and model fine-tuning tuned for improved detection performance. The evaluation metrics-accuracy., recall., F1 score., and AVC-indicate model efficiency., identifying potential phishing with low false positives and negatives. This static approach is their Achilles heel., confirming the powerful potential of AI in cybersecurity., with a nimble mechanism that creates a flexible., scalable system that can adapt to new attacks and more complex phishing schemes. In conclusion., our results highlight the promise of more ubiquitous usage of applied NLP in cybersecurity., leading to more robust cybersecurity systems.

title: Fake News Detector: A Multi-Model Machine Learning Web Application for News Authenticity Verification
authors: Venkatesh Sharma Vellala, Swathy M
publish: 2025 World Conference on Cutting-Edge Science and Technology (WCCEST)
abstract: The rampant dissemination of fake news poses a critical threat to public trust, informed decision-making, and social stability, particularly in the era of digital information. This paper presents a robust multi-model fake news detection system utilizing hybrid machine learning and natural language processing techniques. The architecture integrates TF-IDF-based text classification, transformer-derived semantic embeddings, tone analysis via zero-shot models such as BART, and real-time fact verification through APIs including NewsData.io and Google Fact Check Tools. Multilingual capability is ensured through automated language detection and translation, extending usability across India's diverse linguistic landscape. Experimental evaluation demonstrates high accuracy, with the proposed system achieving up to 94% accuracy, 93% precision, and 94% recall on benchmark datasets. with the proposed ensemble outperforming individual models and achieving reliable verdicts through score aggregation and external fact validation. The system's modular design, interpretability, and domain generalizability highlight its potential for real-world deployment in the ongoing battle against fake news.

title: Beyond the Pre-Service Horizon: Infusing In-Service Behavior for Improved Financial Risk Forecasting
authors: Senhao Liu, Zhiyu Guo, Zhiyuan Ji, Yueguo Chen, Yateng Tang, Yunhai Wang, Xuehao Zheng, Xiang Ao
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Typical financial risk management involves distinct phases for pre-service risk assessment and in-service default detection, often modeled separately. This paper proposes a novel framework, Multi-Granularity Knowledge Distillation (abbreviated as MGKD), aimed at improving pre-service risk prediction through the integration of in-service user behavior data. MGKD follows the idea of knowledge distillation, where the teacher model, trained on historical in-service data, guides the student model, which is trained on pre-service data. By using soft labels derived from in-service data, the teacher model helps the student model improve its risk prediction prior to service activation. Meanwhile, a multi-granularity distillation strategy is introduced, including coarse-grained, fine-grained, and self-distillation, to align the representations and predictions of the teacher and student models. This approach not only reinforces the representation of default cases but also enables the transfer of key behavioral patterns associated with defaulters from the teacher to the student model, thereby improving the overall performance of pre-service risk assessment. Moreover, we adopt a re-weighting strategy to mitigate the model's bias towards the minority class. Experimental results on large-scale real-world datasets from Tencent Mobile Payment demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach in both offline and online scenarios.

title: Feature Generation Machine for Learning with Label Proportions
authors: Jing Chai
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Learning with Label Proportions (LLP) copes with the problem that training instances are grouped as bags, where the class proportions in each bag rather than the labels of individual training instances are provided. Feature selection, which preserves only a subset of original features, is vital in LLP as it might impact the subsequent disambiguation and classification performances. Accordingly, a new work termed Feature Generation Machine for LLP (FGM-LLP), is proposed to tackle the feature selection, disambiguation and classification challenges simultaneously. In specific, FGM-LLP learns the instance-level labels, the feature selection vector and the maximum margin classifier under a unified framework. To solve FGM-LLP efficiently, a two-layer Cutting Plane (CP) method is also developed with a convergence guarantee. Empirical studies justify not only the superiority of FGM-LLP in classification compared with competing work, but also the high-efficiency of the two-layer CP optimization method.

title: Neural Autoregressive Flows for Markov Boundary Learning
authors: Khoa Nguyen, Bao Duong, Viet Huynh, Thin Nguyen
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Recovering Markov boundary-the minimal set of variables that maximizes predictive performance for a response variable-is crucial in many applications. While recent advances improve upon traditional constraint-based techniques by scoring local causal structures, they still rely on nonparametric estimators and heuristic searches, lacking theoretical guarantees for reliability. This paper investigates a framework for efficient Markov boundary discovery by integrating conditional entropy from information theory as a scoring criterion. We design a novel masked autoregressive network to capture complex dependencies. A parallelizable greedy search strategy in polynomial time is proposed, supported by analytical evidence. We also discuss how initializing a graph with learned Markov boundaries accelerates the convergence of causal discovery. Comprehensive evaluations on real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate the scalability and superior performance of our method in both Markov boundary discovery and causal discovery tasks.

title: Score-Based Integrated Gradient for Root Cause Explanations of Outliers
authors: Phuoc Nguyen, Truyen Tran, Sunil Gupta, Svetha Venkatesh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM)
abstract: Identifying the root causes of outliers is a fundamental problem in causal inference and anomaly detection. Traditional approaches based on heuristics or counterfactual reasoning often struggle under uncertainty and high-dimensional dependencies. We introduce SIREN, a novel and scalable method that attributes the root causes of outliers by estimating the score functions of the data likelihood. Attribution is computed via integrated gradients that accumulate score contributions along paths from the outlier toward the normal data distribution. Our method satisfies three of the four classic Shapley value axioms-dummy, efficiency, and linearity-as well as an asymmetry axiom derived from the underlying causal structure. Unlike prior work, SIREN operates directly on the score function, enabling tractable and uncertainty-aware root cause attribution in nonlinear, high-dimensional, and heteroscedastic causal models. Extensive experiments on synthetic random graphs and real-world cloud service and supply chain datasets show that SIREN outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both attribution accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: TAMO-FoA: Tool-Augmented AIOps for Reliable RCA in Cloud-Native Systems
authors: Akshay Mittal, Krishna Kandi, Rafiuddin Syed, Sagar Mahajan
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for Root Cause Analysis (RCA) in cloud-native systems but suffer from hallucinations, context constraints, and dynamic dependency misunderstandings. This paper presents TAMO-FoA (ToolAugmented Multimodal Operations Framework for AIOps), a comprehensive framework addressing these limitations through four key innovations: tool-augmented perception, SOP-guided reasoning, specialized hallucination detection, and multi-modal fusion. Our framework integrates a hybrid multi-modal encoder combining 1D-CNN, BERT+LSTM, and GNN architectures with diffusion-based fusion for temporal causality preservation across heterogeneous telemetry data. The SOP-enhanced ReAct agent incorporates Standard Operating Procedures to guide diagnostic workflows and reduce irrelevant tool calls by 42%. Our novel HDM-2 hallucination detector employs dual verification mechanisms achieving 21% relative improvement in F1 scores over existing approaches. Experimental evaluation on real-world microservice failures demonstrates 17.2% accuracy improvement and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.3 \times$</tex> token efficiency gains compared to baseline methods. A 3-month production deployment achieved 76.3% root cause identification accuracy with 34% reduction in mean time to resolution. TAMO-FoA provides a concrete path toward dependable AgentOps for self-healing cloud infrastructures, with complete implementation publicly available at https://github.com/akshaymittal143/TAMO-FoA.

title: Hybrid AI for Clinical Prediction: Combining LLM-Derived Features with ML for Explainable Diabetes Risk Assessment
authors: Nikhil Tamvada, Varun Akella, Pragati Dharmale, Mahesh Khadatare
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: The increasing global prevalence of diabetes requires the development of accurate and early predictive models. Traditional machine learning (ML) approaches often struggle with interpretability and the ability to leverage rich and unstructured clinical insights. This study introduces a novel hybrid AI framework that combines a traditional ML model (Bagging Classifier) with features derived from a large language model (LLM), specifically the Gemini model, for improved prediction of diabetes. We used LLM to generate high-level interpretable features (e.g. sentiment scores, keyword flags, and semantic embeddings) from patient profiles constructed using structured data, which were then integrated into a meta-learner model. Although a proprietary LLM was used in this study, the methodology was designed to be model-agnostic and reproducible using open-source software. Our framework was evaluated on a combined dataset of PIMA Indian Diabetes and CDC BRFSS2015 data, demonstrating superior performance, achieving a recall of 0.83 ± 0.009 (95% CI: [0.821, 0.839]) and an accuracy of 0.90 ± 0.019 (95% CI: [0.881, 0.919]) in unseen test data. Beyond performance, the hybrid model offers enhanced explain-ability through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values, revealing key feature contributions from both traditional and LLM derived sources. We also conducted a comprehensive fairness analysis to highlight the model's equitable performance across demographic groups.

title: Revisiting Data Anonymization: Limitations and Challenges in the ERA of Large Language Models
authors: Sandeep Kalari, Sahithi Padidela, Vikas Ashok, Ravi Mukkamala
publish: 2026 IEEE 16th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC)
abstract: Traditional data anonymization techniques-including generalization, suppression, perturbation, and masking-were designed for an era of limited contextual modeling. These methods effectively balanced privacy protection and data utility for structured, textual, audio, and visual data. However, the emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) and multimodal foundation architectures has fundamentally disrupted this balance. Modern models, equipped with powerful representation-learning and cross-modal reasoning, can infer or reconstruct masked identities using contextual, semantic, or visual cues, rendering traditional anonymization insufficient. This paper surveys anonymization techniques across tabular, textual, audio, and video domains, comparing their effectiveness before and after the advent of LLMs. Through controlled experiments, we demonstrate that context-aware LLMs can accurately re-identify subjects from anonymized datasets, even when explicit identifiers are removed. The results reveal that traditional anonymization fails to address contextual inference threats, calling for a paradigm shift toward holistic, multi-layered, privacy-preserving approaches. We discuss emerging strategies-such as differential privacy, federated learning, synthetic data generation, and LLM-in-the-loop anonymization-as potential solutions for privacy preservation in the LLM era.

title: Privacy-Aware AI, Health Report Interpretation, Large Language Models, Medical Summarization, Explainable AI
authors: Ann Mathews, Ammar Khan, Nikhil T Nainan, Moteesh A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: The rapid growth of electronic health records and diagnostic reports creates both an opportunity and a responsibility: we can make clinical information more accessible, but must also protect patient privacy. This paper presents a privacy-aware AI system that automatically detects and removes personally identifiable information before producing clinically relevant summaries and patient-facing recommendations using a large language model (LLM). Unlike many biomedical summarization approaches that primarily extract findings, our approach combines privacy-preserving preprocessing, structured clinical-value extraction, and user-friendly delivery. The system uses adaptive text processing to balance information retention with privacy protection and a structured extraction module to highlight key laboratory parameters and flag abnormal values. To broaden accessibility, the framework supports multilingual spoken explanations via a voice-enabled interface. A prototype demonstrates concise, comprehensible outputs while minimizing sensitive data exposure, illustrating the practicality of secure, explainable, and patient-centered healthcare AI. [1]–[3]

title: Enriching Security in Federated Learning and IoT - A Systematic Review
authors: Divya C, Serin V Simpson
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent and Innovative Technologies in Computing, Electrical and Electronics (IITCEE)
abstract: In this era of ubiquitous connectivity and smart systems, combining Federated Learning (FL) and the Internet of Things (IoT) symbolizes a revolutionary trend in edge-based intelligence. FL offers an exclusive decentralized feature for machine learning wherein the data resides on IoT devices improving privacy and also minimizes the huge data exchange. Integration of FL with IoT covers wide range of applications with the involvement of real time analytics. Still there exists obstacles from this integration like attacks on IoT devices.
